Since \( \angle EAB = \angle BAC + \angle CAD + \angle DAE = 3x \), we solve \( 3x + \frac{180 - x}{2} = 180 \), which simplifies to \( 6x + 180 - x = 360 \), giving \( 5x = 180 \) and \( x = 36 \).

steps.title: (b)(i) Financial Recurrence
steps.bodyLatex: Let \( r = 1 + \frac{0.042}{12} = 1.0035 \). After the first month, \( M_1 = Xr - 2500 \). After the second month, \( M_2 = M_1 r - 2500 = (Xr - 2500)r - 2500 = Xr^2 - 2500r - 2500 \), which factors to \( M_2 = X(r^2 + r) - 2500(r + 1) \). Substituting \( r = 1.0035 \) gives the required expression.

steps.title: (b)(ii) Solving for X
steps.bodyLatex: After \( 48 \) months, \( M_{48} = X(r^{48} + \dots + r) - 2500(r^{47} + \dots + 1) = 80000 \). Using the geometric series sum formula, \( X \cdot r \frac{r^{48}-1}{r-1} - 2500 \frac{r^{48}-1}{r-1} = 80000 \). Substituting \( r = 1.0035 \), we get \( X(52.351) - 2500(52.168) = 80000 \), leading to \( X = 4019.42 \).

steps.title: (c)(ii) Comparing Velocities
steps.bodyLatex: Set \( v_1 = v_2 \): \( 3t^2 + 4 - e^{-t} = 6t + 1 - e^{-t} \). This simplifies to \( 3t^2 - 6t + 3 = 0 \), or \( 3(t-1)^2 = 0 \). Thus, the particles have the same velocity at \( t = 1 \).

steps.title: (c)(iii) Proving Particles Do Not Meet
steps.bodyLatex: Integrate \( v_1 \) and \( v_2 \) to find positions \( x_1 = t^3 + 4t + e^{-t} - 1 \) and \( x_2 = 3t^2 + t + e^{-t} - 1 \). Setting \( x_1 = x_2 \) leads to \( t^3 - 3t^2 + 3t = 0 \), which factors as \( t(t^2 - 3t + 3) = 0 \). The quadratic \( t^2 - 3t + 3 \) has a discriminant \( \Delta = 9 - 12 = -3 < 0 \), so no real solutions exist for \( t > 0 \).

commonMistakesLatex: In geometry, incorrectly naming angles or failing to provide geometric reasons for parallel lines.
In finance, errors in the number of terms or the index of the geometric series.
In calculus, forgetting the constant of integration when finding velocity or position.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply calculus to find a minimum distance in a geometric context, solve for parameters in a limiting geometric series, and construct geometric proofs involving parallel lines and triangle properties.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), we use Pythagoras' theorem to define distance and differentiate to find the minimum. For (b), we use the limiting sum formula \(S_\infty = a/(1-r)\) and the condition \(|r| < 1\). For (c), we use properties of similar triangles and parallel lines to establish equality of sides and angles.

steps.title: (a)(i) Distance formula
steps.bodyLatex: Using Pythagoras' theorem on the right-angled triangles \(ACE\) and \(BDE\), we have \(AE = \sqrt{x^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{x^2 + 25}\) and \(BE = \sqrt{(9-x)^2 + 7^2} = \sqrt{49 + (9-x)^2}\). The total distance is the sum of these, so \(L = \sqrt{x^2 + 25} + \sqrt{49 + (9-x)^2}\).

steps.title: (a)(ii) Differentiate
steps.bodyLatex: Differentiating \(L\) with respect to \(x\) using the chain rule: \(\frac{dL}{dx} = \frac{1}{2}(x^2+25)^{-1/2}(2x) + \frac{1}{2}(49+(9-x)^2)^{-1/2}(2(9-x)(-1))\). Simplifying, we get \(\frac{dL}{dx} = \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+25}} - \frac{9-x}{\sqrt{49+(9-x)^2}}\). Setting \(\frac{dL}{dx} = 0\) gives \(\frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+25}} = \frac{9-x}{\sqrt{49+(9-x)^2}}\), which corresponds to \(\sin \alpha = \sin \beta\) since \(\sin \alpha = \frac{x}{AE}\) and \(\sin \beta = \frac{9-x}{BE}\).

steps.title: (a)(iii) Solve for x
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(\sin \alpha = \sin \beta\) and both are acute angles, \(\alpha = \beta\). This implies \(\triangle ACE\) is similar to \(\triangle BDE\). Therefore, the ratio of corresponding sides is equal: \(\frac{x}{5} = \frac{9-x}{7}\). Cross-multiplying gives \(7x = 45 - 5x\), so \(12x = 45\), which simplifies to \(x = \frac{15}{4} = 3.75\).

steps.title: (a)(iv) Verify minimum
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(\frac{dL}{dx} = 0\) only at \(x = 3.75\) within the interval \((0, 9)\), we check the endpoints. At \(x=0\), \(\frac{dL}{dx} < 0\), and at \(x=9\), \(\frac{dL}{dx} > 0\). Since the derivative changes from negative to positive at \(x = 3.75\), it is a local minimum.

steps.title: (b) Limiting sum
steps.bodyLatex: The limiting sum is \(S_\infty = \frac{a}{1-r} = 2\), so \(a = 2(1-r)\), which means \(r = 1 - \frac{a}{2}\). For a limiting sum to exist, we require \(|r| < 1\), so \(-1 < 1 - \frac{a}{2} < 1\). Subtracting 1 gives \(-2 < -\frac{a}{2} < 0\), and multiplying by \(-2\) (reversing the inequalities) gives \(0 < a < 4\).

steps.title: (c)(i) Isosceles proof
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(M\) is the midpoint of \(BC\) and \(MD \parallel CE\), by the intercept theorem, \(D\) must be the midpoint of \(BE\), so \(BD = DE\). We are given \(BD = DA + AC\). Substituting \(DE = DA + AE\), we get \(DA + AE = DA + AC\), which implies \(AE = AC\). Thus, \(\triangle ACE\) is isosceles.

steps.title: (c)(ii) Angle bisector
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(AF \parallel DM\) and \(DM \parallel CE\), we have \(AF \parallel CE\). Thus, \(\angle BAF = \angle AEC\) (corresponding angles) and \(\angle CAF = \angle ACE\) (alternate angles). Because \(\triangle ACE\) is isosceles, \(\angle AEC = \angle ACE\). Therefore, \(\angle BAF = \angle CAF\), proving \(AF\) bisects \(\angle BAC\).

commonMistakesLatex: In (a)(ii), failing to use the chain rule correctly when differentiating the square root terms.
In (b), incorrectly assuming \(|r| \leq 1\) instead of \(|r| < 1\), or failing to solve the inequality for \(a\).
In (c), struggling to link the parallel lines to the intercept theorem or corresponding/alternate angle properties.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a), check \(x=3.75\) in the derivative: \(\frac{3.75}{\sqrt{3.75^2+25}} \approx 0.6\) and \(\frac{5.25}{\sqrt{5.25^2+49}} \approx 0.6\). For (b), if \(a=2\), \(r=0\), sum is \(2/(1-0)=2\), which is valid.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to solve linear absolute value inequalities.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To solve an inequality of the form \(|f(x)| < a\), we rewrite it as the compound inequality \(-a < f(x) < a\). This allows us to solve for \(x\) by isolating it in the middle.

steps.title: Set up the compound inequality
steps.bodyLatex: Given \(|2x + 3| < 5\), we can rewrite this as: \[ -5 < 2x + 3 < 5 \]

steps.title: Isolate the term with x
steps.bodyLatex: Subtract 3 from all three parts of the inequality: \[ -5 - 3 < 2x < 5 - 3 \] \[ -8 < 2x < 2 \]

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.bodyLatex: Divide all parts by 2: \[ \frac{-8}{2} < x < \frac{2}{2} \] \[ -4 < x < 1 \]

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to apply the negative bound (e.g., solving \(2x + 3 < 5\) only).
Incorrectly flipping the inequality signs when dividing by a negative number (though not applicable here, it is a common trap).
Confusing the 'less than' case (which results in a single interval) with the 'greater than' case (which results in two separate intervals).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Test a value within the range, such as \(x = 0\). Substituting into the original inequality gives \(|2(0) + 3| = |3| = 3\), and \(3 < 5\) is true. Testing \(x = -5\) (outside the range) gives \(|2(-5) + 3| = |-7| = 7\), and \(7 < 5\) is false.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply the vector projection formula to find the projection of one vector onto another in a 2D plane.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the provided formula for the projection of \(\underset{\sim}{u}\) onto \(\underset{\sim}{v}\). We first calculate the dot product \(\underset{\sim}{u} \cdot \underset{\sim}{v}\) and the squared magnitude \(|\underset{\sim}{v}|^2\), then substitute these values into the formula.

steps.title: Calculate the dot product
steps.bodyLatex: Given \(\underset{\sim}{u} = (1, 2)\) and \(\underset{\sim}{v} = (2, -3)\), the dot product is \(\underset{\sim}{u} \cdot \underset{\sim}{v} = (1)(2) + (2)(-3) = 2 - 6 = -4\).

steps.title: Calculate the squared magnitude of v
steps.bodyLatex: The magnitude squared is \(|\underset{\sim}{v}|^2 = 2^2 + (-3)^2 = 4 + 9 = 13\).

steps.title: Substitute into the projection formula
steps.bodyLatex: Using the formula \(\left( \frac{\underset{\sim}{u} \cdot \underset{\sim}{v}}{|\underset{\sim}{v}|^2} \right) \underset{\sim}{v}\), we substitute our values to get \(\left( \frac{-4}{13} \right) (2\underset{\sim}{i} - 3\underset{\sim}{j})\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the projection of u onto v with the projection of v onto u.
Forgetting to square the magnitude of v, often using the magnitude itself instead of the squared magnitude.
Arithmetic errors when calculating the dot product, specifically with negative signs.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The projection vector must be a scalar multiple of the target vector \(\underset{\sim}{v}\). Since our result is a multiple of \((2\underset{\sim}{i} - 3\underset{\sim}{j})\), it is consistent with the definition of a projection onto \(\underset{\sim}{v}\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests the application of trigonometric substitution to evaluate a definite integral.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the substitution \( x = 5 \sin \theta \) to transform the integrand and the limits of integration into terms of \( \theta \).

steps.title: Differentiate the substitution
steps.bodyLatex: Given \( x = 5 \sin \theta \), we differentiate with respect to \( \theta \) to find \( dx = 5 \cos \theta \, d\theta \).

steps.title: Transform the limits of integration
steps.bodyLatex: When \( x = -\frac{5}{2} \), \( -\frac{5}{2} = 5 \sin \theta \implies \sin \theta = -\frac{1}{2} \), so \( \theta = -\frac{\pi}{6} \). When \( x = \frac{5}{2} \), \( \frac{5}{2} = 5 \sin \theta \implies \sin \theta = \frac{1}{2} \), so \( \theta = \frac{\pi}{6} \).

steps.title: Substitute into the integral
steps.bodyLatex: Substituting \( x \) and \( dx \) into the integral gives \[ \int_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \frac{1}{25 - (5 \sin \theta)^2} \cdot (5 \cos \theta) \, d\theta = \int_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \frac{5 \cos \theta}{25(1 - \sin^2 \theta)} \, d\theta. \]

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.bodyLatex: Using the identity \( 1 - \sin^2 \theta = \cos^2 \theta \), the integral becomes \[ \int_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \frac{5 \cos \theta}{25 \cos^2 \theta} \, d\theta = \frac{1}{5} \int_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \frac{1}{\cos \theta} \, d\theta = \frac{1}{5} \int_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \sec \theta \, d\theta. \]

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to transform the limits of integration from \( x \) to \( \theta \).
Failing to include the differential \( dx = 5 \cos \theta \, d\theta \) in the substitution.
Incorrectly simplifying \( 25 - 25 \sin^2 \theta \) as \( 25 \cos \theta \) instead of \( 25 \cos^2 \theta \).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the result by checking the dimensions and the trigonometric identity \( \frac{1}{\cos \theta} = \sec \theta \), which matches option B.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate the mean and variance of a discrete random variable using its probability distribution.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the definitions \( E(X) = \sum x P(x) \) and \( \text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 \). First, calculate the probabilities for \( x=0 \) and \( x=1 \), then apply the expectation formulas.

steps.title: Calculate the mean E(X)
steps.bodyLatex: \( E(X) = \sum x P(x) = (0 \times \frac{1}{3}) + (1 \times \frac{2}{3}) = \frac{2}{3} \).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the probability distribution \( P(x) \) with the values of \( x \).
Forgetting to square the mean when calculating the variance.
Arithmetic errors when subtracting fractions with different denominators.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since \( X \) is a Bernoulli trial with \( p = P(1) = \frac{2}{3} \), the mean is \( p = \frac{2}{3} \) and the variance is \( p(1-p) = \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{9} \), which confirms our result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to solve trigonometric equations by using complementary angle identities to convert between sine and cosine.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the identity \(\sin \theta = \cos(\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta)\) to rewrite the equation as \(\cos 5x = -\cos(\frac{\pi}{2} - x)\). Then, we use the property \(-\cos \theta = \cos(\pi - \theta)\) to solve for \(x\) within the given domain.

steps.title: Rewrite the equation
steps.bodyLatex: Given \(\cos 5x + \sin x = 0\), we rewrite this as \(\cos 5x = -\sin x\). Using the identity \(\sin x = \cos(\frac{\pi}{2} - x)\), the equation becomes \(\cos 5x = -\cos(\frac{\pi}{2} - x)\).

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.bodyLatex: Using the identity \(-\cos \theta = \cos(\pi - \theta)\), we have \(\cos 5x = \cos(\pi - (\frac{\pi}{2} - x)) = \cos(\frac{\pi}{2} + x)\). This gives the general solutions: \(5x = 2k\pi \pm (\frac{\pi}{2} + x)\).

steps.title: Analyze the two cases
steps.bodyLatex: Case 1: \(5x = 2k\pi + \frac{\pi}{2} + x \implies 4x = 2k\pi + \frac{\pi}{2} \implies x = \frac{k\pi}{2} + \frac{\pi}{8}\). For \(0 \le x \le 2\pi\), \(k = 0, 1, 2, 3\) gives 4 solutions. Case 2: \(5x = 2k\pi - (\frac{\pi}{2} + x) \implies 6x = 2k\pi - \frac{\pi}{2} \implies x = \frac{k\pi}{3} - \frac{\pi}{12}\). For \(0 \le x \le 2\pi\), \(k = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\) gives 6 solutions.

steps.title: Total count
steps.bodyLatex: Checking for overlaps: the solutions are \(\frac{\pi}{8}, \frac{5\pi}{8}, \frac{9\pi}{8}, \frac{13\pi}{8}\) and \(\frac{3\pi}{12}, \frac{7\pi}{12}, \frac{11\pi}{12}, \frac{15\pi}{12}, \frac{19\pi}{12}, \frac{23\pi}{12}\). Since there are no common values, we have \(4 + 6 = 10\) distinct solutions.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to check for overlapping solutions between the two cases.
Misapplying the negative cosine identity, leading to incorrect general solution forms.
Missing solutions near the boundaries of the domain \(0 \le x \le 2\pi\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: You can verify by sketching the graphs of \(y = \cos 5x\) and \(y = -\sin x\) on the interval \([0, 2\pi]\) to visually confirm the 10 intersection points.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the properties of definite integrals of even and odd functions over symmetric intervals.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For an integral \(\int_{-a}^{a} f(x) dx\), if \(f(x)\) is an odd function, the integral is equal to zero. Recall that \(f(x)\) is odd if \(f(-x) = -f(x)\) and even if \(f(-x) = f(x)\).

steps.title: Recall properties of odd and even functions
steps.bodyLatex: An odd function multiplied by an even function is odd. An odd function multiplied by an odd function is even. Recall that \(x\) and \(\tan^{-1}(x)\) are odd functions, while \(x^2\) is an even function.

steps.title: Evaluate the parity of the integrand in option D
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(f(x) = x^2 \tan^{-1}(x)\). Since \(x^2\) is even and \(\tan^{-1}(x)\) is odd, the product \(f(x)\) is an odd function because \(f(-x) = (-x)^2 \tan^{-1}(-x) = x^2 (-\tan^{-1}(x)) = -x^2 \tan^{-1}(x) = -f(x)\).

steps.title: Apply the integral property
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(f(x) = x^2 \tan^{-1}(x)\) is an odd function, the integral over the symmetric interval $[-a, a]$ must be zero: \(\int_{-a}^{a} x^2 \tan^{-1}(x) dx = 0\).

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming that any integral over a symmetric interval involving an odd function is zero without checking the parity of the entire integrand.
Confusing the properties of even and odd products (e.g., thinking the product of two odd functions is odd).
Misidentifying \(\cos^{-1}(x)\) as an odd function (it is neither even nor odd).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check parity: \(x^2\) is even, \(\tan^{-1}(x)\) is odd. Even \(\times\) Odd = Odd. The integral of an odd function from \(-a\) to \(a\) is always \(0\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret a slope field and match it to its corresponding differential equation.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Examine the slope field for key features such as where the gradient is zero (horizontal tangents) and the symmetry of the slopes relative to the y-axis.

steps.title: Identify where the slope is zero
steps.bodyLatex: Observe the slope field along the y-axis (where \(x=0\)). The line segments are horizontal, meaning the gradient \(\frac{dy}{dx} = 0\) when \(x=0\).

steps.title: Test the given options
steps.bodyLatex: If we test \(x=0\) in the options: Option A gives \(0^2 = 0\), Option B gives \(0^2 + C = C \neq 0\), Option C gives \(0^3 = 0\), and Option D gives \(0^3 + C = C \neq 0\). This eliminates B and D.

steps.title: Check symmetry and sign
steps.bodyLatex: For both positive and negative values of \(x\), the slopes are positive (the lines point upwards). Since \(x^2\) is always positive (or zero) and \(x^3\) is negative when \(x < 0\), the correct equation must be \(\frac{dy}{dx} = x^2\).

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming the slope field represents the function \(y=f(x)\) rather than the derivative \(\frac{dy}{dx}\).
Ignoring the symmetry of the slope field across the y-axis.
Failing to test specific points like \(x=0\) to quickly eliminate options.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Pick a point, say \(x=2\). The slope field shows a positive gradient. Substituting \(x=2\) into \(\frac{dy}{dx} = x^2\) gives \(4\), which is positive, consistent with the visual representation.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests the understanding of collinear points and section formulas in vector geometry.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the relationship between the vectors to express the position of point \(C\) relative to \(A\) and \(B\). By rearranging the equation \(c = 3a - 2b\) into the form of a section formula or a linear combination where the coefficients sum to \(1\), we can determine the relative positions of the points.

steps.title: Rearrange the vector equation
steps.bodyLatex: Given the position vector \(c = 3a - 2b\), we can rewrite this by subtracting \(a\) from both sides or manipulating the coefficients to identify the ratio. A more direct way is to express the vector \(AC\) in terms of \(AB\): \(c - a = (3a - 2b) - a = 2a - 2b = 2(a - b)\).

steps.title: Interpret the vector relationship
steps.bodyLatex: The equation \(c - a = -2(b - a)\) can be written as \(AC = -2AB\). This means that the vector \(AC\) is in the opposite direction to \(AB\) and is twice as long.

steps.title: Determine the order of points
steps.bodyLatex: Since AC = -2AB, point A must lie between B and C. Specifically, if we place A at the origin of a number line, B is at some position and C is at twice the distance in the opposite direction, confirming A is the middle point.

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming the coefficients 3 and -2 directly correspond to the ratio of distances without considering the vector directions.
Confusing the order of points by failing to correctly identify which point is the 'middle' point in the collinear relationship.
Neglecting to check the signs in the vector subtraction, leading to an incorrect geometric interpretation.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Let \(a = 0\) (\(A\) at origin). Then \(c = -2b\). This places \(A\) between \(B\) and \(C\), confirming that \(A\) is the midpoint relative to the vector directions.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of vector addition and the geometric properties of the dot product.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the property that if the sum of three vectors is zero, the magnitude of one vector is equal to the negative sum of the other two. Squaring both sides allows us to introduce the dot product, which relates the magnitudes and the angle between the vectors.

steps.title: Isolate one vector
steps.bodyLatex: Given \(\underset{\sim}{a} + \underset{\sim}{b} + \underset{\sim}{c} = \underset{\sim}{0}\), we can rearrange this to isolate \(\underset{\sim}{c}\): \(\underset{\sim}{a} + \underset{\sim}{b} = -\underset{\sim}{c}\).

steps.title: Square both sides
steps.bodyLatex: Square both sides of the equation to relate the magnitudes: \((\underset{\sim}{a} + \underset{\sim}{b}) \cdot (\underset{\sim}{a} + \underset{\sim}{b}) = (-\underset{\sim}{c}) \cdot (-\underset{\sim}{c})\). This simplifies to \(|\underset{\sim}{a}|^2 + 2(\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b}) + |\underset{\sim}{b}|^2 = |\underset{\sim}{c}|^2\).

steps.title: Substitute and solve for the dot product
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the known magnitudes: \(3^2 + 2(\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b}) + 5^2 = 7^2\). This gives \(9 + 2(\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b}) + 25 = 49\), which simplifies to \(2(\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b}) = 15\), so \(\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b} = 7.5\).

steps.title: Find the angle
steps.bodyLatex: Using the definition \(\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b} = |\underset{\sim}{a}||\underset{\sim}{b}| \cos \theta\), we have \(7.5 = 3 \times 5 \times \cos \theta\). Thus, \(15 \cos \theta = 7.5\), which means \(\cos \theta = 0.5\), leading to \(\theta = \frac{\pi}{3}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that the angle between vectors is defined when they are placed tail-to-tail, which is handled correctly by the dot product formula.
Incorrectly expanding the square of a vector sum as \(|\underset{\sim}{a}|^2 + |\underset{\sim}{b}|^2\) and forgetting the \(2(\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b})\) term.
Confusing the angle between vectors with the interior angles of the triangle formed by the vectors.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The values 3, 5, and 7 satisfy the Law of Cosines for a triangle: \(7^2 = 3^2 + 5^2 - 2(3)(5)\cos(180^\circ - \theta)\). Since \(\cos(180^\circ - 60^\circ) = \cos(120^\circ) = -0.5\), we get \(49 = 9 + 25 - 30(-0.5) = 34 + 15 = 49\), which confirms the result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the relationship between the derivatives of a function and its inverse function, specifically applying the formula for the second derivative of an inverse.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the identity \(g(f(x)) = x\). By differentiating this twice with respect to \(x\) using the chain rule and product rule, we can express \(g''(f(x))\) in terms of \(f'(x)\) and \(f''(x)\).

steps.title: Establish the first derivative
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(g(f(x)) = x\), differentiating both sides with respect to \(x\) gives \(g'(f(x)) \cdot f'(x) = 1\). Rearranging, we get \(g'(f(x)) = \frac{1}{f'(x)}\). At \(x=3\), \(g'(f(3)) = g'(1) = \frac{1}{f'(3)} = \frac{1}{2}\).

steps.title: Differentiate again
steps.bodyLatex: Differentiate \(g'(f(x)) \cdot f'(x) = 1\) using the product rule: \(g''(f(x)) \cdot (f'(x))^2 + g'(f(x)) \cdot f''(x) = 0\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the chain rule when differentiating \(g'(f(x))\).
Incorrectly assuming \(g''(x) = 1/f''(x)\), which is a common algebraic trap.
Failing to substitute the correct value \(f(3)=1\) into the derivative formulas.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The formula for the second derivative of an inverse is \(g''(y) = -\frac{f''(x)}{(f'(x))^3}\). Substituting \(x=3\): \(g''(1) = -\frac{4}{2^3} = -\frac{4}{8} = -\frac{1}{2}\). This confirms the result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests fundamental algebraic and calculus skills including inverse functions, trigonometric identities, integration techniques, and the properties of polynomial roots.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We will use algebraic rearrangement for inverses, double-angle identities to simplify trigonometric equations and integrals, and Vieta’s formulas to relate roots to coefficients.

steps.title: (a) Find the inverse function
steps.bodyLatex: Set \(y = 1 - \frac{1}{x-2}\). Rearrange to isolate \(x\): \(y - 1 = -\frac{1}{x-2}\), so \(x - 2 = -\frac{1}{y-1}\). Thus, \(x = 2 - \frac{1}{y-1}\). Swapping variables gives \(f^{-1}(x) = 2 - \frac{1}{x-1}\).

steps.title: (b) Solve the trigonometric equation
steps.bodyLatex: Use the identity \(\sin 2\theta = 2\sin \theta \cos \theta\). The equation becomes \(2\sin \theta \cos \theta - \sin \theta = 0\), which factors to \(\sin \theta(2\cos \theta - 1) = 0\). Solving \(\sin \theta = 0\) gives \(\theta = 0, \pi\), and \(\cos \theta = \frac{1}{2}\) gives \(\theta = \frac{\pi}{3}\).

steps.title: (c) Integrate the product
steps.bodyLatex: Use the product-to-sum identity \(\sin A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}(\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B))\). This gives \(\int \frac{1}{2}(\sin 4x + \sin 2x) dx = \frac{1}{2} [-\frac{\cos 4x}{4} - \frac{\cos 2x}{2}] + C = -\frac{\cos 4x}{8} - \frac{\cos 2x}{4} + C\).

steps.title: (d) Sketch the graph
steps.bodyLatex: The function \(y = \frac{1}{3} \cos^{-1}(2x)\) has domain \([-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}]\) and range \([0, \frac{\pi}{3}]\). Plot the endpoints \((-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{\pi}{3})\) and \((\frac{1}{2}, 0)\), noting the curve passes through \((0, \frac{\pi}{6})\) and is concave down.

steps.title: (e) Determine parallel vectors
steps.bodyLatex: Two vectors are parallel if one is a scalar multiple of the other. Since \(\binom{2}{6} = 2\binom{1}{3}\), we compare this to \(\binom{1}{m}\) and see that \(m = 3\).

steps.title: (f) Evaluate the sum of reciprocals
steps.bodyLatex: \(The expression \frac{1}{\alpha\beta} + \frac{1}{\alpha\gamma} + \frac{1}{\beta\gamma} simplifies to \frac{\gamma + \beta + \alpha}{\alpha\beta\gamma} . Using Vieta's formulas, \alpha+\beta+\gamma = -\frac{6}{2} = -3 and \alpha\beta\gamma = -(\frac{-1}{2}) = \frac{1}{2} . Thus, -3 \div \frac{1}{2} = -6.\)

steps.title: (g) Evaluate the definite integral
steps.bodyLatex: Use the identity \(\cos^2(3x) = \frac{1 + \cos(6x)}{2}\). Integrating gives \(\frac{1}{2} [x + \frac{\sin 6x}{6}]_{\pi/6}^{\pi/3}\). Evaluating at the bounds: \(\frac{1}{2}(\frac{\pi}{3} + 0) - \frac{1}{2}(\frac{\pi}{6} + 0) = \frac{\pi}{6} - \frac{\pi}{12} = \frac{\pi}{12}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Dividing by sin(theta) in part (b), which loses the solutions theta = 0 and pi.
Confusing the inverse function notation \(f^{-1}(x)\) with the reciprocal \(1/f(x)\).
Failing to use the product-to-sum identity in part (c), leading to unnecessarily complex integration by parts.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a), check if \(f(f^{-1}(x)) = x\). For (b), substitute values back into the original equation. For (f), use the sum of roots and product of roots to verify the relationship.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply calculus to rates of change, volumes of revolution, mathematical induction, differential equations, and trigonometric identities.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the chain rule for related rates, the disk method for volumes of revolution, standard induction steps for sequences, separation of variables for differential equations, and the harmonic addition formula for trigonometric equations.

steps.title: (a) Related Rates of Change
steps.bodyLatex: The area of a sector is \(A = \frac{1}{2}r^2\theta = 10\). Differentiating with respect to \(t\) gives \(\frac{1}{2}(2r\theta \frac{dr}{dt} + r^2 \frac{d\theta}{dt}) = 0\). Substituting \(r=4\), \(\theta = \frac{20}{r^2} = \frac{20}{16} = 1.25\), and \(\frac{d\theta}{dt} = 2\), we get \(r\theta \frac{dr}{dt} + \frac{r^2}{2}(2) = 0 \implies 4(1.25)\frac{dr}{dt} + 16 = 0\). Solving gives \(5\frac{dr}{dt} = -16\), so \(\frac{dr}{dt} = -3.2 \text{ cm s}^{-1}\).

steps.title: (b) Volume of Revolution
steps.bodyLatex: For rotation about the \(y\)-axis, the volume is \(V = \pi \int_{1}^{a} x^2 dy\). Since \(y = \frac{1}{x}\), we have \(x = \frac{1}{y}\), so \(x^2 = \frac{1}{y^2}\). Thus, \(V = \pi \int_{1}^{a} y^{-2} dy = \pi [-y^{-1}]_{1}^{a} = \pi (-( \frac{1}{a} - 1 )) = \pi - \frac{\pi}{a}\).

steps.title: (c) Mathematical Induction
steps.bodyLatex: Base case \(n=1\): \(1(1!) = 1\) and \((1+1)! - 1 = 2-1 = 1\). Assume true for \(n=k\): \(\sum_{i=1}^{k} i(i!) = (k+1)! - 1\). For \(n=k+1\), the sum is \((k+1)! - 1 + (k+1)(k+1)!\). Factoring out \((k+1)!\) gives \((k+1)!(1 + k + 1) - 1 = (k+2)! - 1\), which confirms the statement for \(n=k+1\).

steps.title: (d) Differential Equations
steps.bodyLatex: Separate variables: \(\int \frac{dy}{\sqrt{4-y^2}} = \int dx\). This integrates to \(\sin^{-1}(\frac{y}{2}) = x + C\). Using \(y=1\) at \(x=0\), we find \(\sin^{-1}(0.5) = 0 + C\), so \(C = \frac{\pi}{6}\). The solution is \(x = \sin^{-1}(\frac{y}{2}) - \frac{\pi}{6}\).

steps.title: (e) Trigonometric Equations
steps.bodyLatex: For (i), \(2(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin x - \frac{1}{2}\cos x) = 2\sin(x - \frac{\pi}{6})\). For (ii), set \(2\sin(x - \frac{\pi}{6}) = 1\), so \(\sin(x - \frac{\pi}{6}) = \frac{1}{2}\). Thus \(x - \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{6}\) or \(\frac{5\pi}{6}\), yielding \(x = \frac{\pi}{3}, \pi\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the factor of \(\pi\) in the volume of revolution formula.
Incorrectly differentiating the area formula by assuming \(r\) or \(\theta\) is constant.
Failing to use the chain rule correctly when relating \(\frac{dr}{dt}\) and \(\frac{d\theta}{dt}\).
Mixing up the axes of rotation in the volume integral.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a), check units match: \(\text{cm}^2 / \text{cm} / \text{s} = \text{cm/s}\). For (b), as \(a \to \infty\), \(V \to \pi\), which is the volume of the infinite horn.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests separation of variables for differential equations, circular permutations with restrictions, angle between velocity and acceleration vectors, normal approximation to the binomial distribution, and combinatorial identities proved by induction-style telescoping.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Each part uses a distinct technique: (a) separate and integrate, (b) subtract restricted arrangements from total circular arrangements, (c) use the dot product formula \(\underline{v}\cdot\underline{a}=|\underline{v}||\underline{a}|\cos\theta\), (d) standardise the binomial using \(Z=\frac{X-\mu}{\sigma}\), and (e) rearrange Pascal's relation then telescope the sum.

steps.title: (a) Separate variables and integrate
steps.bodyLatex:
Rearrange \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{5}{y}\) by multiplying both sides by \(y\):
\[y\,dy = 5\,dx.\]
Integrate both sides:
\[\int y\,dy = \int 5\,dx \implies \frac{y^2}{2} = 5x + C.\]

steps.title: (a) Apply the initial condition and solve for \(y\)
steps.bodyLatex:
Substitute \(x=0,\, y=-4\):
\[\frac{(-4)^2}{2} = 5(0)+C \implies C = 8.\]
So \(y^2 = 10x+16\). Since \(y=-4\) at \(x=0\), \(y\) is negative, giving
\[\boxed{y = -\sqrt{10x+16}}.\]

steps.title: (b) Count unrestricted circular arrangements, then subtract restricted ones
steps.bodyLatex: For \(8\) guests at a round table, fix one person to remove rotational symmetry: unrestricted arrangements \(= 7! = 5040\). Now count arrangements where the two guests who refuse to sit together ARE adjacent: treat them as a single block, giving \(7\) objects around a round table, fixed one \(\Rightarrow 6!\) arrangements, and the pair can swap within the block in \(2!\) ways, so restricted arrangements \(= 6!\times 2 = 1440\). Valid arrangements \(= 7! - 6!\times 2 = 5040 - 1440 = \boxed{3600}\).

steps.title: (c) Find \(\underline{v}(t)\) and \(\underline{a}(t)\)
steps.bodyLatex:
Differentiate \(\underline{r}(t)=t\,\underline{i}+\dfrac{t^2}{9}\,\underline{j}\):
\[\underline{v}(t) = \underline{i}+\frac{2t}{9}\,\underline{j},  \underline{a}(t) = \frac{2}{9}\,\underline{j}.\]

steps.title: (c) Compute the dot product and magnitudes
steps.bodyLatex:
The dot product is
\[\underline{v}\cdot\underline{a} = (1)(0)+\frac{2t}{9}\cdot\frac{2}{9} = \frac{4t}{81}.\]
The magnitudes are
\[|\underline{v}| = \sqrt{1+\frac{4t^2}{81}},  |\underline{a}| = \frac{2}{9}.\]

steps.title: (c) Set up and solve the angle equation
steps.bodyLatex:
Using \(\underline{v}\cdot\underline{a}=|\underline{v}||\underline{a}|\cos\dfrac{\pi}{4}\):
\[\frac{4t}{81} = \sqrt{1+\frac{4t^2}{81}}\cdot\frac{2}{9}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}.\]
Square both sides:
\[\frac{16t^2}{81^2} = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{4}{81}\left(1+\frac{4t^2}{81}\right).\]
Multiply through by \(\dfrac{81^2}{4}\):
\[4t^2 = \frac{81}{2}\left(1+\frac{4t^2}{81}\right) = \frac{81}{2}+2t^2.\]
So \(2t^2 = \dfrac{81}{2}\), giving \(t^2 = \dfrac{81}{4}\), and since \(t>0\),
\[\boxed{t = \frac{9}{2}}.\]

steps.title: (d) Find \(p\), \(\mu\), and \(\sigma\) for the binomial
steps.bodyLatex: Here \(X\sim\text{Binomial}(n=100,\,p)\). Since \(E(X)=np=20\), we get \(p=\dfrac{1}{5}\). The variance is \(\sigma^2 = np(1-p)=100\cdot\dfrac{1}{5}\cdot\dfrac{4}{5}=16\), so \(\sigma=4\).

steps.title: (d) Apply the normal approximation and find \(k\)
steps.bodyLatex:
Using the normal approximation \(X\approx N(20,\,16)\), standardise with \(Z=\dfrac{X-20}{4}\). We need \(P(X\ge k)=0.0668\), so \(P(X<k)=1-0.0668=0.9332\). From the provided table, \(P(Z\le 1.5)=0.9332\). Therefore:
\[\frac{k-20}{4}=1.5 \implies k-20=6 \implies \boxed{k=26}.\]

steps.title: (e)(i) Rearrange Pascal's relation to show the required identity
steps.bodyLatex:
Apply Pascal's relation \(\dbinom{n}{r}=\dbinom{n-1}{r-1}+\dbinom{n-1}{r}\) with \(n=m+1\) and \(r=R+1\):
\[\binom{m+1}{R+1}=\binom{m}{R}+\binom{m}{R+1}.\]
Rearranging gives
\[\binom{m}{R}=\binom{m+1}{R+1}-\binom{m}{R+1}.  \blacksquare\]

steps.title: (e)(ii) Telescope the sum using part (i)
steps.bodyLatex:
Apply part (i) with \(R=2000\) to each term from \(m=2001\) to \(m=2049\) (note \(\dbinom{2000}{2000}=1\) stays as is):
\[\binom{2001}{2000}=\binom{2002}{2001}-\binom{2001}{2001},\]
\[\binom{2002}{2000}=\binom{2003}{2001}-\binom{2002}{2001},\]
\[\vdots\]
\[\binom{2050}{2000}=\binom{2051}{2001}-\binom{2050}{2001}.\]
Summing the left-hand side equals the telescoping right-hand side:
\[\text{LHS}=\binom{2000}{2000}+\left[\binom{2002}{2001}-\binom{2001}{2001}\right]+\left[\binom{2003}{2001}-\binom{2002}{2001}\right]+\cdots+\left[\binom{2051}{2001}-\binom{2050}{2001}\right].\]
All intermediate terms cancel, leaving
\[\text{LHS}=\binom{2000}{2000}-\binom{2001}{2001}+\binom{2051}{2001}=1-1+\binom{2051}{2001}=\binom{2051}{2001}=\text{RHS}.  \blacksquare\]

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) \(y=-\sqrt{10x+16}\)  (b) \(3600\) arrangements  (c) \(t=\dfrac{9}{2}\)  (d) \(k=26\)  (e)(i) Shown by rearranging Pascal's relation.  (e)(ii) Proved by telescoping using part (i).
commonMistakesLatex: In (a), forgetting to choose the negative square root: since \(y=-4<0\) at \(x=0\), you must take \(y=-\sqrt{10x+16}\), not \(y=\pm\sqrt{10x+16}\).
In (b), forgetting to multiply by \(2!\) when the restricted pair can swap seats within their block, giving \(6!\) instead of \(6!\times 2\) for the restricted count.
In (c), not squaring both sides carefully — a common error is forgetting to square \(|\underline{v}|\) fully, or losing the factor of \(\frac{1}{2}\) from \(\cos^2\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{1}{2}\).
In (d), using \(P(X\ge k)=0.0668\) directly as a left-tail probability instead of converting to \(P(X<k)=0.9332\) before reading the \(z\)-table.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: (a) Substitute \(x=0\): \(y=-\sqrt{16}=-4\,\checkmark\); differentiate \(y=-\sqrt{10x+16}\) to get \(\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{-10}{2\sqrt{10x+16}}=\frac{-5}{y}\,\checkmark\) (since \(y<0\)). (c) At \(t=\frac{9}{2}\): \(\underline{v}=\underline{i}+\underline{j}\) and \(\underline{a}=\frac{2}{9}\underline{j}\); the angle between \(\underline{i}+\underline{j}\) and \(\underline{j}\) is indeed \(\frac{\pi}{4}\,\checkmark\). (d) Check: \(P\!\left(Z\ge\frac{26-20}{4}\right)=P(Z\ge 1.5)=0.0668\,\checkmark\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply the Pigeonhole Principle, projectile motion equations, trigonometric identities, differentiation of inverse functions, and the relationship between roots and coefficients of polynomials.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For each part, we use specific mathematical tools: the Pigeonhole Principle for number theory, kinematic equations for projectile motion, scalar product for perpendicular vectors, chain rule for derivatives, and Vieta's formulas for polynomials.

steps.title: (a) Pigeonhole Principle
steps.bodyLatex: The factors of 60 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 10, 12, 15, 20, 30, 60. We group these into 6 pairs that multiply to 60: [1, 60], [2, 30], [3, 20], [4, 15], [5, 12], and [6, 10]. By the Pigeonhole Principle, if we choose 7 distinct factors, at least one pair must be selected, ensuring the product is a multiple of 60.

steps.title: (b) Projectile Motion
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(OA = kh\) and \(OB = h\). For particle A, \(y = kh - \frac{1}{2}gt^2 = 0 \implies t = \sqrt{2kh/g}\). For particle B, \(y = h - \frac{1}{2}g(t-T)^2 = 0 \implies t-T = \sqrt{2h/g}\). Since both land at the same horizontal distance \(x\), \(Ut = V(t-T)\), so \(V/U = t/(t-T) = \sqrt{2kh/g} / \sqrt{2h/g} = \sqrt{k}\).

steps.title: (c) Perpendicular Hands
steps.bodyLatex: The vectors are \(A = (\sin \theta_1, \cos \theta_1)\) and \(B = (2\sin \theta_2, 2\cos \theta_2)\) where \(\theta_1 = \pi t/360\) and \(\theta_2 = \pi t/30\). The hands are perpendicular when their dot product is zero: \(2\sin \theta_1 \sin \theta_2 + 2\cos \theta_1 \cos \theta_2 = 0\). This simplifies to \(\cos(\theta_2 - \theta_1) = 0\), so \(11\pi t/360 = \pi/2, 3\pi/2, \dots\). Solving for \(t\) gives \(t \approx 16.364\) and \(t \approx 49.091\).

steps.title: (d) Differentiating \(f(x)\)
steps.bodyLatex: Using the chain rule, \(f'(x) = -1/\sqrt{1-\sin^2 x} \cdot \cos x = -\cos x / |\cos x|\). For \(x \in (0, \pi/2)\), \(\cos x > 0\) so \(f'(x) = -1\). For \(x \in (\pi/2, \pi)\), \(\cos x < 0\) so \(f'(x) = 1\).

steps.title: (e) Sum of Roots
steps.bodyLatex: By Vieta's, \(\sum \tan \alpha = -b\), \(\sum \tan \alpha \tan \beta = c\), and \(\prod \tan \alpha = 1-b-c\). Using the identity \(\tan(\alpha+\beta+\gamma) = (\sum \tan \alpha - \prod \tan \alpha) / (1 - \sum \tan \alpha \tan \beta) = (-b - (1-b-c)) / (1-c) = (c-1)/(1-c) = -1\). Thus, \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma = 3\pi/4\).

commonMistakesLatex: In (b), assuming both particles have the same time of flight.
In (c), forgetting the scalar product condition for perpendicularity.
In (d), failing to consider the absolute value when simplifying \(\sqrt{\cos^2 x}\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (b), if \(k=4\), \(V/U = 2\), which is consistent with the square root relationship. For (e), testing \(b=0, c=0\) gives \(x^3-1=0\), roots are \(1, \omega, \omega^2\), sum of arctans confirms \(3\pi/4\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the relationship between the roots of a cubic polynomial and its coefficients using Vieta's formulas.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For a cubic polynomial in the form \(ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d = 0\), the sum of the roots is given by \(-\frac{b}{a}\). We can use this property to solve for the unknown root \(\alpha\).

steps.title: Identify the coefficients
steps.bodyLatex: For the polynomial \(x^3 + 2x^2 - 5x - 6\), we identify the coefficients as \(a = 1\) and \(b = 2\).

steps.title: Apply Vieta's formula for the sum of roots
steps.bodyLatex: The sum of the roots of the cubic \(x^3 + 2x^2 - 5x - 6 = 0\) is equal to \(-\frac{b}{a} = -\frac{2}{1} = -2\).

steps.title: Solve for \(\alpha\)
steps.bodyLatex: Given the roots are \(-1\), \(-3\), and \(\alpha\), their sum is \((-1) + (-3) + \alpha = -2\). Simplifying this gives \(-4 + \alpha = -2\), which leads to \(\alpha = 2\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the sign in Vieta's formula (e.g., using \(+\frac{b}{a}\) instead of \(-\frac{b}{a}\)).
Forgetting that the sum of roots includes all three roots, not just the two provided.
Making arithmetic errors when adding negative numbers.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: We can verify this by checking the product of the roots, which should be \(-\frac{d}{a} = -(-\frac{6}{1}) = 6\). Since \((-1) \times (-3) \times 2 = 6\), our value for \(\alpha\) is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of using definite integrals to calculate the area between two curves. It specifically requires you to account for regions where the curves cross, ensuring the resulting area is always positive.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: The area between two curves \(f(x)\) and \(g(x)\) is given by \(\int |f(x) - g(x)| dx\). When the curves intersect, you must split the integral at the intersection points and negate the integral for any interval where \(g(x) > f(x)\) to ensure the area remains positive.

steps.title: Understand the area formula
steps.bodyLatex: The area between two curves is the integral of the 'upper' function minus the 'lower' function. If \(f(x) > g(x)\), the area is \(\int (f(x) - g(x)) dx\). If \(g(x) > f(x)\), the integral \(\int (f(x) - g(x)) dx\) will be negative, so we must multiply by \(-1\) to get a positive area.

steps.title: Analyze the intervals
steps.bodyLatex: Looking at the diagram, the curves intersect at \(x = -3, -1, 1\). This divides the region into four parts: $[-4, -3]$, $[-3, -1]$, $[-1, 1]$, and $[1, 4]$. In intervals $[-4, -3]$ and $[-1, 1]$, \(g(x)\) is above \(f(x)\), so \(f(x) - g(x) < 0\). In intervals $[-3, -1]$ and $[1, 4]$, \(f(x)\) is above \(g(x)\), so \(f(x) - g(x) > 0\).

steps.title: Construct the total area
steps.bodyLatex: To keep all areas positive, we negate the integrals where \(g(x) > f(x)\): \(-\int_{-4}^{-3} (f(x) - g(x)) dx + \int_{-3}^{-1} (f(x) - g(x)) dx - \int_{-1}^{1} (f(x) - g(x)) dx + \int_{1}^{4} (f(x) - g(x)) dx\). This matches option D.

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming the area is simply the integral of the difference over the whole domain, which ignores the sign changes caused by the curves crossing.
Applying an absolute value to the entire integral, which is incorrect because the integral of the sum of absolute values is not the absolute value of the integral.
Forgetting to negate the intervals where the bottom curve is higher than the top curve.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify that each term in the sum represents a positive area by checking the relative positions of \(f(x)\) and \(g(x)\) in each sub-interval against the sign provided in the expression.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the Pigeonhole Principle, specifically the generalized version used to guarantee a minimum number of items in a category.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To find the minimum number of students required to guarantee at least \( n \) students from one school, we use the worst-case scenario. We assume each of the 4 schools has \( n-1 \) students before adding one more student to force the condition.

steps.title: Identify the worst-case scenario
steps.bodyLatex: We want to ensure at least 20 students are from one school. To avoid this for as long as possible, we assume each of the 4 schools has exactly 19 students. This is the maximum number of students we can have without meeting the requirement.

steps.title: Calculate the maximum number of students without the condition
steps.bodyLatex: If there are 4 schools and each has 19 students, the total number of students is \( 4 \times 19 = 76 \).

steps.title: Apply the Pigeonhole Principle
steps.bodyLatex: With 76 students, it is possible that every school has exactly 19 students. By adding one more student, that student must belong to one of the 4 schools, making that school's total \( 19 + 1 = 20 \). Therefore, the minimum number required is \( 76 + 1 = 77 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Calculating \(4 * 20 = 80\), which is the number of students required to have \(20\) in each school, not just one.
Forgetting to add the final student, leading to the answer 76.
Confusing the number of schools with the number of students per school.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: If we have \(77\) students and \(4\) schools, by the Pigeonhole Principle, at least one school must have at least \(\lceil 77/4 \rceil = \lceil 19.25 \rceil = 20\) students. This confirms \(77\) is the correct threshold.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the domain and range of inverse trigonometric functions, specifically the identity relating \(\cos^{-1}(x)\) and \(\sin^{-1}(x)\).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, determine the domain by ensuring the argument of the inverse functions is valid. Then, simplify the expression using the identity \(\sin^{-1}(u) + \cos^{-1}(u) = \frac{\pi}{2}\) to find the range.

steps.title: Determine the domain
steps.bodyLatex: The functions \(\sin^{-1}(u)\) and \(\cos^{-1}(u)\) are only defined for \(-1 \le u \le 1\). Here, \(u = 2x\), so we must have \(-1 \le 2x \le 1\). Dividing by 2, we get \(-0.5 \le x \le 0.5\).

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.bodyLatex: Recall the identity \(\sin^{-1}(u) + \cos^{-1}(u) = \frac{\pi}{2}\). We can factor the given expression: \(y = 2(\cos^{-1}(2x) + \sin^{-1}(2x))\). Substituting the identity, we get \(y = 2(\frac{\pi}{2}) = \pi\).

steps.title: Identify the range
steps.bodyLatex: Since the expression simplifies to a constant value of \(\pi\) for all \(x\) in the domain, the range consists only of the single value \(\{\pi\}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming the range is a wide interval because of the individual inverse functions, forgetting that they sum to a constant.
Incorrectly identifying the domain by failing to account for the coefficient of \(x\) inside the inverse functions.
Confusing the identity \(\sin^{-1}(x) + \cos^{-1}(x) = \frac{\pi}{2}\) with other trigonometric identities.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Test a value in the domain, such as \(x=0\): \(y = 2\cos^{-1}(0) + 2\sin^{-1}(0) = 2(\frac{\pi}{2}) + 2(0) = \pi\). This confirms the range is the constant \(\pi\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of function transformations, specifically how coefficients inside and outside a function affect its graph.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We compare the standard form \( y = a \cdot f(bx) \) to \( g(x) = 2 \sin^{-1}(3x) \). The constant \( a \) represents a vertical dilation, while the coefficient \( b \) inside the function represents a horizontal dilation by a factor of \( 1/b \).

steps.title: Identify the vertical transformation
steps.bodyLatex: The function is \( g(x) = 2 \cdot \sin^{-1}(3x) \). The multiplier \( 2 \) outside the function indicates a vertical dilation by a factor of \( 2 \).

steps.title: Identify the horizontal transformation
steps.bodyLatex: The term inside the function is \( 3x \). For a transformation of the form \( f(bx) \), the horizontal dilation factor is \( 1/b \). Here, \( b = 3 \), so the horizontal dilation factor is \( 1/3 \).

steps.title: Match with the options
steps.bodyLatex: Combining these, we have a vertical dilation by \( 2 \) and a horizontal dilation by \( 1/3 \). This corresponds to option C.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the horizontal dilation factor: incorrectly thinking a coefficient of 3 means a dilation factor of 3 instead of 1/3.
Mixing up vertical and horizontal transformations by applying the factor of 2 to the x-axis or the factor of 3 to the y-axis.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the domain: \( f(x) = \sin^{-1}(x) \) has domain \( [-1, 1] \). For \( g(x) = \sin^{-1}(3x) \), the domain is \( -1 \le 3x \le 1 \), which simplifies to \( -1/3 \le x \le 1/3 \). This confirms the horizontal compression by a factor of 1/3.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to solve trigonometric equations involving absolute values within a specified domain.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Since \(b \neq 0\), we can divide both sides by \(|b|\) to simplify the equation to \(|\sin(4x)| = 1\). This is equivalent to solving \(\sin(4x) = 1\) or \(\sin(4x) = -1\).

steps.title: Simplify the equation
steps.bodyLatex: Given \(|b| = |b \sin(4x)|\), we divide by \(|b|\) (which is non-zero) to get \(1 = |\sin(4x)|\). This implies \(\sin(4x) = 1\) or \(\sin(4x) = -1\).

steps.title: Determine the range for 4x
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(x \in [0, 2\pi]\), multiplying by 4 gives the domain for the argument: \(4x \in [0, 8\pi]\).

steps.title: Solve for 4x
steps.bodyLatex: The equation \(\sin(4x) = 1\) occurs at \(4x = \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{5\pi}{2}, \frac{9\pi}{2}, \frac{13\pi}{2}\). The equation \(\sin(4x) = -1\) occurs at \(4x = \frac{3\pi}{2}, \frac{7\pi}{2}, \frac{11\pi}{2}, \frac{15\pi}{2}\).

steps.title: Count the solutions
steps.bodyLatex: There are 4 solutions from the first case and 4 solutions from the second case, resulting in a total of 8 distinct values for \(x\) within the interval \([0, 2\pi]\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that |sin(4x)| = 1 implies both positive and negative cases.
Failing to adjust the domain correctly for the 4x term, leading to missing solutions.
Confusing the number of solutions with the period of the function.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The function \(\sin(4x)\) completes 4 full cycles in the interval \([0, 2\pi]\). In each cycle, the absolute value reaches 1 exactly twice (at the peak and the trough), so \(4 \text{ cycles} \times 2 = 8 \text{ solutions}\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate probabilities involving independent events, specifically using the binomial distribution logic for a subset of trials.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Since the first 25 questions are already correct, the applicant only needs to pass the remaining 5 questions. We need to calculate the probability of getting at least 29 total correct, which means getting either 4 or 5 of the remaining 5 questions correct.

steps.title: Determine the required outcomes
steps.bodyLatex: The applicant has 25 correct answers. To reach at least 29, they need at least 4 more correct answers from the remaining 5 questions. This means they must get exactly 4 correct or exactly 5 correct.

steps.title: Calculate the probability of exactly 4 correct
steps.bodyLatex: Using the binomial probability formula \(P(X=k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}\), where \(n=5\), \(p=1/4\), and \(k=4\): \(P(X=4) = \binom{5}{4} (\frac{1}{4})^4 (\frac{3}{4})^1 = 5 \times \frac{1}{256} \times \frac{3}{4} = \frac{15}{1024}\).

steps.title: Calculate the probability of exactly 5 correct
Sum the probabilities
steps.bodyLatex: The total probability of passing is \(P(X \ge 4) = P(X=4) + P(X=5) = \frac{15}{1024} + \frac{1}{1024} = \frac{16}{1024}\). Simplifying this fraction gives \(\frac{16}{1024} = \frac{1}{64}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to include the case where all 5 remaining questions are answered correctly.
Misinterpreting 'at least 29' as needing exactly 29, leading to an incorrect binomial calculation.
Errors in simplifying the fraction 16/1024.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since \(1/64 = 16/1024\), and \(15/1024 + 1/1024 = 16/1024\), the calculation is consistent with the binomial distribution requirements.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the sampling distribution of a sample proportion, specifically the formula for the standard deviation of \(\hat{p}\).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the formula for the standard deviation of the sample proportion: \(\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}\). We set this expression to be less than 0.06 and solve the resulting inequality for \(n\).

steps.title: State the formula and given values
steps.bodyLatex: The standard deviation of the sample proportion is given by \(\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}\). We are given \(p = \frac{7}{12}\), so \(1-p = \frac{5}{12}\).

steps.title: Set up the inequality
steps.bodyLatex: We require \(\sqrt{\frac{\frac{7}{12} \times \frac{5}{12}}{n}} < 0.06\). Simplifying the numerator gives \(\sqrt{\frac{35}{144n}} < 0.06\).

steps.title: Solve for n
steps.bodyLatex: Square both sides to remove the square root: \(\frac{35}{144n} < 0.0036\). Rearranging for \(n\) gives \(n > \frac{35}{144 \times 0.0036}\). Calculating the right side: \(n > \frac{35}{0.5184} \approx 67.519\).

steps.title: Determine the smallest integer n
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(n\) must be an integer and \(n > 67.519\), the smallest possible value for \(n\) is 68.

commonMistakesLatex: Rounding down to 67 instead of rounding up to the next integer.
Forgetting to square the 0.06 when solving the inequality.
Incorrectly calculating the product of the probabilities \(p(1-p)\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For \(n=67\), \(\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{35}{144 \times 67}} \approx 0.0602 > 0.06\). For \(n=68\), \(\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{35}{144 \times 68}} \approx 0.0598 < 0.06\). The result is verified.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate probabilities for dependent events (sampling without replacement) and your understanding of combinations and permutations.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To find the probability that both coins are the same metal, we sum the probability of drawing two silver coins and the probability of drawing two bronze coins. Since the order of selection matters in the denominator (n(n-1)), we use the product rule for dependent events.

steps.title: Identify the two successful scenarios
steps.bodyLatex: The two coins are the same metal if we draw either (Silver, Silver) or (Bronze, Bronze).

steps.title: Calculate the probability of two silver coins
steps.bodyLatex: The probability of drawing the first silver coin is \( \frac{k}{n} \). Since there is no replacement, the probability of drawing a second silver coin is \( \frac{k-1}{n-1} \). Thus, the probability of two silver coins is \( \frac{k}{n} \times \frac{k-1}{n-1} = \frac{k(k-1)}{n(n-1)} \).

steps.title: Calculate the probability of two bronze coins
steps.bodyLatex: There are \( n-k \) bronze coins. Using the same logic, the probability of drawing two bronze coins is \( \frac{n-k}{n} \times \frac{n-k-1}{n-1} = \frac{(n-k)(n-k-1)}{n(n-1)} \).

steps.title: Combine the probabilities
steps.bodyLatex: Since these are mutually exclusive events, we add them together: \( P(\text{Same}) = \frac{k(k-1)}{n(n-1)} + \frac{(n-k)(n-k-1)}{n(n-1)} = \frac{k(k-1) + (n-k)(n-k-1)}{n(n-1)} \).

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming the events are independent and using replacement probabilities like \((k/n)^2\).
Confusing the formula for combinations (nCr) with the probability of sequential draws.
Forgetting to subtract 1 from the numerator when calculating the second draw without replacement.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: If \(n=3\) and \(k=2\) (\(2\) silver, \(1\) bronze), the probability of same is \((2/3 * 1/2) + (1/3 * 0/2) = 1/3\). Testing option A: \((2*1 + 1*0) / (3*2) = 2/6 = 1/3\). The formula holds.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the harmonic addition theorem and the relationship between phase shifts in trigonometric functions.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We express the function \(a \cos x + b \sin x\) in the form \(R \sin(x + \theta)\) and \(R \cos(x + \phi)\). By relating these to the four given forms, we determine the specific values of the phase shifts \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta\) within the interval \((0, 2\pi)\).

steps.title: Step 1: Define the R-formula
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(R = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}\). We know that \(a \cos x + b \sin x = R \sin(x + \theta_0)\) where \(\cos \theta_0 = b/R\) and \(\sin \theta_0 = a/R\). Similarly, \(a \cos x + b \sin x = R \cos(x - \phi_0)\) where \(\cos \phi_0 = a/R\) and \(\sin \phi_0 = b/R\).

steps.title: Step 2: Relate the forms to the phase shifts
steps.bodyLatex: Matching the forms: \(R \sin(x + \alpha) = R \sin(x + \theta_0) \implies \alpha = \theta_0\). For \(R \sin(x - \beta) = R \sin(x + \theta_0)\), we use the identity \(\sin(x + \theta_0) = \sin(x + \theta_0 + 2\pi) = \sin(x - (2\pi - \theta_0))\), so \(\beta = 2\pi - \theta_0\). Adding these gives \(\alpha + \beta = 2\pi\).

steps.title: Step 3: Solve for the cosine forms
steps.bodyLatex: For the cosine forms, \(R \cos(x + \gamma) = R \cos(x - \phi_0) \implies \gamma = -\phi_0\). Since we require \(0 < \gamma < 2\pi\), we use \(\gamma = 2\pi - \phi_0\). For \(R \cos(x - \delta) = R \cos(x - \phi_0) \implies \delta = \phi_0\). Adding these gives \(\gamma + \delta = 2\pi\).

steps.title: Step 4: Calculate the total sum
steps.bodyLatex: Summing the results from the previous steps: \(\alpha + \beta + \gamma + \delta = 2\pi + 2\pi = 4\pi\).

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming the phase shifts must be in the first quadrant, ignoring the requirement that they lie in the interval (0, 2π).
Forgetting that α, β, γ, δ are defined by the periodicity of the trigonometric functions, leading to missing the 2π offsets.
Confusing the sine and cosine phase shift relationships.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Test with \(a=1, b=1\). Then \(R=\sqrt{2}\). \(1\cos x + 1\sin x = \sqrt{2}\sin(x+\pi/4)\). Thus \(\alpha=\pi/4, \beta=7\pi/4, \gamma=7\pi/4, \delta=\pi/4\). Sum is \(\pi/4 + 7\pi/4 + 7\pi/4 + \pi/4 = 16\pi/4 = 4\pi\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your proficiency in vector operations, quadratic inequalities, integration techniques, differential equations, differentiation of inverse trigonometric functions, related rates, and finding areas between curves.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We apply standard algebraic, calculus, and geometric techniques for each part, ensuring we follow the specific instructions like substitution or formatting requirements.

steps.title: (a) Vector operations
steps.bodyLatex: (i) Compute \(2\underset{\sim}{a} - \underset{\sim}{b} = 2(3\underset{\sim}{i} + 2\underset{\sim}{j}) - (-1\underset{\sim}{i} + 4\underset{\sim}{j}) = (6+1)\underset{\sim}{i} + (4-4)\underset{\sim}{j} = 7\underset{\sim}{i}\) \ (ii) The dot product is given by \(a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2 = (3)(-1) + (2)(4) = -3 + 8 = 5\).

steps.title: (b) Solve quadratic inequality
steps.bodyLatex: Factor the quadratic: \(x^2 - 8x - 9 = (x - 9)(x + 1) \le 0\). The roots are \(x = -1\) and \(x = 9\). Since the parabola opens upwards, the expression is less than or equal to zero between the roots, so \(-1 \le x \le 9\).

steps.title: (d) Solve differential equation
steps.bodyLatex: Separate variables: \(\int \frac{1}{y} dy = \int x dx\). Integrating gives \(\ln y = \frac{x^2}{2} + C\). Exponentiating both sides yields \(y = e^{\frac{x^2}{2} + C}\).

steps.title: (e) Differentiate
steps.bodyLatex: Using the chain rule for \(f(x) = \arcsin(u)\) where \(u = x^5\), we have \(f'(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-u^2}} \cdot \frac{du}{dx} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(x^5)^2}} \cdot 5x^4 = \frac{5x^4}{\sqrt{1-x^{10}}}\).

steps.title: (f) Related rates
steps.bodyLatex: Differentiate \(V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3\) with respect to \(t\): \(\frac{dV}{dt} = 4\pi r^2 \frac{dr}{dt}\). Given \(\frac{dV}{dt} = 10\), we have \(10 = 4\pi r^2 \frac{dr}{dt}\), which rearranges to \(\frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{10}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{5}{2\pi r^2}\).

steps.title: (g) Area between curves
steps.bodyLatex: The area is \(\int_0^{\pi/2} (x - \sin x) dx = [\frac{x^2}{2} + \cos x]_0^{\pi/2}\). Evaluating at the boundaries: \((\frac{(\pi/2)^2}{2} + \cos(\pi/2)) - (0 + \cos 0) = \frac{\pi^2}{8} + 0 - 1 = \frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1\).

commonMistakesLatex: In part (c), forgetting to substitute back from u to x.
In part (d), failing to correctly handle the constant of integration when exponentiating.
In part (g), confusing the order of subtraction between the curves or failing to evaluate the trigonometric integral correctly.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (g), the area must be positive. Since \(\pi^2/8 \approx 1.23\), the result \(\approx 0.23\) is positive and reasonable.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply vector dot products, volumes of revolution, normal approximation of binomial distributions, mathematical induction, and solving absolute value inequalities.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For each part, we use standard techniques: dot product equals zero for perpendicular vectors, the disk method for volumes, Z-scores for normal approximations, the inductive step for proofs, and case analysis for absolute values.

steps.title: (a) Perpendicular Vectors
steps.bodyLatex: Two vectors are perpendicular if their dot product is zero: \( a^2(a+5) + 2(a-4) = 0 \). Expanding this gives the cubic equation \( a^3 + 5a^2 + 2a - 8 = 0 \). By testing values, \( a=1 \) is a root. Dividing the cubic by \( (a-1) \) yields \( (a-1)(a^2+6a+8) = 0 \), which factors further to \( (a-1)(a+2)(a+4) = 0 \).

steps.title: (b) Volume of Revolution
steps.bodyLatex: The volume is given by \( V = \pi \int_{1}^{2} y^2 dx = \pi \int_{1}^{2} (x^3)^2 dx = \pi \int_{1}^{2} x^6 dx \). Evaluating the integral: \( \pi \left[ \frac{x^7}{7} \right]_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{7}(2^7 - 1^7) = \frac{127\pi}{7} \).

steps.title: (c) Normal Approximation
steps.bodyLatex: With \( n=100, p=0.31 \), the mean is \( \mu = 31 \) and standard deviation \( \sigma = \sqrt{100 \times 0.31 \times 0.69} = \sqrt{21.39} \approx 4.625 \). To find \( P(X \ge 35) \), we calculate the Z-score: \( z = \frac{35-31}{4.625} \approx 0.865 \). Using standard normal tables, \( P(Z \ge 0.865) \approx 1 - 0.8065 = 0.1935 \) (or \( 0.1949 \) depending on table precision).

steps.title: (d) Mathematical Induction
steps.bodyLatex: Base case: For \( n=1 \), \( 2^3 + 13 = 21 \), which is divisible by 7. Assume \( 2^{3k} + 13 = 7M \) for some integer \( M \). For \( n=k+1 \), \( 2^{3(k+1)} + 13 = 8(2^{3k}) + 13 = 8(7M - 13) + 13 = 56M - 104 + 13 = 56M - 91 = 7(8M - 13) \). Since this is a multiple of 7, the statement holds for \( n=k+1 \).

steps.title: (e) Absolute Value Inequality
steps.bodyLatex: We solve \( \frac{x}{6} \ge \frac{1}{|x-5|} \) by considering two cases. For \( x > 5 \), \( x(x-5) \ge 6 \implies x^2-5x-6 \ge 0 \implies (x-6)(x+1) \ge 0 \), giving \( x \ge 6 \). For \( x < 5 \), \( x(5-x) \ge 6 \implies -x^2+5x-6 \ge 0 \implies x^2-5x+6 \le 0 \implies (x-2)(x-3) \le 0 \), giving \( 2 \le x \le 3 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to check both cases when solving absolute value inequalities.
Errors in algebraic manipulation during the inductive step.
Incorrectly calculating the standard deviation for the binomial distribution.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a), substitute \( a=1 \) into vectors: \( (1, 2) \cdot (6, -3) = 6 - 6 = 0 \). For (e), test \( x=2.5 \): \( 2.5/6 \approx 0.41 \), \( 1/|2.5-5| = 1/2.5 = 0.4 \). Since \( 0.41 \ge 0.4 \), the interval [2, 3] is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret logistic differential equations, perform trigonometric integration, apply vector projections, and use substitution in calculus.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), use the properties of logistic growth and equilibrium solutions. For (b), use double-angle identities. For (c), use the projection formula \( \text{proj}_{\underset{\sim}{a}} \underset{\sim}{x} = \frac{\underset{\sim}{x} \cdot \underset{\sim}{a}}{|\underset{\sim}{a}|^2} \underset{\sim}{a} \). For (d), use the provided substitution to transform the integral into a standard arctan form.

steps.title: (a) Logistic Growth Analysis
steps.bodyLatex: (i) The differential equation has equilibrium solutions at \( P=0 \) and \( P=2000 \). Since \( S \) is below the horizontal asymptote \( P=2000 \) and \( T \) is above it, the solution curve through \( S \) is bounded by these asymptotes and cannot cross them, thus it cannot reach \( T \). (ii) Sketch a curve starting at \( S \) that is concave up, increasing, and approaches the asymptote \( P=2000 \) as \( t \to \infty \). (iii) The rate of growth \( \frac{dP}{dt} = 2000P - P^2 \) is a parabola in \( P \). The maximum occurs at the vertex, where \( \frac{d}{dP}(\frac{dP}{dt}) = 2000 - 2P = 0 \), giving \( P = 1000 \).

steps.title: (b) Trigonometric Integration
steps.bodyLatex: (i) \( \cos^4 x + \sin^4 x = (\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x)^2 - 2\sin^2 x \cos^2 x = 1 - \frac{1}{2}(2\sin x \cos x)^2 = 1 - \frac{1}{2}\sin^2 2x = 1 - \frac{1}{2}(\frac{1 - \cos 4x}{2}) = \frac{3 + \cos 4x}{4} \). Alternatively, using \( \sin^2 2x = 1 - \cos^2 2x \), we get \( 1 - \frac{1}{2}(1 - \cos^2 2x) = \frac{1 + \cos^2 2x}{2} \). (ii) \( \int_0^{\pi/4} \frac{1 + \cos^2 2x}{2} dx = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^{\pi/4} (1 + \frac{1 + \cos 4x}{2}) dx = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^{\pi/4} (\frac{3}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\cos 4x) dx = [\frac{3x}{4} + \frac{1}{16}\sin 4x]_0^{\pi/4} = \frac{3\pi}{16} \).

steps.title: (c) Vector Projections
steps.bodyLatex: Let \( \underset{\sim}{x} = \begin{pmatrix} u \\ v \end{pmatrix} \). The projection of \( \underset{\sim}{x} \) onto \( \underset{\sim}{a} \) is \( \frac{\underset{\sim}{x} \cdot \underset{\sim}{a}}{|\underset{\sim}{a}|^2} \underset{\sim}{a} = k \underset{\sim}{a} \), so \( \frac{u + 3v}{10} = k \implies u + 3v = 10k \). Similarly, for \( \underset{\sim}{b} \), \( \frac{2u - v}{5} = p \implies 2u - v = 5p \). Solving this system: multiply the second by 3 to get \( 6u - 3v = 15p \), add to the first to get \( 7u = 10k + 15p \implies u = \frac{10k + 15p}{7} \). Substituting back gives \( v = \frac{20k - 5p}{7} \), so \( \underset{\sim}{x} = \frac{5}{7} \begin{pmatrix} 2k + 3p \\ 4k - p \end{pmatrix} \).

steps.title: (d) Integration by Substitution
steps.bodyLatex: Given \( u = e^x + 2e^{-x} \), then \( \frac{du}{dx} = e^x - 2e^{-x} \). Note that \( u^2 = e^{2x} + 4 + 4e^{-2x} = \frac{e^{4x} + 4e^{2x} + 4}{e^{2x}} \). The integrand is \( \frac{e^x(e^{2x} - 2)}{e^{4x} + 8e^{2x} + 4} \). Using the substitution, the integral simplifies to \( \int \frac{du}{u^2 + 4} = \frac{1}{2} \tan^{-1}(\frac{u}{2}) + C = \frac{1}{2} \tan^{-1}(\frac{e^x + 2e^{-x}}{2}) + C \).

commonMistakesLatex: In (a)(iii), differentiating with respect to t instead of P.
In (b)(ii), failing to use the double angle identity for \( \cos^2 2x \) correctly.
In (c), confusing the projection vector \( k\underset{\sim}{a} \) with the scalar component \( k \).
In (d), incorrectly identifying the derivative of the substitution \( u \).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (c), test with \(\underset{\sim}{x} = \underset{\sim}{a} \implies k=1, p=0.4\). The formula yields \(\frac{5}{7} \begin{pmatrix} 2+1.2 \ 4-0.4 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{5}{7} \begin{pmatrix} 3.2 \ 3.6 \end{pmatrix} \neq \begin{pmatrix} 1 \ 3 \end{pmatrix} \dots \text{Wait, verify the projection scalar definition: } k = \frac{\underset{\sim}{x} \cdot \underset{\sim}{a}}{|\underset{\sim}{a}|^2}.\)

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to solve a first-order differential equation, determine the conditions for a function to be invertible, solve inverse trigonometric equations, and apply calculus to kinematic distance functions.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), we use separation of variables. For (b), we use the condition that a function is invertible if it is strictly monotonic (derivative does not change sign). For (c), we use the compound angle formula for tangent. For (d), we analyze the derivative of the squared distance function to show it is always positive.

steps.title: (a) Solve the differential equation
steps.bodyLatex: Separating variables gives \( e^{-y} dy = e^x dx \). Integrating both sides yields \( -e^{-y} = e^x + C \). Since the graph passes through (0,0), we have \( -e^0 = e^0 + C \), so \( C = -2 \). Thus, \( e^{-y} = 2 - e^x \), which leads to \( y = -\ln(2 - e^x) \).

steps.title: (a) Determine Domain and Range
steps.bodyLatex: For the logarithm to be defined, \( 2 - e^x > 0 \), so \( e^x < 2 \), giving the domain \( x < \ln 2 \). As \( x \to -\infty \), \( e^x \to 0 \), so \( y \to -\ln 2 \). As \( x \to \ln 2 \), \( 2 - e^x \to 0^+ \), so \( y \to \infty \). The range is \( (-\ln 2, \infty) \).

steps.title: (b) Find values of k for an inverse
steps.bodyLatex: A function has an inverse if it is strictly monotonic. We find \( f'(x) = \frac{k(1+x^2) - kx(2x)}{(1+x^2)^2} + \frac{1}{1+x^2} = \frac{k+1 + (1-k)x^2}{(1+x^2)^2} \). For \( f'(x) \ge 0 \), we need \( k+1 + (1-k)x^2 \ge 0 \). This holds for all \( x \) if \( k+1 \ge 0 \) and \( 1-k \ge 0 \), resulting in \( -1 \le k \le 1 \).

steps.title: (c)(i) Explain existence of one solution
steps.bodyLatex: Let \( g(x) = \tan^{-1}(3x) + \tan^{-1}(10x) \). Since \( g'(x) = \frac{3}{1+9x^2} + \frac{10}{1+100x^2} > 0 \), \( g(x) \) is strictly increasing. As the range of \( g(x) \) is \( (-\pi, \pi) \), the horizontal line \( y = \theta \) must intersect the graph exactly once.

steps.title: (c)(ii) Solve the equation
steps.bodyLatex: Taking the tangent of both sides: \( \tan(\tan^{-1}(3x) + \tan^{-1}(10x)) = \tan(3\pi/4) = -1 \). Using the identity \( \tan(A+B) = \frac{\tan A + \tan B}{1 - \tan A \tan B} \), we get \( \frac{3x + 10x}{1 - 30x^2} = -1 \). This simplifies to \( 13x = -1 + 30x^2 \), or \( 30x^2 - 13x - 1 = 0 \). Factoring gives \( (15x + 1)(2x - 1) = 0 \), so \( x = 1/2 \) (as \( x = -1/15 \) yields a negative sum).

steps.title: (d) Show distance is increasing
steps.bodyLatex: Let \( S = D^2 = (Vt \cos \theta)^2 + (Vt \sin \theta - \frac{gt^2}{2})^2 \). Expanding, \( S = V^2t^2 \cos^2 \theta + V^2t^2 \sin^2 \theta - Vgt^3 \sin \theta + \frac{g^2t^4}{4} = V^2t^2 - Vgt^3 \sin \theta + \frac{g^2t^4}{4} \). The derivative \( \frac{dS}{dt} = 2V^2t - 3Vgt^2 \sin \theta + g^2t^3 = t(g^2t^2 - 3Vgt \sin \theta + 2V^2) \). For \( D(t) \) to increase, \( \frac{dS}{dt} > 0 \), which requires the quadratic \( g^2t^2 - 3Vgt \sin \theta + 2V^2 > 0 \). The discriminant \( \Delta = (3Vg \sin \theta)^2 - 4(g^2)(2V^2) = V^2g^2(9 \sin^2 \theta - 8) < 0 \) when \( \sin^2 \theta < 8/9 \), i.e., \( \theta < \sin^{-1}(\sqrt{8/9}) \).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the constant of integration in part (a).
Failing to check both the monotonic increasing and decreasing cases in part (b).
Assuming the quadratic in part (d) has no roots without checking the discriminant.
Incorrectly applying the inverse tangent addition formula.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of exponential functions in the context of physical modelling, specifically identifying the initial value of a function.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: The initial temperature occurs at the start of the time interval, which corresponds to \(t = 0\). Simply substitute \(t = 0\) into the given function \(T(t) = 15 + 4e^{-3t}\) to solve for \(T\).

steps.title: Identify the initial time
steps.bodyLatex: The initial temperature is the temperature at time \(t = 0\) seconds.

steps.title: Substitute t = 0 into the equation
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute \(t = 0\) into the model \(T(t) = 15 + 4e^{-3t}\) to get \(T(0) = 15 + 4e^{-3(0)}\).

steps.title: Evaluate the expression
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(-3 \times 0 = 0\) and any non-zero number raised to the power of \(0\) is \(1\), we have \(e^{0} = 1\). Therefore, \(T(0) = 15 + 4(1) = 15 + 4 = 19\).

commonMistakesLatex: Mistaking the constant term \(15\) as the initial temperature, forgetting that the exponential term is not zero when \(t=0\).
Incorrectly evaluating \(e^{0}\) as \(0\) instead of \(1\).
Confusing the coefficient \(4\) with the initial value.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by checking the limit as \(t \to \infty\), which is \(15\). Since the temperature is cooling, the initial temperature must be higher than the ambient temperature of \(15\), confirming that \(19\) is the logical result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of sample proportions in a binomial distribution context.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We identify the number of successful trials \(X\) and relate it to the sample proportion \( \hat{p} = \frac{X}{n} \). By substituting the given values into the inequality, we can match the condition to the correct option.

steps.title: Define the variables
steps.bodyLatex: The number of trials is \(n = 12\). Let \(X\) be the number of rolls resulting in a \(2\). The sample proportion is defined as \( \hat{p} = \frac{X}{n} = \frac{X}{12} \).

steps.title: Translate the condition
steps.bodyLatex: The question asks for the probability that at least \(9\) rolls result in a \(2\), which is written as \(P(X \ge 9)\).

steps.title: Convert to sample proportion
steps.bodyLatex: To express this in terms of \( \hat{p} \), we divide the inequality \(X \ge 9\) by \(n = 12\): \[ \frac{X}{12} \ge \frac{9}{12} \] This simplifies to \( \hat{p} \ge \frac{3}{4} \).

steps.title: Identify the correct option
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing our result to the given options, we find that \(P(\hat{p} \ge \frac{3}{4})\) matches option A.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the number of successes \(X\) with the sample proportion \( \hat{p} \).
Incorrectly simplifying the fraction \( \frac{9}{12} \) to something other than \( \frac{3}{4} \).
Misinterpreting the phrase 'at least' as 'less than' or 'greater than' without the equality.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since \(n=12\), if \( \hat{p} = \frac{3}{4} \), then \(X = 12 \times \frac{3}{4} = 9\). Thus, \( \hat{p} \ge \frac{3}{4} \) is equivalent to \(X \ge 9\), which confirms the result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret a direction field by sketching the trajectory of a particular solution curve.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Start at the point \((-2, 1)\) and follow the slope markers (the small line segments) as you move towards the \(y\)-axis (where \(x=0\)). By carefully tracing the path that remains tangent to these markers, you can estimate the \(y\)-intercept.

steps.title: Identify the starting point
steps.bodyLatex: Locate the point \((-2, 1)\) on the provided grid. This is where our solution curve begins.

steps.title: Trace the trajectory
steps.bodyLatex: Move to the right from \(x = -2\) towards \(x = 0\). At \(x = -2\), the slope is slightly positive. As you move towards \(x = -1\), the slope increases, causing the curve to rise. By the time you reach \(x = 0\), the curve has climbed above the \(y = 1\) level.

steps.title: Estimate the y-intercept
steps.bodyLatex: Looking at the grid, the curve passes through \(x = -1\) at approximately \(y = 1.2\). Continuing to follow the slope markers towards \(x = 0\), the curve reaches a height clearly above \(1.5\) but below \(1.6\). Comparing this to the given options, \(1.56\) is the most accurate estimate.

commonMistakesLatex: Misinterpreting the slope markers by drawing a straight line instead of following the curvature.
Losing track of the path due to the density of the direction field markers.
Rushing the estimation and choosing an extreme value like 1.12 or 1.78.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Visually verify that the slope of your sketched curve matches the orientation of the segments it passes through at each step from \(x = -2\) to \(x = 0\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate the area between two curves using definite integrals.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: The area between two curves \(y=f(x)\) and \(y=g(x)\) from \(x=a\) to \(x=c\) is given by the integral of the upper function minus the lower function. Since \(f(x) > g(x)\) for the entire interval \([a, c]\), the area is \(\int_{a}^{c} (f(x) - g(x)) \, dx\).

steps.title: Set up the integral for the area
steps.bodyLatex: The area \(A\) between the curves is given by \[ A = \int_{a}^{c} (f(x) - g(x)) \, dx \]

steps.title: Split the integral
steps.bodyLatex: Using the linearity of integrals, we can rewrite the expression as \[ A = \int_{a}^{c} f(x) \, dx - \int_{a}^{c} g(x) \, dx \]

steps.title: Evaluate the integrals
steps.bodyLatex: We are given \(\int_{a}^{c} f(x) \, dx = 10\). For \(g(x)\), we use the interval addition property: \(\int_{a}^{c} g(x) \, dx = \int_{a}^{b} g(x) \, dx + \int_{b}^{c} g(x) \, dx = -2 + 3 = 1\).

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the values back into the area formula: \(A = 10 - 1 = 9\). This corresponds to option C.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the definite integral (which can be negative) with the geometric area (which must be positive).
Failing to combine the two parts of the integral for \(g(x)\) correctly by ignoring the signs.
Subtracting the integral values in the wrong order.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by checking the logic: if the total area under \(f\) is \(10\) and the net area under \(g\) is \(1\), the difference is indeed \(9\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the restricted domain of the inverse sine function, specifically the range of \(y = \sin^{-1}(x)\), which is \(-\frac{\pi}{2} \leq y \leq \frac{\pi}{2}\).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Since the input \(a\) lies outside the principal range of the inverse sine function, we use the identity \(\sin(a) = \sin(\pi - a)\) or the symmetry properties of the unit circle to find an angle within the range \([-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}]\) that has the same sine value.

steps.title: Identify the range of the inverse sine function
steps.bodyLatex: The function \(f(a) = \sin^{-1}(\sin a)\) returns a value in the interval \([-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}]\). Given \(\pi < a < \frac{3\pi}{2}\), the angle \(a\) is in the third quadrant.

steps.title: Use trigonometric identities
steps.bodyLatex: In the third quadrant, \(\sin(a)\) is negative. We know that \(\sin(a) = \sin(\pi - a)\). If we test the range for \(\pi - a\), since \(\pi < a < \frac{3\pi}{2}\), then \(-\frac{\pi}{2} < \pi - a < 0\). This value falls perfectly within the required range \([-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}]\).

steps.title: Evaluate the expression
steps.bodyLatex: Because \(\pi - a\) is within the principal range of the inverse sine function, \(\sin^{-1}(\sin a) = \sin^{-1}(\sin(\pi - a)) = \pi - a\).

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming that \(\sin^{-1}(\sin a) = a\) for all values of \(a\), ignoring the restricted domain.
Selecting \(a - \pi\) because it looks similar to the identity, without checking if it lies within the range \([-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}]\).
Failing to account for the negative sign of the sine function in the third quadrant.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Pick a value in the interval, such as \(a = \frac{5\pi}{4}\). Then \(\sin(\frac{5\pi}{4}) = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\). The inverse sine of \(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\) is \(-\frac{\pi}{4}\). Checking option B: \(\pi - \frac{5\pi}{4} = -\frac{\pi}{4}\), which matches.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the properties of vector projections and how scaling the input vectors affects the resulting projection vector.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Use the standard formula for the projection of a vector \( \underset{\sim}{a} \) onto \( \underset{\sim}{b} \), which is \( \text{proj}_{\underset{\sim}{b}} \underset{\sim}{a} = \left( \frac{\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b}}{|\underset{\sim}{b}|^2} \right) \underset{\sim}{b} = \underset{\sim}{c} \). We will substitute the scaled vectors into this formula to see how the result relates to \( \underset{\sim}{c} \).

steps.title: Define the original projection
steps.bodyLatex: We are given that \( \underset{\sim}{c} = \text{proj}_{\underset{\sim}{b}} \underset{\sim}{a} = \left( \frac{\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b}}{|\underset{\sim}{b}|^2} \right) \underset{\sim}{b} \).

steps.title: Calculate the new projection
steps.bodyLatex: We want to find the projection of \( 10\underset{\sim}{a} \) onto \( 2\underset{\sim}{b} \). Using the formula, this is \( \text{proj}_{2\underset{\sim}{b}} (10\underset{\sim}{a}) = \left( \frac{(10\underset{\sim}{a}) \cdot (2\underset{\sim}{b})}{|2\underset{\sim}{b}|^2} \right) (2\underset{\sim}{b}) \).

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.bodyLatex: Simplify the numerator and denominator: \( \frac{20(\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b})}{|2\underset{\sim}{b}|^2} (2\underset{\sim}{b}) = \frac{20(\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b})}{4|\underset{\sim}{b}|^2} (2\underset{\sim}{b}) \). This simplifies to \( 5 \left( \frac{\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b}}{|\underset{\sim}{b}|^2} \right) (2\underset{\sim}{b}) = 10 \left( \frac{\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b}}{|\underset{\sim}{b}|^2} \right) \underset{\sim}{b} \).

steps.title: Relate back to the original vector
