Since \( \sin\theta \cos\theta = \frac{1}{2}\sin(2\theta) \), this becomes \( \int_0^{\pi/2} (\frac{1}{2}\sin(2\theta))^{2n} \sin(2\theta) \, d\theta = \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^{2n+1}(2\theta) \, d\theta \). Letting \( \phi = 2\theta \), the integral becomes \( \frac{1}{2^{2n+1}} \int_0^{\pi} \sin^{2n+1}\phi \, d\phi = \frac{2}{2^{2n+1}} \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^{2n+1}\phi \, d\phi = \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^{2n+1}\theta \, d\theta \).

steps.title: (a)(iii) Recurrence Relation
steps.bodyLatex: From (ii), \( I_n = \frac{1}{2^{2n}} J_{2n+1} \). Using the reduction formula \( J_{2n+1} = \frac{2n}{2n+1} J_{2n-1} \), we have \( I_n = \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \cdot \frac{2n}{2n+1} J_{2n-1} \). Since \( I_{n-1} = \frac{1}{2^{2n-2}} J_{2n-1} \), we substitute \( J_{2n-1} = 2^{2n-2} I_{n-1} \). Thus, \( I_n = \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \cdot \frac{2n}{2n+1} \cdot 2^{2n-2} I_{n-1} = \frac{2n}{4(2n+1)} I_{n-1} = \frac{n}{4n+2} I_{n-1} \).

steps.title: (b)(i) Vector Geometry
steps.bodyLatex: We have \( \vec{LP} = \vec{LB} + \vec{BC} + \vec{CP} \). Since \( L \) is the midpoint of \( AB \), \( \vec{LB} = \frac{1}{2}\vec{AB} = \frac{1}{2}\underset{\sim}{b} \). Since \( P \) is the midpoint of \( CD \), \( \vec{CP} = \frac{1}{2}\vec{CD} = \frac{1}{2}(\vec{AD} - \vec{AC}) = \frac{1}{2}(\underset{\sim}{d} - \underset{\sim}{c}) \). Thus, \( \vec{LP} = \frac{1}{2}\underset{\sim}{b} + (\underset{\sim}{c} - \underset{\sim}{b}) + \frac{1}{2}(\underset{\sim}{d} - \underset{\sim}{c}) = \frac{1}{2}\underset{\sim}{b} - \underset{\sim}{b} + \underset{\sim}{c} - \frac{1}{2}\underset{\sim}{c} + \frac{1}{2}\underset{\sim}{d} = \frac{1}{2}(-\underset{\sim}{b} + \underset{\sim}{c} + \underset{\sim}{d}) \).

steps.title: (b)(ii) Proof of Identity
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate \( 4|\vec{LP}|^2 = |-\underset{\sim}{b} + \underset{\sim}{c} + \underset{\sim}{d}|^2 \). Similarly, \( 4|\vec{MQ}|^2 = |\underset{\sim}{b} - \underset{\sim}{c} + \underset{\sim}{d}|^2 \) and \( 4|\vec{NR}|^2 = |\underset{\sim}{b} + \underset{\sim}{c} - \underset{\sim}{d}|^2 \). Summing these and expanding using dot products \( |\vec{u} + \vec{v} + \vec{w}|^2 = |\vec{u}|^2 + |\vec{v}|^2 + |\vec{w}|^2 + 2(\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} + \vec{v}\cdot\vec{w} + \vec{w}\cdot\vec{u}) \), the cross terms cancel out, leaving \( 3(|\underset{\sim}{b}|^2 + |\underset{\sim}{c}|^2 + |\underset{\sim}{d}|^2) \). By comparing the expansion of the RHS \( |\vec{AB}|^2 + |\vec{AC}|^2 + |\vec{AD}|^2 + |\vec{BC}|^2 + |\vec{BD}|^2 + |\vec{CD}|^2 \), we confirm the equality.

steps.title: (c) Parametric Equations
steps.bodyLatex: The particle moves from \( z = -3 \) to \( z = 3 \), so let \( z = t \) where \( t \in [-3, 3] \). The radius of the cross-section at height \( t \) is \( r = \sqrt{9-t^2} \). The spiral makes 3 full rotations over the interval \( t \in [-3, 3] \), which is a length of 6, so the angle is \( \pi t \). Thus, \( x = \sqrt{9-t^2} \cos(\pi t) \) and \( y = \sqrt{9-t^2} \sin(\pi t) \) (or similar depending on orientation).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (c) \( x = \sqrt{9-t^2}\cos(\pi t), \, y = \sqrt{9-t^2}\sin(\pi t), \, z = t \)
commonMistakesLatex: In (a)(i), failing to correctly apply the integration by parts formula or sign errors.
In (a)(ii), forgetting the factor of 2 when changing the limits of integration from \( [0, \pi] \) to \( [0, \pi/2] \).
In (b)(i), incorrectly defining the vector path by mixing up the order of points.
In (c), struggling to relate the radius of the sphere to the function \( g(x) = \sqrt{9-x^2} \).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a)(i), test \( n=2 \): \( J_2 = \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^2\theta \, d\theta = \pi/4 \). Formula: \( J_2 = \frac{1}{2} J_0 = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^{\pi/2} 1 \, d\theta = \pi/4 \). It matches.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to manipulate complex numbers in geometric contexts, apply calculus to prove inequalities, and interpret complex arguments geometrically.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For complex geometry, use the property that rotating a vector by \( \frac{2\pi}{3} \) is equivalent to multiplying by \( w = e^{\frac{2i\pi}{3}} \). For inequalities, define a function \( f(x) = x - \ln x \) and use its minimum to prove the result, then extend this to products using logarithms.

steps.title: (a)(i) Show 1 + w + w^2 = 0
steps.bodyLatex: Since \( w = e^{\frac{2i\pi}{3}} \), we have \( w^3 = e^{2i\pi} = 1 \). Using the identity for the difference of two cubes, \( w^3 - 1 = (w - 1)(1 + w + w^2) = 0 \). Since \( w \neq 1 \), it must be that \( 1 + w + w^2 = 0 \).

steps.title: (a)(ii) Show a + bw + cw^2 = 0 for anticlockwise equilateral triangle
steps.bodyLatex: In an anticlockwise equilateral triangle, the vector \( \vec{AC} = c - a \) is the vector \( \vec{AB} = b - a \) rotated by \( \frac{2\pi}{3} \). Thus, \( c - a = w(b - a) \). Rearranging gives \( c - a = wb - wa \), or \( a(w - 1) + bw - c = 0 \). Multiplying by \( -w \) and using \( w^3 = 1 \) and \( 1 + w + w^2 = 0 \), we obtain \( a + bw + cw^2 = 0 \).

steps.title: (a)(iii) Show a^2 + b^2 + c^2 = ab + bc + ca
steps.bodyLatex: An equilateral triangle is either anticlockwise or clockwise, so the product of the two conditions must be zero: \( (a + bw + cw^2)(a + bw^2 + cw) = 0 \). Expanding this product gives \( a^2 + abw^2 + acw + abw + b^2w^3 + bcw^2 + acw^2 + bcw^4 + c^2w^3 = 0 \). Using \( w^3 = 1 \), \( w^4 = w \), and \( w + w^2 = -1 \), this simplifies to \( a^2 + b^2 + c^2 - (ab + bc + ca) = 0 \), which is the required result.

steps.title: (b)(i) Prove x > ln x
steps.bodyLatex: Let \( f(x) = x - \ln x \) for \( x > 0 \). The derivative is \( f'(x) = 1 - \frac{1}{x} \). Setting \( f'(x) = 0 \) gives a minimum at \( x = 1 \), where \( f(1) = 1 \). Since the minimum value is \( 1 > 0 \), it follows that \( x - \ln x > 0 \), so \( x > \ln x \).

steps.title: (b)(ii) Prove e^{n^2+n} > (n!)^2
steps.bodyLatex: From (b)(i), \( k > \ln k \) for all integers \( k \ge 1 \). Summing from \( k=1 \) to \( n \), we get \( \sum_{k=1}^n k > \sum_{k=1}^n \ln k \), which is \( \frac{n(n+1)}{2} > \ln(n!) \). Multiplying by \( 2 \), we have \( n^2 + n > 2 \ln(n!) = \ln((n!)^2) \). Exponentiating both sides yields \( e^{n^2+n} > (n!)^2 \).

steps.title: (c) Sketch the region
steps.bodyLatex: Let \( \theta = \text{Arg}(z/w) \). The expression is \( x + y(w/z) \). Since \( |z| = |w| = 1 \), \( w/z = e^{-i\theta} = \cos \theta - i \sin \theta \). The condition \( \frac{\pi}{2} < \text{Arg}(x + y(\cos \theta - i \sin \theta)) < \pi \) implies the real part \( x + y \cos \theta < 0 \) and imaginary part \( -y \sin \theta > 0 \). Given \( \frac{\pi}{2} < \theta < \pi \), \( \sin \theta > 0 \), so \( y < 0 \). The real part condition becomes \( x < -y \cos \theta \), which rearranges to \( y > -\sec \theta x \).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: The region is defined by \( y > -\sec(\theta)x \) and \( y < 0 \).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to use the property \( w^3 = 1 \) in algebraic expansions.
Incorrectly identifying the rotation direction for clockwise vs anticlockwise triangles.
Failing to justify the minimum of the function in (b)(i) before concluding the inequality.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret geometric regions in the complex plane defined by inequalities involving the real part and the argument of a complex number \(z\).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We analyze the two given inequalities separately: the vertical strip defined by \(1 < \text{Re}(z) \le 3\) and the angular sector defined by \( \frac{\pi}{6} \le \text{Arg}(z) < \frac{\pi}{3} \). We then identify the intersection of these two regions, paying close attention to whether the boundaries are inclusive (solid line) or exclusive (dashed line).

steps.title: Analyze the real part inequality
steps.bodyLatex: The condition \(1 < \text{Re}(z) \le 3\) defines a vertical strip between the lines \(x=1\) and \(x=3\). Because the inequality is strict at \(x=1\), the boundary at \(x=1\) must be represented by a dashed line. Because the inequality is inclusive at \(x=3\), the boundary at \(x=3\) must be represented by a solid line.

steps.title: Analyze the argument inequality
steps.bodyLatex: The condition \( \frac{\pi}{6} \le \text{Arg}(z) < \frac{\pi}{3} \) defines a sector originating from the origin. The lower boundary \( \text{Arg}(z) = \frac{\pi}{6} \) is inclusive (solid line), while the upper boundary \( \text{Arg}(z) = \frac{\pi}{3} \) is exclusive (dashed line).

steps.title: Combine the conditions
steps.bodyLatex: We look for the region that satisfies both conditions simultaneously. The boundary at \(x=1\) is dashed (from the real part) and the boundary at \(x=3\) is solid (from the real part). The lower angular boundary is solid and the upper angular boundary is dashed. Matching these properties to the provided diagrams, only diagram A correctly displays the dashed line at \(x=1\) and the upper angular boundary, and the solid line at \(x=3\) and the lower angular boundary.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing strict inequalities (represented by dashed lines) with inclusive inequalities (represented by solid lines).
Misinterpreting the boundaries of the angular sector relative to the vertical strip.
Failing to check all four boundaries (two vertical lines and two rays) against the given inequalities.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify that the point \(z = 2 + i\sqrt{3}\) lies in the region: \(\text{Re}(z) = 2\) (between 1 and 3) and \(\text{Arg}(z) = \arctan(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}) \approx 40.9^\circ\), which is between \(30^\circ\) and \(60^\circ\). This confirms the region is correctly identified.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests the understanding of algebraic implications and the property that if \(x^2 = y^2\), then \(x = \pm y\).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We evaluate each step of the algebraic manipulation to identify where the logic fails. Specifically, we examine the transition from a squared equality to a linear equality.

steps.title: Analyze Line 3
steps.bodyLatex: In Line 3, we have \((a + 2)^2 = 2^2\). This is a correct algebraic statement since \((-4 + 2)^2 = (-2)^2 = 4\) and \(2^2 = 4\).

steps.title: Analyze Line 4
steps.bodyLatex: Line 4 states that if \((a + 2)^2 = 2^2\), then \(a + 2 = 2\). This is an invalid logical implication because the square root of a squared term yields two possibilities: \(a + 2 = 2\) or \(a + 2 = -2\).

steps.title: Identify the error
steps.bodyLatex: By ignoring the negative root \(a + 2 = -2\), the proof forces \(a = 0\) instead of the original value \(a = -4\). Therefore, the error occurs in the transition to Line 4.

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming that \(x^2 = y^2\) implies \(x = y\) without considering the negative case.
Failing to check the validity of the final result against the initial definition of the variable.
Overlooking that algebraic steps must hold for all values, including negative numbers.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Substitute \(a = -4\) into the equation \((a + 2)^2 = 2^2\): \((-4 + 2)^2 = (-2)^2 = 4\), which is correct. However, \(a + 2 = 2\) becomes \(-4 + 2 = 2\), which is \(-2 = 2\), a clear contradiction.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of logical statements, specifically the construction of a contrapositive for a conditional statement involving quantifiers.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: The contrapositive of a statement 'If \(P\), then \(Q\)' is 'If not \(Q\), then not \(P\)'. When dealing with quantifiers, 'there exists' becomes 'for all' and the conclusion is negated.

steps.title: Identify the components of the statement
steps.bodyLatex: The original statement is: 'If \(P\) is on the line \(AB\)' (Condition \(P\), let's call it \(C_1\)), 'then there exists a real number \(\lambda\) such that \(\vec{AP} = \lambda \vec{AB}\)' (Conclusion \(Q\), let's call it \(C_2\)).

steps.title: Apply the rule for negation
steps.bodyLatex:
To form the contrapositive, we negate the conclusion and make it the new condition, then negate the original condition and make it the new conclusion. The negation of 'there exists a \(\lambda\) such that \(\vec{AP} = \lambda \vec{AB}\)' is 'for all \(\lambda\), \(\vec{AP} 
e \lambda \vec{AB}\)'.

steps.title: Construct the contrapositive
steps.bodyLatex:
The new condition is 'If for all real numbers \(\lambda\), \(\vec{AP} 
e \lambda \vec{AB}\)'. The new conclusion is 'then \(P\) is not on the line \(AB\)'.

steps.title: Match with the options
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing our constructed statement to the given options, we see it matches exactly with option B.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the contrapositive with the converse (Option C) or the inverse (Option D).
Failing to correctly negate the existential quantifier 'there exists' into the universal quantifier 'for all'.
Assuming that the negation of an equality is simply the same expression without changing the quantifier.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the logic: If the original statement is true (which it is, as it defines collinearity), the contrapositive must also be logically equivalent and true. Option B correctly states that if no such scalar exists, the point cannot lie on the line.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to identify when a rational function's integral results in a logarithmic function versus other forms like inverse trigonometric functions or simplified rational functions.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We evaluate the integral of each option. We look for cases where the numerator is a multiple of the derivative of the denominator, or where the expression can be simplified by cancelling common factors.

steps.title: Analyze Option A
steps.bodyLatex: For \( \int \frac{x+2}{x^2+4x+5} dx \), note that the derivative of \( x^2+4x+5 \) is \( 2x+4 = 2(x+2) \). Thus, the integral is \( \frac{1}{2} \ln|x^2+4x+5| + C \), which contains a logarithm.

steps.title: Analyze Option B
steps.bodyLatex: For \( \int \frac{x+2}{x^2-4x-5} dx \), we can use partial fractions. Since the denominator factors as \( (x-5)(x+1) \), the integral will result in a sum of logarithms, \( A \ln|x-5| + B \ln|x+1| + C \).

steps.title: Analyze Option C
steps.bodyLatex: Factor the denominator: \( x^3-x^2+x-1 = x^2(x-1) + 1(x-1) = (x^2+1)(x-1) \). The expression becomes \( \frac{x-1}{(x^2+1)(x-1)} = \frac{1}{x^2+1} \). The integral is \( \int \frac{1}{x^2+1} dx = \arctan(x) + C \), which does not contain a logarithm.

steps.title: Analyze Option D
steps.bodyLatex: For \( \int \frac{x+1}{(x^2+1)(x-1)} dx \), partial fractions will yield terms of the form \( \frac{Ax+B}{x^2+1} + \frac{C}{x-1} \). The \( \frac{C}{x-1} \) term integrates to \( C \ln|x-1| \), so it contains a logarithm.

commonMistakesLatex: Failing to factor the cubic denominator in options C and D completely.
Assuming every rational function integral must result in a natural logarithm.
Overlooking the possibility of cancelling common factors before integrating.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Differentiating \( \arctan(x) \) gives \( \frac{1}{x^2+1} \), which matches the simplified form of option C, confirming no logarithm is required.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and the chain rule for integration.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to find the derivative of \( g(x) \), then identify the integrand as the derivative of a composite function.

steps.title: Differentiate the given equation
steps.bodyLatex: Given \( g(x) = \int_{a}^{x} f(t) dt \), by the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, the derivative is \( g'(x) = f(x) \).

steps.title: Substitute into the expression
steps.bodyLatex: We want to find the primitive (integral) of \( f(x)g(x) \). Since \( f(x) = g'(x) \), the expression becomes \( g'(x)g(x) \).

steps.title: Integrate using the chain rule
steps.bodyLatex: Recognising the form \( u'u \), we know that \( \frac{d}{dx} [\frac{1}{2}(g(x))^2] = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2g(x) \cdot g'(x) = g(x)g'(x) \). Therefore, the primitive is \( \frac{1}{2}[g(x)]^2 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the derivative of the integral with the function itself.
Attempting to integrate by parts unnecessarily.
Misapplying the chain rule when differentiating \( [g(x)]^2 \).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Differentiating option C: \( \frac{d}{dx} [\frac{1}{2}(g(x))^2] = g(x)g'(x) = g(x)f(x) \), which matches the original integrand.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the properties of complex numbers, specifically the relationship between a complex number \( z \), its conjugate \( \bar{z} \), and its modulus.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We test each option by substituting \( z = x + iy \) (where \( y \neq 0 \) since \( z \) is not real) and checking if the equation can hold true for non-real values.

steps.title: Evaluate Option A
steps.bodyLatex: Let \( z = x + iy \). Then \( \bar{z} = x - iy \). The equation \( \bar{z} = iz \) becomes \( x - iy = i(x + iy) = ix - y \). Equating real and imaginary parts gives \( x = -y \) and \( -y = x \). This is consistent, meaning \( z = x - ix \) (e.g., \( 1-i \)) is a valid non-real solution.

steps.title: Evaluate Option B
steps.bodyLatex: The right side \( |z^2| \) is \( |z|^2 = x^2 + y^2 \), which is always a real number. If \( \bar{z} = |z|^2 \), then \( \bar{z} \) must be real, implying \( z \) must be real. This contradicts the condition that \( z \) is not real.

steps.title: Evaluate Option C
steps.bodyLatex: Since \( iz = i(x + iy) = ix - y \), we have \( \text{Re}(iz) = -y \) and \( \text{Im}(z) = y \). The equation \( \text{Re}(iz) = \text{Im}(z) \) implies \( -y = y \), so \( y = 0 \). This forces \( z \) to be real, which is excluded.

steps.title: Evaluate Option D
steps.bodyLatex: The equation \( \text{Arg}(z^3) = \text{Arg}(z) \) implies \( 3\text{Arg}(z) = \text{Arg}(z) + 2k\pi \), so \( 2\text{Arg}(z) = 2k\pi \), meaning \( \text{Arg}(z) = 0 \) or \( \pi \). In both cases, \( z \) lies on the real axis, so \( z \) must be real.

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming \( \text{Arg}(z^3) = \text{Arg}(z) \) allows for non-real solutions without considering the full range of the argument.
Forgetting that \( |z^2| \) is always a real number.
Failing to substitute \( z = x + iy \) to rigorously test the constraints on the imaginary part \( y \neq 0 \).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For Option A, let \( z = 1 - i \). Then \( \bar{z} = 1 + i \). Also, \( iz = i(1 - i) = i - i^2 = i + 1 \). Since \( 1 + i = 1 + i \), the solution is confirmed.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of logical statements, specifically the truth values of a conditional statement and its converse.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To determine the truth of a statement, test small values of \(n\). If you find a counterexample, the statement is false. The converse is formed by swapping the hypothesis and the conclusion.

steps.title: Evaluate statement P
steps.bodyLatex: Statement \(P\) claims: If \(n\) is prime, then \(T_n = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\) is prime. Let \(n = 3\). Then \(T_3 = \frac{3(4)}{2} = 6\). Since \(6\) is not prime, statement \(P\) is false.

steps.title: Formulate and evaluate the converse
steps.bodyLatex: The converse is: If \(T_n = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\) is prime, then \(n\) is prime. Let's test values: if \(n=1\), \(T_1 = 1\) (not prime). If \(n=2\), \(T_2 = 3\) (prime, \(n=2\) is prime). If \(n=3\), \(T_3 = 6\) (not prime). If \(n=4\), \(T_4 = 10\) (not prime). If \(n=5\), \(T_5 = 15\) (not prime). For any \(n > 2\), \(T_n = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\) is a triangular number which is composite because it is the product of two integers greater than 1. Thus, the only prime value is \(T_2=3\), where \(n=2\) is indeed prime. Therefore, the converse is true.

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming that because a statement is false, its converse must also be false.
Failing to test enough values to identify the pattern of triangular numbers.
Misidentifying the number 1 as a prime number.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since \(P\) is false due to \(n=3\) and the converse is true because the only prime triangular number is 3 (where \(n=2\)), the choice D is consistent.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to model physical motion using Newton's Second Law, specifically incorporating vector forces for gravity and air resistance.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the vector form of Newton's Second Law, \( \vec{F}_{net} = m\vec{a} \). The net force is the sum of the gravitational force and the air resistance force.

steps.title: Identify the forces
steps.bodyLatex: The gravitational force acts downwards: \( \vec{F}_{g} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ -mg \end{pmatrix} \). The air resistance force acts in the opposite direction to the velocity vector \( \vec{v} = \begin{pmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{pmatrix} \).

steps.title: Model the air resistance
steps.bodyLatex: The magnitude of the air resistance is given as \( kv^2 \). To express this as a vector, we multiply the magnitude by the unit vector in the direction of motion, which is \( \frac{1}{v} \begin{pmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{pmatrix} \). Thus, the force vector is \( -kv^2 \left( \frac{1}{v} \begin{pmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{pmatrix} \right) = -kv \begin{pmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{pmatrix} \).

steps.title: Apply Newton's Second Law
steps.bodyLatex: Combining these forces, we have \( \vec{F}_{net} = \vec{F}_{g} + \vec{F}_{air} = m\vec{a} \). Substituting the vectors gives \( \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ -mg \end{pmatrix} - kv \begin{pmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{pmatrix} = m \begin{pmatrix} \ddot{x} \\ \ddot{y} \end{pmatrix} \).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the magnitude of the force \(kv^2\) with the vector force, which requires dividing by the speed \(v\) to obtain the correct direction.
Forgetting that air resistance must act in the direction opposite to the velocity, requiring a negative sign.
Misinterpreting the proportionality constant k relative to the velocity components.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the units: \( m \ddot{x} \) has units of force (Newtons). The term \( kv \dot{x} \) has units of \( (N s^2/m^2) \cdot (m/s) \cdot (m/s) = N \), which is dimensionally consistent.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the geometric properties of vectors, specifically the locus of points defined by dot products and distance equalities in 3D space.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We identify \(S_1\) as the sphere with diameter \(AB\) and \(S_2\) as a plane perpendicular to \(AM\). By setting up a coordinate system with \(M\) at the origin, we can solve for the intersection of these two surfaces.

steps.title: Identify the locus S1
steps.bodyLatex: The condition \(\vec{AP} \cdot \vec{BP} = 0\) defines a sphere where \(AB\) is the diameter. Let \(M\) be the origin \((0,0,0)\). If we let \(\vec{a} = \vec{MA}\), then \(\vec{A} = \vec{a}\) and \(\vec{B} = -\vec{a}\). The condition becomes \((\vec{P} - \vec{a}) \cdot (\vec{P} + \vec{a}) = 0\), which simplifies to \(|\vec{P}|^2 = |\vec{a}|^2\). Since \(|\vec{a}| = \frac{|\vec{AB}|}{2}\), this is a sphere of radius \(R = \frac{|\vec{AB}|}{2}\) centered at \(M\).

steps.title: Identify the locus S2
steps.bodyLatex: The condition \(|\vec{AN}| = |\vec{MN}|\) defines a plane. Using the same coordinates, \(|\vec{N} - \vec{a}| = |\vec{N} - 0|\). Squaring both sides gives \(|\vec{N}|^2 - 2\vec{N} \cdot \vec{a} + |\vec{a}|^2 = |\vec{N}|^2\). This simplifies to \(2\vec{N} \cdot \vec{a} = |\vec{a}|^2\), or \(\vec{N} \cdot \vec{a} = \frac{|\vec{a}|^2}{2}\).

steps.title: Find the radius of the intersection circle
steps.bodyLatex: The distance \(d\) from the center \(M\) to the plane \(S_2\) is the projection of \(\vec{N}\) onto the unit vector of \(\vec{a}\), which is \(d = \frac{|\vec{a}|^2}{2|\vec{a}|} = \frac{|\vec{a}|}{2}\). Using Pythagoras on the triangle formed by the sphere radius \(R\), the distance \(d\), and the circle radius \(r\), we have \(r^2 = R^2 - d^2\). Substituting \(R = |\vec{a}|\) and \(d = \frac{|\vec{a}|}{2}\), we get \(r^2 = |\vec{a}|^2 - \frac{|\vec{a}|^2}{4} = \frac{3|\vec{a}|^2}{4}\).

steps.title: Final calculation
steps.bodyLatex: Taking the square root, \(r = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} |\vec{a}|\). Since \(|\vec{a}| = \frac{|\vec{AB}|}{2}\), we find \(r = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \frac{|\vec{AB}|}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{3} |\vec{AB}|}{4}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming the intersection of a sphere and a plane through the center, which would result in a great circle.
Misinterpreting the locus \(S_2\) as a sphere rather than a plane.
Algebraic errors when calculating the distance from the origin to the plane \(S_2\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: If \(|\vec{AB}| = 2\), then \(R=1\) and the plane is at distance \(0.5\) from the center. The radius of the circle is \(\sqrt{1^2 - 0.5^2} = \sqrt{0.75} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\). Our formula gives \(\frac{\sqrt{3}(2)}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\), which matches.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of terminal velocity in a resistive medium where the resistive force is proportional to velocity, specifically \( F_r = -kv \).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We model the motion using Newton's Second Law, \( m \ddot{x} = mg - kv \), where the particle experiences gravity and a resistive force. By analyzing the differential equation, we identify the existence of a terminal velocity \( v_t = \frac{mg}{k} \) as the steady-state solution.

steps.title: Model the equation of motion
steps.bodyLatex: Taking the downward direction as positive, the net force is \( F_{net} = mg - kv \). Applying Newton's Second Law, we have \( m \frac{dv}{dt} = mg - kv \).

steps.title: Analyze the terminal velocity
steps.bodyLatex: Terminal velocity occurs when the acceleration is zero, \( \frac{dv}{dt} = 0 \). Setting \( mg - kv = 0 \), we find the terminal velocity is \( v_t = \frac{mg}{k} \), which is a positive constant (downwards).

steps.title: Evaluate the options
steps.bodyLatex: If the particle moves upwards, its velocity \( v \) is negative. The resistive force \( -kv \) acts downwards, assisting gravity. Regardless of the initial direction, the velocity \( v(t) \) will always approach the value \( v_t = \frac{mg}{k} \) as \( t \to \infty \).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the direction of the resistive force with the direction of gravity.
Assuming that terminal velocity can only be reached if the particle starts by moving downwards.
Neglecting that the differential equation \( \frac{dv}{dt} = g - \frac{k}{m}v \) has a unique equilibrium solution regardless of initial conditions.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: As \( t \to \infty \), the term \( e^{-(k/m)t} \) in the general solution \( v(t) = \frac{mg}{k} + Ce^{-(k/m)t} \) approaches \( 0 \), meaning \( v(t) \to \frac{mg}{k} \), confirming that all paths lead to the same terminal velocity.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests proficiency in complex numbers, integration techniques, 3D vector geometry, and coordinate geometry.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We will use algebraic manipulation for complex division, the reverse chain rule for integration, De Moivre's Theorem for powers, the dot product formula for angles, and standard linear forms for lines.

steps.title: (a) Simplify Complex Division
steps.bodyLatex: Multiply the numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator, \(2-i\). This gives \(\frac{(3-i)(2-i)}{(2+i)(2-i)} = \frac{6-3i-2i+i^2}{4-i^2} = \frac{6-5i-1}{4+1} = \frac{5-5i}{5} = 1-i\).

steps.title: (b) Evaluate Integral
steps.bodyLatex: Recognize the reverse chain rule: \(\int \sin^3(2x) \cos(2x) dx\). Let \(u = \sin(2x)\), so \(du = 2 \cos(2x) dx\). The integral becomes \(\frac{1}{2} \int u^3 du = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{u^4}{4} + C = \frac{1}{8} \sin^4(2x) + C\).

steps.title: (c) De Moivre's Theorem
steps.bodyLatex: For (i), the modulus is \(r = \sqrt{(-\sqrt{3})^2 + 1^2} = 2\). The argument \(\theta\) satisfies \(\tan \theta = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\) in the second quadrant, so \(\theta = \frac{5\pi}{6}\). Thus, \(-\sqrt{3}+i = 2e^{i\frac{5\pi}{6}}\). For (ii), \((2e^{i\frac{5\pi}{6}})^{10} = 2^{10} e^{i\frac{50\pi}{6}} = 1024 e^{i\frac{25\pi}{3}}\). Since \(\frac{25\pi}{3} = 8\pi + \frac{\pi}{3}\), this is \(1024(\cos \frac{\pi}{3} + i \sin \frac{\pi}{3}) = 1024(\frac{1}{2} + i\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}) = 512 + 512\sqrt{3}i\).

steps.title: (d) Angle Between Vectors
steps.bodyLatex: Define vectors \(\vec{BA} = (1, -3, 3)\) and \(\vec{BC} = (2, -1, 2)\). The dot product is \(\vec{BA} \cdot \vec{BC} = (1)(2) + (-3)(-1) + (3)(2) = 2 + 3 + 6 = 11\). The magnitudes are \(|\vec{BA}| = \sqrt{1^2+(-3)^2+3^2} = \sqrt{19}\) and \(|\vec{BC}| = \sqrt{2^2+(-1)^2+2^2} = 3\). Thus, \(\cos \angle ABC = \frac{11}{3\sqrt{19}}\), so \(\angle ABC \approx 33^\circ\).

steps.title: (e) Equation of Parallel Line
steps.bodyLatex: The direction vector of \(\ell_1\) is \((3, 2)\), so the slope is \(m = \frac{2}{3}\). Using the point-slope form \(y - 5 = \frac{2}{3}(x - (-6))\), we get \(y - 5 = \frac{2}{3}x + 4\), which simplifies to \(y = \frac{2}{3}x + 9\).

steps.title: (f) Weierstrass Substitution
steps.bodyLatex: Using \(t = \tan \frac{x}{2}\), then \(dx = \frac{2dt}{1+t^2}\), \(\cos x = \frac{1-t^2}{1+t^2}\), and \(\sin x = \frac{2t}{1+t^2}\). Substituting these into the integral gives \(\int \frac{2dt/(1+t^2)}{1 + (1-t^2)/(1+t^2) - 2t/(1+t^2)} = \int \frac{2dt}{1+t^2+1-t^2-2t} = \int \frac{2dt}{2-2t} = \int \frac{dt}{1-t} = -\ln|1-t| + k = -\ln|1-\tan \frac{x}{2}| + k\).

commonMistakesLatex: In (a), failing to correctly handle the sign of i squared in the denominator.
In (c), miscalculating the argument of the complex number or failing to simplify the angle after applying De Moivre's.
In (d), using the wrong vectors (e.g., AB and AC instead of BA and BC) to find the angle at B.
In (f), forgetting the differential term \(dx = 2dt/(1+t^2)\) when performing the substitution.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a), \((1-i)(2+i) = 2 + i - 2i - i^2 = 3 - i\). Matches. For (d), the dot product and magnitudes are verified by standard vector calculation.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your proficiency in algebraic inequalities, kinematics with non-constant acceleration, differential equations involving resistive forces, integration using partial fractions, and properties of complex numbers.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the square of a real number property for the inequality, integrate acceleration to find position, solve a separable differential equation for velocity, decompose rational functions into partial fractions, and apply Euler's form for complex numbers.

steps.title: (a) Proving the Inequality
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(a, b \ge 0\), their square roots are real. Consider the square of the difference: \((\sqrt{a} - \sqrt{b})^2 \ge 0\). Expanding this gives \(a - 2\sqrt{ab} + b \ge 0\), which rearranges to \(a + b \ge 2\sqrt{ab}\). Dividing by \(2\) yields the required result: \(\frac{a+b}{2} \ge \sqrt{ab}\).

steps.title: (b) Kinematics and Maximum Velocity
steps.bodyLatex: Given \(a = \frac{dv}{dt} = 12 - 6t\), integrate with respect to \(t\) to find \(v = 12t - 3t^2 + C\). Since the particle starts from rest at \(t=0\), \(C=0\), so \(v = 12t - 3t^2\). Maximum velocity occurs when \(a = 0\), so \(12 - 6t = 0 \implies t = 2\). Integrating \(v = \frac{dx}{dt}\) gives \(x = 6t^2 - t^3 + C_2\); since \(x(0)=0\), \(C_2=0\). At \(t=2\), \(x = 6(2)^2 - (2)^3 = 24 - 8 = 16\) units.

steps.title: (c) Resistive Force Differential Equation
steps.bodyLatex: For (i), using \(F = ma = m v \frac{dv}{dx}\) with \(m=1\) and \(F = -(v + 3v^2)\), we have \(v \frac{dv}{dx} = -v(1 + 3v)\). Dividing by \(v\) (since \(v \neq 0\)) gives \(\frac{dv}{dx} = -(1 + 3v)\). For (ii), separate variables: \(\int \frac{dv}{1+3v} = -\int dx\). Integrating yields \(\frac{1}{3} \ln(1+3v) = -x + C\). At \(x=0, v=u\), so \(C = \frac{1}{3} \ln(1+3u)\). Rearranging gives \(x = \frac{1}{3} \ln \left( \frac{1+3u}{1+3v} \right)\).

steps.title: (d) Partial Fractions and Integration
steps.bodyLatex: Decompose \(\frac{4+x}{(1-x)(4+x^2)} = \frac{A}{1-x} + \frac{Bx+C}{4+x^2}\). Solving gives \(A=1, B=1, C=0\). The integral becomes \(\int_2^n (\frac{1}{1-x} + \frac{x}{4+x^2}) dx = [-\ln|1-x| + \frac{1}{2}\ln(4+x^2)]_2^n\). Evaluating at the limits: \((-\ln|n-1| + \frac{1}{2}\ln(4+n^2)) - (-\ln|1| + \frac{1}{2}\ln(8))\). Combining logs results in \(\frac{1}{2} \ln \left( \frac{4+n^2}{8(n-1)^2} \right)\).

steps.title: (e) Complex Number Property
steps.bodyLatex: Given \(z = e^{i\theta}\), then \(z^2 = e^{i2\theta}\). Substitute into \(w = \frac{z^2-1}{z^2+1}\) and divide numerator and denominator by \(z\): \(w = \frac{z - 1/z}{z + 1/z} = \frac{e^{i\theta} - e^{-i\theta}}{e^{i\theta} + e^{-i\theta}}\). Using Euler's identities, this simplifies to \(\frac{2i \sin \theta}{2 \cos \theta} = i \tan \theta\), which is purely imaginary.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: See individual parts: (a) Proof; (b) 16 units; (c)(ii) \(x = \frac{1}{3} \ln \left( \frac{1+3u}{1+3v} \right)\); (d) \(\frac{1}{2} \ln \left( \frac{4+n^2}{8(n-1)^2} \right)\); (e) Proof.
commonMistakesLatex: In (b), forgetting that maximum velocity occurs when acceleration is zero, not when velocity is zero.
In (c), failing to use the chain rule form \(a = v \frac{dv}{dx}\) for resistive forces.
In (d), missing the absolute value signs or constant of integration during partial fraction evaluation.
In (e), struggling to simplify the fraction without dividing by \(z\) or using Euler's formula.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (b), check \(x(2)=16\). For (c), differentiate \(x(v)\) to recover the DE. For (d), differentiate the result to see if it matches the integrand.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to construct logical proofs using contrapositive and mathematical induction, and your proficiency in solving complex equations and applying trigonometric identities.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), we use the contrapositive. For (b), we follow the standard steps of mathematical induction. For (c), we use De Moivre's Theorem and the properties of roots of unity to relate complex numbers to trigonometric values.

steps.title: (a) Proof by Contrapositive
steps.bodyLatex: Assume the contrapositive: if \( n \) is even, then \( 2^n - 1 \) is not prime. Since \( n \ge 3 \) and \( n \) is even, let \( n = 2k \) for some integer \( k \ge 2 \). Then \( 2^n - 1 = 2^{2k} - 1 = (2^k)^2 - 1^2 = (2^k - 1)(2^k + 1) \). Since \( k \ge 2 \), both factors are greater than 1, meaning \( 2^n - 1 \) is composite.

steps.title: (b) Proof by Mathematical Induction
steps.bodyLatex: Base case \( n=1 \): \( a_1 = \sqrt{2} = 2\cos(\frac{\pi}{4}) \), which is true. Assume for \( n=k \), \( a_k = 2\cos(\frac{\pi}{2^{k+1}}) \). For \( n=k+1 \), \( a_{k+1}^2 = 2 + 2\cos(\frac{\pi}{2^{k+1}}) \). Using the identity \( 1 + \cos(\theta) = 2\cos^2(\frac{\theta}{2}) \), we get \( a_{k+1}^2 = 2(1 + \cos(\frac{\pi}{2^{k+1}})) = 4\cos^2(\frac{\pi}{2^{k+2}}) \). Taking the positive root, \( a_{k+1} = 2\cos(\frac{\pi}{2^{k+2}}) \), completing the inductive step.

steps.title: (c)(i) Solving the Equation
steps.bodyLatex: The equation \( z^5 = -1 = e^{i(\pi + 2k\pi)} \) gives roots \( z = e^{i(\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{5})} \) for \( k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 \). These are \( z = e^{i\pi/5}, e^{i3\pi/5}, e^{i\pi} = -1, e^{i7\pi/5} = e^{-i3\pi/5}, e^{i9\pi/5} = e^{-i\pi/5} \).

steps.title: (c)(ii) Showing the Quadratic Relation
steps.bodyLatex: Let \( u = z + \frac{1}{z} \). Then \( u^2 - u - 1 = (z + \frac{1}{z})^2 - (z + \frac{1}{z}) - 1 = z^2 + 2 + \frac{1}{z^2} - z - \frac{1}{z} - 1 = \frac{z^4 - z^3 + z^2 - z + 1}{z^2} \). Since \( z^5 + 1 = 0 \), the numerator is the geometric series \( \frac{z^5 + 1}{z + 1} = 0 \), hence \( u^2 - u - 1 = 0 \).

steps.title: (c)(iii) Finding the Exact Value
steps.bodyLatex: For \( z = e^{i3\pi/5} \), \( u = z + \frac{1}{z} = 2\cos(3\pi/5) \). Since \( 3\pi/5 \) is in the second quadrant, \( \cos(3\pi/5) < 0 \). Solving \( u^2 - u - 1 = 0 \) gives \( u = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2} \). Choosing the negative value, \( 2\cos(3\pi/5) = \frac{1 - \sqrt{5}}{2} \), so \( \cos(3\pi/5) = \frac{1 - \sqrt{5}}{4} \).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to check the sign of the cosine value when solving the quadratic in part (c)(iii).
Failing to justify why the positive root is taken in the induction step of part (b).
Incorrectly identifying the roots of unity for negative values.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a), test \( n=4 \): \( 2^4-1 = 15 = 3 \times 5 \), which is not prime. For (c)(iii), \( \cos(108^\circ) \approx -0.309 \), and \( (1 - 2.236)/4 \approx -0.309 \).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to manipulate vector linear combinations in geometry and perform integration by parts to establish properties of a sequence of integrals.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), use the property that non-parallel vectors are linearly independent. For part (b), use integration by parts for the reduction formula and induction to prove the general term.

steps.title: (a)(i) Proof of linear independence
steps.bodyLatex: Assume \( \lambda \vec{u} + \mu \vec{v} = \vec{0} \). If \( \lambda \neq 0 \), then \( \vec{u} = -\frac{\mu}{\lambda} \vec{v} \), which implies \( \vec{u} \) and \( \vec{v} \) are parallel, contradicting the premise. Thus, \( \lambda = 0 \), which leaves \( \mu \vec{v} = \vec{0} \). Since \( \vec{v} \neq \vec{0} \), we must have \( \mu = 0 \).

steps.title: (a)(ii) Equating coefficients
steps.bodyLatex: Given \( \lambda_1 \vec{u} + \mu_1 \vec{v} = \lambda_2 \vec{u} + \mu_2 \vec{v} \), we rearrange to \( (\lambda_1 - \lambda_2) \vec{u} + (\mu_1 - \mu_2) \vec{v} = \vec{0} \). By the result in (i), the coefficients must be zero, so \( \lambda_1 - \lambda_2 = 0 \) and \( \mu_1 - \mu_2 = 0 \), meaning \( \lambda_1 = \lambda_2 \) and \( \mu_1 = \mu_2 \).

steps.title: (a)(iii) Position of L
steps.bodyLatex: Since \( L \) lies on \( SK \), \( \vec{SL} = \alpha \vec{SK} = \frac{\alpha}{4} \vec{SB} + \frac{\alpha}{3} \vec{SC} \). Since \( L \) lies on \( BC \), \( \vec{SL} = (1-\beta) \vec{SB} + \beta \vec{SC} \). Equating coefficients: \( \frac{\alpha}{4} = 1-\beta \) and \( \frac{\alpha}{3} = \beta \). Substituting \( \alpha = 3\beta \) gives \( \frac{3\beta}{4} = 1-\beta \implies \frac{7}{4}\beta = 1 \implies \beta = \frac{4}{7} \). Thus \( \vec{BL} = \frac{4}{7} \vec{BC} \).

steps.title: (a)(iv) Collinearity of P
steps.bodyLatex: Express \( \vec{AL} = \vec{AB} + \vec{BL} = \vec{AB} + \frac{4}{7}(\vec{AC} - \vec{AB}) = \frac{3}{7} \vec{AB} + \frac{4}{7} \vec{AC} \). Given \( \vec{AP} = -6 \vec{AB} - 8 \vec{AC} = -14(\frac{3}{7} \vec{AB} + \frac{4}{7} \vec{AC}) \), we see \( \vec{AP} = -14 \vec{AL} \). Since \( \vec{AP} \) is a scalar multiple of \( \vec{AL} \) and they share point \( A \), \( P \) lies on the line \( AL \).

steps.title: (b)(i)-(iii) Calculus properties
steps.bodyLatex: For (i), \( J_0 = \int_0^1 e^{-x} dx = [-e^{-x}]_0^1 = 1 - e^{-1} \). For (ii), since \( e^{-x} \le 1 \) on \( [0,1] \), \( J_n \le \int_0^1 x^n dx = \frac{1}{n+1} \). For (iii), use parts: \( u=x^n, dv=e^{-x}dx \implies J_n = [-x^n e^{-x}]_0^1 + n \int_0^1 x^{n-1} e^{-x} dx = -\frac{1}{e} + n J_{n-1} \).

steps.title: (b)(iv)-(v) Induction and Limit
steps.bodyLatex: Using the reduction formula, we iterate to find \( J_n = n! - \frac{n!}{e} \sum_{r=0}^n \frac{1}{r!} \). As \( n \to \infty \), \( J_n \to 0 \) by (ii). Thus, \( 0 = \lim_{n \to \infty} (n! - \frac{n!}{e} \sum_{r=0}^n \frac{1}{r!}) \), which rearranges to \( e = \lim_{n \to \infty} \sum_{r=0}^n \frac{1}{r!} \).

commonMistakesLatex: Failing to recognize that non-parallel vectors are linearly independent.
Incorrectly setting up the vector equation for the intersection of two lines.
Errors in signs during the integration by parts process.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify \( \vec{BL} = \frac{4}{7}\vec{BC} \) by checking that the sum of coefficients in the barycentric coordinate system equals 1.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply force equilibrium in statics, model simple harmonic motion, evaluate integrals using substitution, and apply the triangle inequality to complex numbers.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For statics, resolve forces into horizontal and vertical components. For SHM, use the standard equation \(\ddot{x} = -n^2(x-b)\). For the integral, use the suggested substitution to simplify the inverse trigonometric function. For complex numbers, use the triangle inequality \(|z_1 + z_2| \le |z_1| + |z_2|\).

steps.title: (a)(i) Force Equilibrium
steps.bodyLatex: Resolving horizontally, the components must balance: \(T_1 \cos \theta = T_2 \cos \phi\). Resolving vertically, the upward components must balance the downward weight: \(T_1 \sin \theta = Mg + T_2 \sin \phi\). Dividing the vertical equation by the horizontal equation gives \(\frac{T_1 \sin \theta}{T_1 \cos \theta} = \frac{Mg + T_2 \sin \phi}{T_2 \cos \phi}\), which simplifies to \(\tan \theta = \frac{Mg}{T_2 \cos \phi} + \tan \phi\).

steps.title: (a)(ii) Geometric Constraint
steps.bodyLatex: From the geometry, \(\tan \theta = \frac{\ell}{d}\) and \(\tan \phi = \frac{h - \ell}{2d}\). Substituting these into the result from (i), we get \(\frac{\ell}{d} = \frac{Mg}{T_2 \cos \phi} + \frac{h - \ell}{2d}\). Since \(\frac{Mg}{T_2 \cos \phi} > 0\), it follows that \(\frac{\ell}{d} > \frac{h - \ell}{2d}\), which simplifies to \(2\ell > h - \ell\), or \(\ell > \frac{h}{3}\). The height of the machine is \(h - \ell\), so \(h - \ell < h - \frac{h}{3} = \frac{2h}{3}\).

steps.title: (b) Simple Harmonic Motion
steps.bodyLatex: The amplitude is \(a = \frac{0.17 - 0.05}{2} = 0.06\) m and the centre is \(b = 0.11\) m. The period is \(T = \frac{1}{40} = 0.025\) s, so \(n = \frac{2\pi}{0.025} = 80\pi\). The maximum acceleration is \(|\ddot{x}|_{\text{max}} = n^2 a = (80\pi)^2 \times 0.06 = 384\pi^2\). The maximum force is \(F = m \times |\ddot{x}|_{\text{max}} = 0.8 \times 384\pi^2 \approx 3032\) N.

steps.title: (c) Integration by Substitution
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(x = \tan^2 \theta\), then \(dx = 2 \tan \theta \sec^2 \theta \, d\theta\). The integrand becomes \(\sin^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{\tan^2 \theta}{1 + \tan^2 \theta}} = \sin^{-1} \sqrt{\sin^2 \theta} = \theta\). The integral is \(\int_0^{\pi/4} 2\theta \tan \theta \sec^2 \theta \, d\theta\). Using integration by parts with \(u = \theta\) and \(dv = 2 \tan \theta \sec^2 \theta \, d\theta\), we get \([\theta \tan^2 \theta]_0^{\pi/4} - \int_0^{\pi/4} \tan^2 \theta \, d\theta = \frac{\pi}{4} - \int_0^{\pi/4} (\sec^2 \theta - 1) \, d\theta = \frac{\pi}{4} - [\tan \theta - \theta]_0^{\pi/4} = \frac{\pi}{2} - 1\).

steps.title: (d) Triangle Inequality
steps.bodyLatex: By the triangle inequality, \(|z| = |(z - \frac{4}{z}) + \frac{4}{z}| \le |z - \frac{4}{z}| + |\frac{4}{z}|\). Substituting the given \(|z - \frac{4}{z}| = 2\), we have \(|z| \le 2 + \frac{4}{|z|}\). Multiplying by \(|z|\) gives \(|z|^2 - 2|z| - 4 \le 0\). Solving the quadratic \(|z|^2 - 2|z| - 4 = 0\) gives \(|z| = \frac{2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 16}}{2} = 1 \pm \sqrt{5}\). Since \(|z| \ge 0\), we conclude \(|z| \le 1 + \sqrt{5}\).

commonMistakesLatex: In (a)(i), failing to resolve forces correctly into horizontal and vertical components.
In (b), confusing the centre of motion with the maximum height.
In (c), forgetting the chain rule or incorrectly simplifying the \(\sin^{-1}\) term.
In (d), incorrectly applying the triangle inequality or failing to solve the resulting quadratic inequality.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (b), \(0.8 \times 384 \times 9.8696 \approx 3032\). For (d), if \(|z| = 1+\sqrt{5} \approx 3.236\), then \(|z - 4/z| \approx |3.236 - 1.236| = 2\), which satisfies the boundary.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply complex number geometry, solve differential equations for projectile motion with air resistance, use the AM-GM inequality for optimization, and manipulate properties of roots of unity.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), use complex rotation. For (b), set up the equation of motion \(M\ddot{y} = -Mg - 0.1Mv\) and integrate. For (c), apply the given inequality to the products of dimensions. For (d), use Vieta's formulas and the property that for unimodular complex numbers, the reciprocal is the conjugate.

steps.title: (a) Find \(z_B\)
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(z_B = b + 5i\) and \(z_C = c - 5i\). Since \(z_A, z_B, z_C\) form an equilateral triangle, rotating \(z_B - z_A\) by \(60^\circ\) (\(e^{i\pi/3}\)) gives \(z_C - z_A\). Thus, \((z_C - z_A) = e^{i\pi/3}(z_B - z_A)\). Substituting \(z_A = 5 + i\), we get \((c-5) - 6i = (\frac{1}{2} + i\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2})((b-5) + 4i)\). Equating the imaginary parts: \(-6 = \frac{1}{2}(4) + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(b-5)\). Solving for \(b\), we find \(b-5 = -\frac{16}{\sqrt{3}}\), so \(z_B = (5 - \frac{16}{\sqrt{3}}) + 5i\).

steps.title: (b) Projectile motion
steps.bodyLatex: The equation of motion is \(M\frac{dv}{dt} = -Mg - 0.1Mv\), which simplifies to \(\frac{dv}{dt} = -(10 + 0.1v)\). Separating variables and integrating gives \(10\ln(10 + 0.1v) = -t + C\). Using \(v(0) = v_0\), we find \(v(t) = 100(e^{-0.1t} - 1) + v_0 e^{-0.1t}\). Integrating \(v(t)\) to find position \(y(t)\) and setting \(y(7) = 0\) allows us to solve for \(v_0 = \frac{70}{1 - e^{-0.7}} - 100 \approx 39.1 \text{ m s}^{-1}\).

steps.title: (c) Optimization
steps.bodyLatex: For (i), let \(x_1 = ab, x_2 = bc, x_3 = ca\). The surface area is \(S = 2(ab+bc+ca)\), so the mean \(A = \frac{S}{6}\). Substituting into the given inequality \(\frac{(abc)^2}{A^3} \le 1\) yields \((abc)^2 \le (S/6)^3\), so \(abc \le (S/6)^{3/2}\). For (ii), if the prism is a cube, \(a=b=c\), then \(S = 6a^2\). The inequality becomes \(a^3 \le (6a^2/6)^{3/2} = (a^2)^{3/2} = a^3\), confirming the volume is maximized when the prism is a cube.

steps.title: (d) Complex numbers
steps.bodyLatex: Given \(|z_1|=|z_2|=|z_3|=1\) and \(z_1z_2z_3=1\), we have \(\bar{z}_1 = 1/z_1\), etc. The condition \(z_1+z_2+z_3=1\) implies \(\bar{z}_1+\bar{z}_2+\bar{z}_3 = 1/z_1 + 1/z_2 + 1/z_3 = 1\), which simplifies to \(\frac{z_2z_3 + z_1z_3 + z_1z_2}{z_1z_2z_3} = 1\), so \(z_1z_2 + z_2z_3 + z_3z_1 = 1\). The numbers are roots of \(z^3 - z^2 + z - 1 = 0\), which factors as \((z-1)(z^2+1)=0\). Thus, the solutions are \(1, i, -i\).

commonMistakesLatex: In (a), failing to use the correct rotation formula or miscalculating the imaginary part expansion.
In (b), incorrectly integrating the differential equation or failing to account for the total time of 7 seconds.
In (d), forgetting that for complex numbers on the unit circle, the reciprocal is equal to the conjugate.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a), check that the distance \(|z_B - z_A| = |z_C - z_A|\). For (d), verify that \(1+i-i=1\) and \(1 \cdot i \cdot (-i) = 1 \cdot (-i^2) = 1\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of vector addition in three-dimensional space using the properties of geometric shapes.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Use the commutative property of vector addition to rearrange the expression, then identify equivalent vectors based on the geometry of the cubes.

steps.title: Rearrange the expression
steps.bodyLatex: We are given the expression \( \vec{AB} + \vec{CQ} \). By the commutative law of vector addition, we can write this as \( \vec{CQ} + \vec{AB} \).

steps.title: Identify equivalent vectors
steps.bodyLatex: Observe the diagram: the vector \( \vec{AB} \) represents the translation across one cube edge. Since the cubes are identical and aligned, \( \vec{AB} = \vec{BC} = \vec{CD} = \vec{DE} \). Similarly, \( \vec{CQ} \) is a vector spanning from the bottom-front edge to the top-back edge of the cube.

steps.title: Apply vector addition
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute \( \vec{AB} \) with \( \vec{BC} \) in our expression: \( \vec{CQ} + \vec{BC} = \vec{BC} + \vec{CQ} \). By the triangle law of vector addition, \( \vec{BC} + \vec{CQ} = \vec{BQ} \). However, looking at the options, we can also see that \( \vec{CQ} + \vec{AB} = \vec{CQ} + \vec{BC} \) is not the only path. Let's look at \( \vec{CQ} + \vec{AB} \) as \( \vec{CQ} + \vec{CP} \) is not correct, but rather \( \vec{CQ} + \vec{AB} = \vec{CQ} + \vec{BC} \). Wait, note that \( \vec{AB} = \vec{CP} \) is incorrect, but \( \vec{CQ} + \vec{AB} = \vec{CQ} + \vec{BC} \) is not quite right. Let's re-evaluate: \( \vec{CQ} + \vec{AB} = \vec{CQ} + \vec{BC} \) is actually \( \vec{BQ} \). Looking at the diagram, \( \vec{CP} \) is the vector from \( C \) to \( P \). Since \( \vec{AB} = \vec{BC} \), then \( \vec{CQ} + \vec{AB} = \vec{CQ} + \vec{BC} \). Actually, \( \vec{CQ} + \vec{BC} \) is the same as \( \vec{BC} + \vec{CQ} \), which is \( \vec{BQ} \). Checking the options, \( \vec{CP} \) is equivalent to \( \vec{BQ} \) because they represent the same displacement across the cubes.

commonMistakesLatex: Misinterpreting the 3D geometry of the cubes and assuming vectors are in 2D.
Failing to recognize that parallel edges of identical cubes represent equal vectors.
Incorrectly applying the triangle law of vector addition by misidentifying the start and end points.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by assigning coordinates to the vertices; for example, let \( A = (0,0,0) \), \( B = (1,0,0) \), \( C = (2,0,0) \), \( Q = (3,1,1) \). Then \( \vec{AB} = (1,0,0) \) and \( \vec{CQ} = (1,1,1) \). Their sum is \( (2,1,1) \). Option B, \( \vec{CP} \), where \( P = (4,1,1) \), gives \( P - C = (4-2, 1-0, 1-0) = (2,1,1) \). The vectors match.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply the integration by parts formula to reduce the power of a polynomial term in an integral.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the integration by parts formula: \( \int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du \). By choosing \( u = x^5 \) and \( dv = e^{7x} \, dx \), we can simplify the integral.

steps.title: Identify components for integration by parts
steps.bodyLatex: Let \( u = x^5 \) and \( dv = e^{7x} \, dx \). Then, differentiating \( u \) gives \( du = 5x^4 \, dx \), and integrating \( dv \) gives \( v = \frac{1}{7} e^{7x} \).

steps.title: Apply the integration by parts formula
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute these into the formula \( \int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du \): \[ \int x^5 e^{7x} \, dx = (x^5) \left( \frac{1}{7} e^{7x} \right) - \int \left( \frac{1}{7} e^{7x} \right) (5x^4 \, dx) \]

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.bodyLatex: Simplify the constants and the integral expression to obtain: \[ \frac{1}{7} x^5 e^{7x} - \frac{5}{7} \int x^4 e^{7x} \, dx \]

commonMistakesLatex: Incorrectly differentiating \( e^{7x} \) instead of integrating it when finding \( v \).
Forgetting the constant factor \( \frac{1}{7} \) that arises from the chain rule during integration.
Swapping the \( u \) and \( dv \) terms, which would make the integral more complex rather than simpler.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check by differentiating the result: the derivative of \( \frac{1}{7} x^5 e^{7x} \) using the product rule produces \( x^5 e^{7x} + \frac{5}{7} x^4 e^{7x} \). Subtracting the derivative of the integral term \( \frac{5}{7} x^4 e^{7x} \) leaves exactly \( x^5 e^{7x} \), confirming the result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests the ability to determine the vector equation of a line in three-dimensional space given two points.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: A vector equation of a line is given by \( \underset{\sim}{r} = \underset{\sim}{a} + \lambda \underset{\sim}{v} \), where \( \underset{\sim}{a} \) is the position vector of a point on the line and \( \underset{\sim}{v} \) is the direction vector. We calculate the direction vector by finding the displacement between points \( A \) and \( B \).

steps.title: Calculate the direction vector
steps.bodyLatex: The direction vector \( \underset{\sim}{v} \) can be found by calculating \( \vec{AB} = \vec{OB} - \vec{OA} \). Substituting the coordinates, we get \( \underset{\sim}{v} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 - 4 \\ 2 - 2 \\ 1 - 5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -6 \\ 0 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix} \).

steps.title: Simplify the direction vector
steps.bodyLatex: Any scalar multiple of the direction vector is also a valid direction vector for the line. We can divide \( \begin{pmatrix} -6 \\ 0 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix} \) by \( -2 \) to obtain the simpler vector \( \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} \).

steps.title: Form the vector equation
steps.bodyLatex: Using point \( A(4, 2, 5) \) as our position vector \( \underset{\sim}{a} \) and our simplified direction vector, the equation becomes \( \underset{\sim}{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} \). This matches option B.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the position vector with the direction vector.
Incorrectly calculating the displacement vector by using \( \vec{OA} - \vec{OB} \) (though this is still a valid direction vector, it must be checked against the options).
Failing to recognise that direction vectors can be scaled by any non-zero constant.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the points by testing values of \( \lambda \). For \( \lambda = 0 \), \( \underset{\sim}{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} \) (Point A). For \( \lambda = -2 \), \( \underset{\sim}{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} - 2 \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \) (Point B).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of logical equivalence, specifically the definition of the contrapositive of a conditional statement.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For a conditional statement of the form "If \(P\), then \(Q\)", the contrapositive is defined as "If not \(Q\), then not \(P\)". Both statements are logically equivalent.

steps.title: Identify the components of the statement
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(P\) be the statement "\(n\) is a multiple of \(6\)" and \(Q\) be the statement "\(n\) is a multiple of \(2\)". The original statement is "If \(P\), then \(Q\)".

steps.title: Form the contrapositive
steps.bodyLatex: The contrapositive is "If not \(Q\), then not \(P\)". Substituting our definitions, this becomes "If \(n\) is not a multiple of \(2\), then \(n\) is not a multiple of \(6\)".

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing our result to the given options, we see that option \(C\) matches our derived statement exactly.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the contrapositive with the converse (If \(Q\), then \(P\)) or the inverse (If not \(P\), then not \(Q\)).
Negating the quantifier "For all" instead of keeping it the same, which would lead to incorrect options like A or B.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The contrapositive must have the same truth value as the original statement. Since the original statement is true (all multiples of \(6\) are indeed even), and option \(C\) is also true (if a number is odd, it cannot be a multiple of \(6\)), the logic holds.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of inequalities and the properties of monotonic functions over different domains.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We evaluate each statement by checking if the inequality is preserved or reversed when applying the given functions, specifically looking for counterexamples where the implication fails.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement A
steps.bodyLatex: The function \( f(x) = x^3 \) is strictly increasing for all \( x \in \mathbb{R} \). Therefore, if \( a < b \), then \( a^3 < b^3 \) is always true.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement B
steps.bodyLatex: The function \( f(x) = e^{-x} \) is strictly decreasing because its derivative is \( -e^{-x} < 0 \). Thus, if \( a < b \), then \( -a > -b \), which implies \( e^{-a} > e^{-b} \). This statement is true.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement C
steps.bodyLatex: The natural logarithm function \( f(x) = \ln x \) is strictly increasing on its domain \( (0, +\infty) \). Thus, \( a < b \implies \ln a < \ln b \) is true.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement D
steps.bodyLatex: Consider the function \( f(x) = \frac{1}{x} \). If we choose \( a = -2 \) and \( b = 1 \), we have \( a < b \). However, \( \frac{1}{a} = -0.5 \) and \( \frac{1}{b} = 1 \). Since \( -0.5 < 1 \), the condition \( \frac{1}{a} > \frac{1}{b} \) does not hold. Therefore, statement D is false.

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming that the reciprocal function is decreasing across its entire domain, forgetting that it is discontinuous at \( x = 0 \).
Failing to test negative values when evaluating inequalities involving fractions.
Confusing the properties of strictly increasing functions with those that are only increasing on specific intervals.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: To verify D is false, simply test values on opposite sides of the origin, such as \( a = -1 \) and \( b = 1 \), where \( -1 < 1 \) but \( -1 < 1 \) is true, contradicting the claim that the reciprocal must be greater.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests the Conjugate Root Theorem for polynomials with real coefficients.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Since the polynomial has real coefficients and \(2 + i\) is a zero, its complex conjugate \(2 - i\) must also be a zero. We can then form a quadratic factor using the sum and product of these roots and check which option is divisible by this factor.

steps.title: Identify the quadratic factor
steps.bodyLatex: If \(z_1 = 2 + i\) and \(z_2 = 2 - i\) are zeros, the quadratic factor is given by \((x - z_1)(x - z_2) = x^2 - (z_1 + z_2)x + z_1z_2\). Calculating these: sum = \((2+i) + (2-i) = 4\) and product = \((2+i)(2-i) = 4 - i^2 = 5\). Thus, the quadratic factor is \(x^2 - 4x + 5\).

steps.title: Test the options
steps.bodyLatex: We need the polynomial \(P(x)\) to be divisible by \(x^2 - 4x + 5\). Let us test option A: \(P(x) = x^3 - 4x^2 + kx\). We can factor this as \(x(x^2 - 4x + k)\). For this to be divisible by \(x^2 - 4x + 5\), we must have \(k = 5\).

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.bodyLatex: If \(k = 5\), then \(P(x) = x(x^2 - 4x + 5)\). The zeros are \(x = 0\) and the roots of \(x^2 - 4x + 5 = 0\), which are \(2 \pm i\). This confirms that \(2+i\) is indeed a zero.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that the conjugate root theorem only applies when all coefficients are real.
Incorrectly calculating the sum or product of the complex roots.
Assuming the constant term must be 5 without performing polynomial division or factoring.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Substitute \(x = 2+i\) into option A: \((2+i)^3 - 4(2+i)^2 + k(2+i) = (8 + 12i - 6 - i) - 4(4 + 4i - 1) + k(2+i) = (2 + 11i) - 12 - 16i + 2k + ki = (-10 + 2k) + i(k - 5)\). Setting this to zero gives \(k=5\), which is consistent.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret a 3D parametric vector equation and identify the corresponding geometric shape.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We analyze the components of the position vector \( \mathbf{r}(t) = x(t)\mathbf{i} + y(t)\mathbf{j} + z(t)\mathbf{k} \). By observing the circular motion in the \( xy \)-plane and the linear increase in height along the \( z \)-axis, we can identify the curve as a helix.

steps.title: Analyze the xy-projection
steps.bodyLatex: The components in the \( xy \)-plane are \( x(t) = -5\cos(t) \) and \( y(t) = 5\sin(t) \). Since \( x^2 + y^2 = (-5\cos t)^2 + (5\sin t)^2 = 25(\cos^2 t + \sin^2 t) = 25 \), the projection of the curve onto the \( xy \)-plane is a circle of radius \( 5 \) centered at the origin.

steps.title: Analyze the z-component
steps.bodyLatex: The \( z \)-component is \( z(t) = t \). As \( t \) increases from \( 0 \) to \( 4\pi \), the height \( z \) increases linearly from \( 0 \) to \( 4\pi \approx 12.56 \). This indicates the curve is a helix winding upwards along the \( z \)-axis.

steps.title: Determine the starting point and direction
steps.bodyLatex: At \( t = 0 \), the position is \( \mathbf{r}(0) = -5\cos(0)\mathbf{i} + 5\sin(0)\mathbf{j} + 0\mathbf{k} = -5\mathbf{i} \). This means the curve starts at the point \( (-5, 0, 0) \) on the negative \( x \)-axis. As \( t \) increases, the point moves counter-clockwise when viewed from above, as \( y \) becomes positive for small positive \( t \).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the direction of the helix (clockwise vs counter-clockwise).
Misinterpreting the starting point on the axes.
Failing to recognize that \( z=t \) implies a vertical helix rather than one lying on its side.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the point at \( t = \pi \): \( \mathbf{r}(\pi) = -5\cos(\pi)\mathbf{i} + 5\sin(\pi)\mathbf{j} + \pi\mathbf{k} = 5\mathbf{i} + 0\mathbf{j} + \pi\mathbf{k} \). The curve must pass through \( (5, 0, \pi) \), which matches the visual path in diagram D.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the relationship between velocity, acceleration, and the motion of a particle along a curved path.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We decompose the acceleration vector \( \underset{\sim}{a} \) into two components: the tangential component \( \underset{\sim}{a_t} \) (responsible for speed change) and the normal component \( \underset{\sim}{a_n} \) (responsible for changing the direction of the velocity).

steps.title: Analyze the tangential component
steps.bodyLatex: Since the speed of the particle is increasing, the tangential component of the acceleration \( \underset{\sim}{a_t} \) must point in the same direction as the velocity vector \( \underset{\sim}{v} \).

steps.title: Analyze the normal component
steps.bodyLatex: To follow the curve towards point \( B \), the particle must turn in the direction of the curve. This requires a normal component of acceleration \( \underset{\sim}{a_n} \) that points toward the inside of the curve's path.

steps.title: Combine the components
steps.bodyLatex: The total acceleration \( \underset{\sim}{a} \) is the vector sum \( \underset{\sim}{a} = \underset{\sim}{a_t} + \underset{\sim}{a_n} \). By vector addition, the resulting vector \( \underset{\sim}{a} \) must point 'forward' along the path (due to speed increase) and 'inward' toward the curve (due to the change in direction).

steps.title: Select the correct diagram
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing the options, diagram \( D \) shows a vector that points both in the direction of the velocity and towards the concave side of the curve, which is the only configuration that satisfies both requirements.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the tangential component with the normal component.
Assuming acceleration must point only in the direction of the velocity.
Ignoring the requirement for the particle to turn along the curve, which necessitates a centripetal (normal) component.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify that the vector \( \underset{\sim}{a} \) has a positive projection onto the velocity vector \( \underset{\sim}{v} \) (speeding up) and a component perpendicular to \( \underset{\sim}{v} \) pointing towards the center of curvature (turning).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of logical implication, specifically the Wason selection task. It requires you to identify which cases could potentially falsify a conditional statement of the form "If P, then Q".
workedSolutions.approachLatex: The statement is "If a card is RED (P), then it has WIN (Q) on the other side". To test this, you must check cases that could prove the statement false, which are P (a RED card) and not-Q (a card that is NOT WIN).

steps.title: Identify the condition
steps.bodyLatex: The statement is an implication: \( P \implies Q \), where \( P \) is "the card is RED" and \( Q \) is "the card has WIN". The statement is only false if we find a card that is \( P \) (RED) but not \( Q \) (NOT WIN).

steps.title: Evaluate Card 4 (RED)
steps.bodyLatex: We must check Card 4 because it is \( P \) (RED). If the other side is not WIN, the statement is proven false. Therefore, we must turn over Card 4.

steps.title: Evaluate Card 3 (TRY AGAIN)
steps.bodyLatex: Card 3 shows "TRY AGAIN", which is not WIN (not \( Q \)). If the other side is RED (\( P \)), the statement is proven false. Therefore, we must turn over Card 3.

steps.title: Evaluate Cards 1 and 2
steps.bodyLatex: Card 1 shows WIN (\( Q \)). Whether the other side is RED or BLACK does not affect the truth of the statement. Card 2 shows BLACK (not \( P \)), so it cannot falsify the statement regardless of what is on the other side.

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming that because the statement mentions RED and WIN, you only need to check the cards showing those labels.
Forgetting to check the 'not-Q' case (the card showing TRY AGAIN), which is a classic error in logical reasoning.
Checking the 'not-P' case (the BLACK card), which is irrelevant to the truth of the implication.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The statement is only falsified by the combination (RED, NOT WIN). Card 4 is RED, so it could hide NOT WIN. Card 3 is NOT WIN, so it could hide RED. No other cards can form this falsifying pair.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the geometric interpretation of complex numbers as vectors in the Argand plane and the algebraic definition of a vector projection.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recall that the scalar projection of a vector \( z \) onto a vector \( w \) is given by the dot product \( z \cdot \hat{w} \), where \( \hat{w} \) is the unit vector in the direction of \( w \). We use the identity \( z \cdot w = \text{Re}(z\bar{w}) \) and the property that the projection is a vector in the direction of \( w \).

steps.title: Define the projection formula
steps.bodyLatex: The projection of vector \( z \) onto vector \( w \) is given by \( \text{proj}_w z = \left( \frac{z \cdot w}{|w|^2} \right) w \).

steps.title: Use complex number properties
steps.bodyLatex: In the complex plane, the dot product of two vectors \( z \) and \( w \) is \( \text{Re}(z\bar{w}) \). Substituting this into the projection formula gives \( \text{proj}_w z = \frac{\text{Re}(z\bar{w})}{|w|^2} w \).

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.bodyLatex: Note that \( |w|^2 = w\bar{w} \). We can rewrite the fraction as \( \frac{\text{Re}(z\bar{w})}{w\bar{w}} w = \text{Re}\left( \frac{z\bar{w}}{w\bar{w}} \right) w \). Since \( \frac{\bar{w}}{w\bar{w}} = \frac{\bar{w}}{|w|^2} = \frac{1}{w} \), this simplifies to \( \text{Re}\left( \frac{z}{w} \right) w \).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the scalar projection (a real number) with the vector projection (a complex number/vector).
Incorrectly assuming the dot product of complex numbers is simply \( zw \) rather than \( \text{Re}(z\bar{w}) \).
Forgetting to multiply by the unit vector \( \frac{w}{|w|} \) or the direction vector \( w \) to ensure the result is a vector.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: If \( z = w \), the projection should be \( w \). Substituting into C: \( \text{Re}(w/w)w = \text{Re}(1)w = 1w = w \), which confirms the formula is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your proficiency in complex number arithmetic, vector geometry, partial fraction decomposition, and solving quadratic equations involving complex numbers.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For complex numbers, use polar form properties for multiplication and Cartesian form for division. For vectors, use the dot product formula to find the angle. For partial fractions, equate coefficients after clearing denominators.

steps.title: (a) Multiply complex numbers
steps.bodyLatex: To multiply numbers in polar form, multiply the moduli and add the arguments: \(zw = (2 \times 6)e^{i(\frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{\pi}{6})} = 12e^{i(\frac{3\pi}{6} + \frac{\pi}{6})} = 12e^{i\frac{4\pi}{6}} = 12e^{i\frac{2\pi}{3}}\).

steps.title: (b) Evaluate the sum of powers of i
(c) Find the angle between vectors
steps.bodyLatex: Use the formula \(\cos \theta = \frac{\underline{a} \cdot \underline{b}}{|\underline{a}||\underline{b}|}\). The dot product is \((2)(-3) + (0)(1) + (4)(2) = -6 + 0 + 8 = 2\). The magnitudes are \(|\underline{a}| = \sqrt{2^2 + 0^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{20}\) and \(|\underline{b}| = \sqrt{(-3)^2 + 1^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{14}\). Thus, \(\cos \theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{20}\sqrt{14}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{280}} \approx 0.1195\), so \(\theta \approx 83.1^{\circ}\).

steps.title: (d)(i) Find square roots of -i
steps.bodyLatex: Let \((x+iy)^2 = -i\), so \(x^2 - y^2 = 0\) and \(2xy = -1\). From \(x^2=y^2\), we have \(y = \pm x\). Substituting into \(2xy=-1\), we find \(2x(-x) = -1 \implies x^2 = 1/2\), so \(x = \pm 1/\sqrt{2}\). The roots are \(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}\) and \(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + \frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}\).

steps.title: (d)(ii) Solve the quadratic equation
steps.bodyLatex: Complete the square: \((z+1)^2 + i = 0\), so \((z+1)^2 = -i\). From part (i), \(z+1 = \pm(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \frac{i}{\sqrt{2}})\). Thus, \(z = -1 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}\) and \(z = -1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + \frac{i}{\sqrt{2}}\).

steps.title: (e) Divide complex numbers
steps.bodyLatex: Given \(z=5+i\), then \(\overline{z}=5-i\). Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of \(w=2-4i\), which is \(2+4i\): \(\frac{(5-i)(2+4i)}{(2-4i)(2+4i)} = \frac{10 + 20i - 2i - 4i^2}{4 + 16} = \frac{14 + 18i}{20} = \frac{7}{10} + \frac{9}{10}i\).

steps.title: (f) Partial fractions
steps.bodyLatex: Set \(\frac{3x^2-5}{(x-2)(x^2+x+1)} = \frac{A}{x-2} + \frac{Bx+C}{x^2+x+1}\). Multiplying by the denominator gives \(3x^2-5 = A(x^2+x+1) + (Bx+C)(x-2)\). Setting \(x=2\) gives \(7=7A \implies A=1\). Equating \(x^2\) terms gives \(3=A+B \implies B=2\). Equating constants gives \(-5=A-2C \implies -6=-2C \implies C=3\). The result is \(\frac{1}{x-2} + \frac{2x+3}{x^2+x+1}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to add the arguments in polar multiplication.
Errors in sign when calculating the dot product or expanding complex conjugates.
Failing to connect part (d)(ii) to the result in part (d)(i).
Arithmetic errors when simplifying fractions or using \( i^2 = -1 \).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify (a) by converting to Cartesian, (c) by checking if the dot product is positive (acute angle), and (f) by recombining the fractions to see if they return to the original expression.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your proficiency in integral calculus, logical reasoning, 3D vector geometry, and mathematical induction.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For integration, complete the square and split the fraction. For logic, define the converse. For vectors, use the dot product for perpendicularity and equate components for intersection. For induction, follow the standard base case, assumption, and inductive step structure.

steps.title: (a) Integration
steps.bodyLatex: Rewrite the numerator as the derivative of the denominator plus a constant: \( 2x + 3 = (2x + 2) + 1 \). Thus, \( \int \frac{2x+2}{x^2+2x+2} dx + \int \frac{1}{(x+1)^2 + 1} dx \). This gives \( \ln(x^2+2x+2) + \tan^{-1}(x+1) + k \).

steps.title: (b) Logical Reasoning
steps.bodyLatex: (i) The converse is: 'If \( n \) is even, then \( n^2 \) is even'. (ii) If \( n \) is even, \( n = 2k \) for some integer \( k \). Then \( n^2 = (2k)^2 = 4k^2 = 2(2k^2) \), which is even.

steps.title: (c) 3D Geometry
steps.bodyLatex: Since the lines are perpendicular, the dot product of their direction vectors is zero: \( (1, 0, 2) \cdot (p, 3, -1) = p + 0 - 2 = 0 \implies p = 2 \). Equating components, \( -2 + \lambda = 4 + 2\mu \) and \( 1 = -2 + 3\mu \implies \mu = 1 \). Substituting \( \mu = 1 \) into the first equation gives \( \lambda = 8 \). Finally, using the third components: \( 3 + 2(8) = q - 1 \implies 19 = q - 1 \implies q = 20 \).

steps.title: (d) Mathematical Induction
steps.bodyLatex: Base case \( n=9 \): \( \sqrt{9!} \approx 602.4 > 2^9 = 512 \). Assume \( \sqrt{k!} > 2^k \). For \( n=k+1 \), \( \sqrt{(k+1)!} = \sqrt{k!} \cdot \sqrt{k+1} > 2^k \cdot \sqrt{k+1} \). Since \( \sqrt{k+1} > 2 \) for \( k \ge 9 \), it follows that \( \sqrt{(k+1)!} > 2^k \cdot 2 = 2^{k+1} \).

steps.title: (e) Vector Geometry
steps.bodyLatex: (i) Since \( H \) is the midpoint of diagonals \( AC \) and \( BD \), \( \vec{HA} + \vec{HC} = \mathbf{0} \) and \( \vec{HB} + \vec{HD} = \mathbf{0} \), so their sum is \( \mathbf{0} \). (ii) Using \( \vec{GX} = \vec{GH} + \vec{HX} \), the sum \( \sum \vec{GA} = 4\vec{GH} + (\vec{HA} + \vec{HB} + \vec{HC} + \vec{HD}) - \vec{GS} = 4\vec{GH} + \mathbf{0} - \vec{GS} = \mathbf{0} \), hence \( 4\vec{GH} + \vec{GS} = \mathbf{0} \). (iii) Since \( \vec{GS} = \vec{GH} + \vec{HS} \), we have \( 4\vec{GH} + \vec{GH} + \vec{HS} = \mathbf{0} \implies 5\vec{GH} = -\vec{HS} \implies \vec{HG} = \frac{1}{5}\vec{HS} \), so \( \lambda = \frac{1}{5} \).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) \(\ln(x^2+2x+2)+\tan^{-1}(x+1)+k; (b)(i) If n is even, then n^2 is even; (c) p=2, q=20; (e)(iii) \lambda=\frac{1}{5}\)
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the constant of integration in part (a).
Confusing the converse with the contrapositive in part (b).
Failing to use the dot product correctly for perpendicular lines in part (c).
Algebraic slips when manipulating the vector sum in part (e).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a), differentiate the result to recover the integrand. For (c), check that the point of intersection satisfies both line equations.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply De Moivre's theorem for roots of complex numbers, use integration by parts to derive reduction formulas, calculate volumes of solids of revolution, and solve simple harmonic motion problems.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For complex roots, use polar form and De Moivre's theorem. For the integral, use integration by parts with \( u = (\ln x)^n \) and \( dv = dx \). For volumes, use the disk method \( V = \pi \int y^2 dx \). For SHM, use the standard equations \( \ddot{x} = -n^2(x-c) \) and \( v^2 = n^2(A^2 - (x-c)^2) \).

steps.title: (a) Locating fourth roots
steps.bodyLatex: Let \( z = r(\cos \theta + i \sin \theta) \). The four roots are given by \( z_k = r^{1/4} \left( \cos \frac{\theta + 2k\pi}{4} + i \sin \frac{\theta + 2k\pi}{4} \right) \) for \( k = 0, 1, 2, 3 \). These roots lie on a circle of radius \( r^{1/4} \) centered at the origin, spaced at equal angular intervals of \( \frac{\pi}{2} \) radians.

steps.title: (c)(i) Proving the reduction formula
steps.bodyLatex: Using integration by parts, let \( u = (\ln x)^n \) and \( dv = dx \). Then \( du = n(\ln x)^{n-1} \cdot \frac{1}{x} dx \) and \( v = x \). Applying \( \int u dv = uv - \int v du \), we get \( I_n = [x(\ln x)^n]_1^e - \int_1^e x \cdot \frac{n(\ln x)^{n-1}}{x} dx \). Evaluating the boundary terms gives \( e(1)^n - 0 - n \int_1^e (\ln x)^{n-1} dx = e - nI_{n-1} \).

steps.title: (c)(ii) Calculating volume ratio
steps.bodyLatex: For \( V_A \), the region is between \( y=1 \) and \( y=\sqrt{1-x^2} \), so \( V_A = \pi \int_0^1 (1^2 - (1-x^2)) dx = \pi \int_0^1 x^2 dx = \frac{\pi}{3} \). For \( V_B \), \( V_B = \pi \int_1^e (1^2 - (\ln x)^2) dx = \pi(e-1) - \pi I_2 \). Using the recurrence, \( I_2 = e - 2I_1 = e - 2(e - I_0) = e - 2(e - (e-1)) = e - 2 \). Thus, \( V_B = \pi(e-1) - \pi(e-2) = \pi \). The ratio is \( \frac{\pi}{3} : \pi = 1 : 3 \).

steps.title: (d)(i) SHM oscillation range
steps.bodyLatex: The equation \( \ddot{x} = -4(x-3) \) implies \( n^2 = 4 \) (\( n=2 \)) and center \( c=3 \). Using \( v^2 = n^2(A^2 - (x-c)^2) \), at \( x=0, v=8 \), we have \( 64 = 4(A^2 - (0-3)^2) \), so \( 16 = A^2 - 9 \), giving \( A^2 = 25 \) and \( A = 5 \). The particle oscillates between \( c-A = 3-5 = -2 \) and \( c+A = 3+5 = 8 \).

steps.title: (d)(ii) Finding time \( t \)
steps.bodyLatex: The displacement is \( x(t) = 5\cos(2t + \alpha) + 3 \). At \( t=0, x=5.5 \), so \( 5.5 = 5\cos \alpha + 3 \implies \cos \alpha = 0.5 \implies \alpha = \frac{\pi}{3} \approx 1.047 \). We want \( x=0 \), so \( 0 = 5\cos(2t + 1.047) + 3 \implies \cos(2t + 1.047) = -0.6 \). Solving \( 2t + 1.047 = \arccos(-0.6) \approx 2.214 \) gives \( 2t \approx 1.167 \), so \( t \approx 0.58 \) seconds.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) Roots on circle of radius \( |a+ib|^{1/4} \) spaced by \( \pi/2 \); (c)(ii) \( 1:3 \); (d)(i) \( x \in [-2, 8] \); (d)(ii) \( t \approx 0.58 \text{ s} \)
commonMistakesLatex: In part (c)(ii), failing to use the difference of squares for the volume integral, i.e., incorrectly calculating \( \int (y_1 - y_2)^2 dx \) instead of \( \int (y_1^2 - y_2^2) dx \).
In part (d)(i), confusing the center of oscillation with the origin.
In part (d)(ii), using degrees instead of radians when solving the trigonometric equation.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (c)(ii), \( V_A = \pi/3 \) is the volume of a cylinder minus a hemisphere, which is physically intuitive. For (d)(i), checking the endpoints: at \( x=8 \), \( \ddot{x} = -4(5) = -20 \), which is the maximum negative acceleration, consistent with an amplitude of 5.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests the application of the Weierstrass substitution (t-substitution) for integration, Newtonian mechanics involving forces on an inclined plane, and the use of de Moivre's theorem to derive trigonometric identities and solve for specific values.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), we use the t-substitution \(t = \tan(x/2)\). For (b), we resolve forces parallel to the slope and set the net force to zero for constant velocity. For (c), we expand the binomial expression and equate real parts to form a polynomial equation for \(\cos(\pi/10)\).

steps.title: (a) Evaluate the integral
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(t = \tan(x/2)\), so \(dx = \frac{2}{1+t^2} dt\) and \(\cos x = \frac{1-t^2}{1+t^2}\). As \(x\) goes from \(0\) to \(\pi/2\), \(t\) goes from \(0\) to \(1\). The integral becomes \(\int_0^1 \frac{1}{3 + 5(\frac{1-t^2}{1+t^2})} \cdot \frac{2}{1+t^2} dt = \int_0^1 \frac{2}{3+3t^2+5-5t^2} dt = \int_0^1 \frac{2}{8-2t^2} dt = \int_0^1 \frac{1}{4-t^2} dt\).

steps.title: (a) Partial fractions and integration
steps.bodyLatex: Using partial fractions, \(\frac{1}{4-t^2} = \frac{1}{(2-t)(2+t)} = \frac{1}{4}(\frac{1}{2-t} + \frac{1}{2+t})\). Integrating gives \(\frac{1}{4} [-\ln|2-t| + \ln|2+t|]_0^1 = \frac{1}{4} [\ln|\frac{2+t}{2-t}|]_0^1 = \frac{1}{4}(\ln 3 - \ln 1) = \frac{1}{4}\ln 3\).

steps.title: (b)(i) Resultant force
steps.bodyLatex: The component of gravity acting down the slope is \(mg \sin(90^\circ - 60^\circ) = mg \cos(30^\circ) = 5g \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2}g\). Since the forces \(2v\) and \(2v^2\) act up the slope, the resultant force down the slope is \(F_{res} = \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2}g - 2v - 2v^2\).

steps.title: (b)(ii) Constant speed
steps.bodyLatex: At constant speed, \(F_{res} = 0\), so \(2v^2 + 2v - 25\sqrt{3} = 0\). Using the quadratic formula \(v = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 - 4(2)(-25\sqrt{3})}}{4} = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 200\sqrt{3}}}{4}\). Since \(v > 0\), we take the positive root: \(v = \frac{-2 + \sqrt{350.41}}{4} \approx 4.1798\), which is \(4.2 \text{ m s}^{-1}\) to one decimal place.

steps.title: (c)(i) Trigonometric identity
steps.bodyLatex: By de Moivre's theorem, \((\cos \theta + i \sin \theta)^5 = \cos 5\theta + i \sin 5\theta\). Expanding the left side using the binomial theorem: \(\cos^5 \theta + 5i \cos^4 \theta \sin \theta - 10 \cos^3 \theta \sin^2 \theta - 10i \cos^2 \theta \sin^3 \theta + 5 \cos \theta \sin^4 \theta + i \sin^5 \theta\). Equating real parts: \(\cos 5\theta = \cos^5 \theta - 10 \cos^3 \theta (1-\cos^2 \theta) + 5 \cos \theta (1-\cos^2 \theta)^2 = 16 \cos^5 \theta - 20 \cos^3 \theta + 5 \cos \theta\).

steps.title: (c)(ii) Solving for the cosine value
