steps.bodyLatex: For \(\theta = \pi/10\), \(\cos 5\theta = \cos(\pi/2) = 0\). Thus, \(16 \cos^5 \theta - 20 \cos^3 \theta + 5 \cos \theta = 0\). Factoring out \(\cos \theta \neq 0\), we solve \(16 \cos^4 \theta - 20 \cos^2 \theta + 5 = 0\). Letting \(u = \cos^2 \theta\), the quadratic formula gives \(u = \frac{20 \pm \sqrt{400 - 320}}{32} = \frac{5 \pm \sqrt{5}}{8}\). Since \(\cos(\pi/10) > \cos(\pi/4)\), we select the larger root: \(\cos(\pi/10) = \sqrt{\frac{5+\sqrt{5}}{8}}\).

commonMistakesLatex: In part (a), forgetting to change the integration limits or failing to simplify the algebraic fraction before integrating.
In part (b)(ii), failing to identify that constant speed implies zero acceleration and zero net force.
In part (c)(ii), incorrectly choosing the root of the quadratic equation by not considering the magnitude of the angle.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: \(For (a), numerically integrating 1/(3+5cos(x)) from 0 to pi/2 yields approx 0.2746, while (1/4)ln(3) is approx 0.2746. For (b), 2(4.2)^2 + 2(4.2) = 35.28 + 8.4 = 43.68, and 25*sqrt(3) is approx 43.3, which is consistent.\)

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests the application of the Arithmetic Mean-Geometric Mean (AM-GM) inequality, algebraic manipulation of sequences, and the use of differential equations to model physical motion with air resistance.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), we use the AM-GM inequality iteratively. For part (b), we express the sequence formulas algebraically to compare them. For part (c), we set up and solve the differential equations for motion under gravity and quadratic air resistance. For part (d), we use the Binomial Theorem.

steps.title: (a)(i) Show the inequality
steps.bodyLatex: Using the given inequality \( \sqrt{xy} \le \frac{x+y}{2} \), let \( x = ab \) and \( y = c \). Then \( \sqrt{abc} \le \frac{ab + c}{2} \). Since \( ab \le \frac{a^2 + b^2}{2} \) by AM-GM, we substitute this into the expression: \( \sqrt{abc} \le \frac{\frac{a^2+b^2}{2} + c}{2} = \frac{a^2 + b^2 + 2c}{4} \).

steps.title: (a)(ii) Extend the inequality
steps.bodyLatex: By symmetry, we also have \( \sqrt{abc} \le \frac{a^2 + c^2 + 2b}{4} \) and \( \sqrt{abc} \le \frac{b^2 + c^2 + 2a}{4} \). Summing these three inequalities gives \( 3\sqrt{abc} \le \frac{2a^2 + 2b^2 + 2c^2 + 2a + 2b + 2c}{4} = \frac{a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + a + b + c}{2} \). Dividing by \( 3 \) yields \( \sqrt{abc} \le \frac{a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + a + b + c}{6} \).

steps.title: (b)(i) Triangular and Hexagonal numbers
steps.bodyLatex: Let the odd index be \( n = 2m - 1 \) for \( m \ge 1 \). Then \( t_{2m-1} = \frac{(2m-1)(2m-1+1)}{2} = \frac{(2m-1)(2m)}{2} = m(2m-1) = 2m^2 - m \). This is exactly the definition of \( h_m \).

steps.title: (b)(ii) Even triangular numbers
steps.bodyLatex: For even indices, \( t_{2m} = \frac{2m(2m+1)}{2} = 2m^2 + m \). If \( 2m^2 + m = 2k^2 - k \), then \( m+k = 2(k^2 - m^2) = 2(k-m)(k+m) \). Dividing by \( k+m \) gives \( 1 = 2(k-m) \), which is impossible for integers since \( 1 \) is odd and \( 2(k-m) \) is even.

steps.title: (c)(i) Time to maximum height
steps.bodyLatex: The equation of motion is \( m \frac{dv}{dt} = -mg - kv^2 \). With \( m=1 \), \( \frac{dv}{dt} = -(g + kv^2) \). Separating variables: \( \int dt = -\int \frac{dv}{g+kv^2} \). This integrates to \( t = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{gk}} \arctan(v\sqrt{\frac{k}{g}}) + C \). At \( t=0, v=u \), so \( C = \frac{1}{\sqrt{gk}} \arctan(u\sqrt{\frac{k}{g}}) \). At max height, \( v=0 \), so \( t = \frac{1}{\sqrt{gk}} \arctan(u\sqrt{\frac{k}{g}}) \).

steps.title: (c)(ii) Maximum height
steps.bodyLatex: Using \( v \frac{dv}{dx} = -(g + kv^2) \), we have \( \int dx = -\int \frac{v}{g+kv^2} dv \). Using substitution \( w = g+kv^2 \), we get \( x = -\frac{1}{2k} \ln(g+kv^2) + C \). At \( x=0, v=u \), so \( C = \frac{1}{2k} \ln(g+ku^2) \). At \( v=0 \), \( x = \frac{1}{2k} \ln(\frac{g+ku^2}{g}) \).

steps.title: (d) Prove the inequality
steps.bodyLatex: By the Binomial Theorem, \( 5^n = (2+3)^n = 2^n + 3^n + \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \binom{n}{k} 2^k 3^{n-k} \). For \( n \ge 2 \), the sum contains at least one term \( \binom{n}{1} 2^1 3^{n-1} = 2n \cdot 3^{n-1} > 0 \). Thus, \( 5^n > 2^n + 3^n \), meaning they cannot be equal.

commonMistakesLatex: In part (a), failing to use the AM-GM inequality correctly by not identifying the correct terms to substitute.
In part (b), attempting to prove the statement by testing specific numbers rather than using general algebraic forms.
In part (c), forgetting the constant of integration or failing to correctly handle the negative sign during integration.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a), test with \( a=b=c=1 \): \( \sqrt{1} \le \frac{1+1+1+1+1+1}{6} \implies 1 \le 1 \). For (c), as \( k \to 0 \), the formula for \( t \) approaches \( \frac{u}{g} \) using the small-angle approximation \( \arctan(x) \approx x \), which matches standard kinematics.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply vector identities, projectile motion kinematics, and complex number geometry.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the triangle inequality for vectors, the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, the condition for perpendicular vectors (dot product equals zero), and the geometric interpretation of the complex argument.

steps.title: (a) (i) Lower bound of the sum of coordinates
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(P(x, y, z)\) lies on the unit sphere, \(|\vec{OP}| = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} = 1\). By the triangle inequality, \(|x| + |y| + |z| = |x\underset{\sim}{i}| + |y\underset{\sim}{j}| + |z\underset{\sim}{k}| \ge |x\underset{\sim}{i} + y\underset{\sim}{j} + z\underset{\sim}{k}| = |\vec{OP}| = 1\).

steps.title: (a) (ii) Cauchy-Schwarz inequality
steps.bodyLatex: Using the definition of the dot product, \(\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b} = |\underset{\sim}{a}||\underset{\sim}{b}| \cos \theta\). Since \(|\cos \theta| \le 1\), it follows that \(|\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b}| \le |\underset{\sim}{a}||\underset{\sim}{b}|\). Substituting the components, we get \(|a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + a_3b_3| \le \sqrt{a_1^2 + a_2^2 + a_3^2} \sqrt{b_1^2 + b_2^2 + b_3^2}\).

steps.title: (a) (iii) Upper bound of the sum of coordinates
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(\underset{\sim}{a} = (|x|, |y|, |z|)\) and \(\underset{\sim}{b} = (1, 1, 1)\). Applying the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, \(|x| + |y| + |z| \le \sqrt{|x|^2 + |y|^2 + |z|^2} \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2} = 1 \times \sqrt{3} = \sqrt{3}\).

steps.title: (b) Perpendicularity in projectile motion
steps.bodyLatex: The velocity is \(\dot{\underset{\sim}{r}}(t) = (u \cos \theta, -gt + u \sin \theta)\). For perpendicularity, \(\underset{\sim}{r} \cdot \dot{\underset{\sim}{r}} = 0\), which simplifies to \(g^2t^2 - 3ugt \sin \theta + 2u^2 = 0\). For two distinct positive times, the discriminant must be positive: \(\Delta = (-3ug \sin \theta)^2 - 4(g^2)(2u^2) = g^2u^2(9 \sin^2 \theta - 8) > 0\). This leads to \(\sin^2 \theta > 8/9\), or \(\sin \theta > \sqrt{8}/3\), which gives \(\theta > 1.23\) radians.

steps.title: (c) Sketching the complex region
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(z = x + iy\). The condition \(\text{Re}(z) \ge \text{Arg}(z)\) becomes \(x \ge \theta\). Since \(\tan \theta = y/x\), we have \(\tan x \ge y/x\), or \(y \le x \tan x\). The region is bounded by the curve \(y = x \tan x\) in the Argand plane.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the modulus of a sum of components with the sum of the absolute values of components.
Failing to justify why the times \(t\) are positive or real in the projectile motion part.
Incorrectly interpreting the principal argument as a linear function without considering the domain of \(\text{Arg}(z)\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (b), test \(\theta = \pi/2\): \(\sin \theta = 1 > \sqrt{8}/3 \approx 0.94\). The quadratic becomes \(g^2t^2 - 3ugt + 2u^2 = 0\), which factors to \((gt - u)(gt - 2u) = 0\), giving two positive times \(t = u/g\) and \(t = 2u/g\), both within flight time \(2u/g\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate the magnitude (length) of a vector in three-dimensional space.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To find the length of a vector \(\vec{v} = a\underset{\sim}{i} + b\underset{\sim}{j} + c\underset{\sim}{k}\), we use the magnitude formula: \(\|\vec{v}\| = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}\).

steps.title: Identify the components
steps.bodyLatex: Given the vector \(-\underset{\sim}{i} + 18\underset{\sim}{j} - 6\underset{\sim}{k}\), we identify the components as \(a = -1\), \(b = 18\), and \(c = -6\).

steps.title: Apply the magnitude formula
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the components into the formula: \(\|\vec{v}\| = \sqrt{(-1)^2 + (18)^2 + (-6)^2}\).

steps.title: Sum and find the square root
steps.bodyLatex: Add the values together: \(1 + 324 + 36 = 361\). Finally, calculate the square root: \(\|\vec{v}\| = \sqrt{361} = 19\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to square the negative signs, leading to incorrect values like \(-1 + 324 - 36\).
Confusing the magnitude formula with the vector components themselves.
Arithmetic errors when squaring \(18\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the result by squaring the answer: \(19^2 = 361\), which matches the sum of the squares of the components.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the Conjugate Root Theorem for polynomials with real coefficients.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Since the coefficients \(p\) and \(q\) are real, if \(z = 3 + i\) is a root, its complex conjugate \(z = 3 - i\) must also be a root. We can then find \(p\) and \(q\) by using the sum and product of roots for a quadratic equation \(z^2 + pz + q = 0\).

steps.title: Identify the second root
steps.bodyLatex: According to the Conjugate Root Theorem, because the coefficients \(p\) and \(q\) are real, the complex conjugate of \(3 + i\), which is \(3 - i\), must also be a root of the equation.

steps.title: Use the sum of roots
steps.bodyLatex: For a quadratic equation \(z^2 + pz + q = 0\), the sum of the roots is equal to \(-p\). Therefore, \((3 + i) + (3 - i) = -p\), which simplifies to \(6 = -p\), so \(p = -6\).

steps.title: Use the product of roots
steps.bodyLatex: The product of the roots is equal to \(q\). Therefore, \((3 + i)(3 - i) = q\). Expanding this using the difference of two squares, we get \(3^2 - i^2 = 9 - (-1) = 10\), so \(q = 10\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that the conjugate must also be a root, leading to incorrect values for \(p\) and \(q\).
Making a sign error when calculating the sum of roots, resulting in \(p = 6\) instead of \(p = -6\).
Miscalculating the product \((3+i)(3-i)\) by forgetting that \(i^2 = -1\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Substitute \(p = -6\) and \(q = 10\) back into the quadratic: \((z - (3+i))(z - (3-i)) = z^2 - z(3-i+3+i) + (3+i)(3-i) = z^2 - 6z + 10 = 0\). This matches the given form.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to convert a vector equation of a line in two dimensions into its Cartesian form.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We express the vector equation as a pair of parametric equations for \( x \) and \( y \). We then eliminate the parameter \( \lambda \) to find the relationship between \( x \) and \( y \).

steps.title: Write the parametric equations
steps.bodyLatex: From the vector equation \( r = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix} \), we can equate the components: \[ x = 1 - 2\lambda \] \[ y = 3 + 4\lambda \]

steps.title: Eliminate the parameter
steps.bodyLatex: Rearrange the equation for \( x \) to solve for \( \lambda \): \[ \lambda = \frac{1 - x}{2} \] Substitute this expression for \( \lambda \) into the equation for \( y \): \[ y = 3 + 4\left( \frac{1 - x}{2} \right) \]

steps.title: Simplify to Cartesian form
steps.bodyLatex: Simplify the expression: \[ y = 3 + 2(1 - x) \] \[ y = 3 + 2 - 2x \] \[ y = 5 - 2x \] Rearranging this gives \( y + 2x = 5 \), which matches option C.

commonMistakesLatex: Sign errors when rearranging the parametric equations for \( \lambda \).
Incorrectly substituting the expression for \( \lambda \) into the wrong variable.
Errors in algebraic expansion when simplifying the final equation.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by testing the point \( (1, 3) \) from the original vector equation: \( 3 + 2(1) = 5 \), which is correct. Also, the direction vector \( \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix} \) has a gradient of \( m = \frac{4}{-2} = -2 \); the line \( y = -2x + 5 \) also has a gradient of \( -2 \).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the polar form of complex numbers, specifically how operations like squaring and division by a modulus affect the argument and modulus of a complex number.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Represent \(z\) in polar form as \(z = r(\cos \theta + i \sin \theta) = re^{i\theta}\). Then, simplify the expression \(\frac{z^2}{|z|}\) using the properties of exponents and moduli to determine the new modulus and argument.

steps.title: Express z in polar form
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(z = r e^{i\theta}\), where \(r = |z|\) is the distance from the origin and \(\theta = \arg(z)\) is the angle with the positive real axis. From the diagram, we see that \(0 < \theta < \frac{\pi}{4}\) and \(r > 1\) (since the point lies outside the unit circle).

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.bodyLatex:
We substitute \(z = re^{i\theta}\) into the expression: 
\[ \frac{z^2}{|z|} = \frac{(re^{i\theta})^2}{r} = \frac{r^2 e^{i2\theta}}{r} = r e^{i2\theta} \]

steps.title: Analyze the result
steps.bodyLatex: The new complex number has a modulus of \(r\) and an argument of \(2\theta\). Since the modulus remains \(r\), the point stays on the same circle as \(z\). Since the argument is \(2\theta\), the angle is doubled, moving the point further counter-clockwise.

steps.title: Select the correct diagram
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing the options, diagram A shows a point on the same circle as \(z\) but with a larger angle, consistent with an argument of \(2\theta\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the modulus of the result with the modulus of \(z^2\), which would be \(r^2\).
Failing to recognize that dividing by \(|z|\) keeps the point on the same circle as \(z\).
Incorrectly doubling the modulus instead of the argument.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: If \(z = 2e^{i(\pi/6)}\), then \(|z|=2\). The expression gives \(\frac{(2e^{i\pi/6})^2}{2} = \frac{4e^{i\pi/3}}{2} = 2e^{i\pi/3}\). The modulus remains 2, and the angle doubles from \(30^\circ\) to \(60^\circ\), which matches the geometric transformation seen in diagram A.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the relationships between amplitude, angular frequency, maximum velocity, and maximum acceleration in simple harmonic motion (SHM).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the standard formulas for maximum velocity \( v_{max} = nA \) and maximum acceleration \( a_{max} = n^2A \), where \( n \) is the angular frequency and \( A \) is the amplitude. By dividing these two expressions, we can isolate \( n \) and then calculate the period \( T = \frac{2\pi}{n} \).

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.bodyLatex: We are given \( a_{max} = n^2A = 6 \) and \( v_{max} = nA = 4 \).

steps.title: Solve for the angular frequency
steps.bodyLatex: Dividing the acceleration equation by the velocity equation gives: \[ \frac{n^2A}{nA} = \frac{6}{4} \] which simplifies to \( n = 1.5 \) or \( n = \frac{3}{2} \).

steps.title: Calculate the period
steps.bodyLatex: The period \( T \) is given by the formula \( T = \frac{2\pi}{n} \). Substituting \( n = \frac{3}{2} \), we get: \[ T = \frac{2\pi}{3/2} = 2\pi \times \frac{2}{3} = \frac{4\pi}{3} \].

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the formulas for maximum velocity (\( nA \)) and maximum acceleration (\( n^2A \)).
Forgetting to convert the angular frequency \( n \) into the period \( T \) using \( T = \frac{2\pi}{n} \).
Algebraic errors when dividing the two equations.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: If \( n = 1.5 \) and \( A = v_{max}/n = 4/1.5 = 8/3 \), then \( a_{max} = n^2A = (1.5)^2 \times (8/3) = 2.25 \times 2.666... = 6 \). The values match the question requirements.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to integrate functions of the form \( \int \frac{1}{x^2+bx+c} dx \) by completing the square and using the standard inverse tangent integral result.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, complete the square for the quadratic expression in the denominator. Then, apply the standard integral formula \( \int \frac{1}{u^2 + a^2} du = \frac{1}{a} \tan^{-1}(\frac{u}{a}) + c \).

steps.title: Complete the square
steps.bodyLatex: We rewrite the denominator \( x^2 + 4x + 10 \) by completing the square: \( x^2 + 4x + 4 + 6 = (x+2)^2 + 6 \). Thus, the integral becomes \( \int \frac{1}{(x+2)^2 + 6} dx \).

steps.title: Apply the standard integral formula
steps.bodyLatex: We use the form \( \int \frac{1}{u^2 + a^2} du = \frac{1}{a} \tan^{-1}(\frac{u}{a}) + c \), where \( u = x + 2 \) and \( a^2 = 6 \), so \( a = \sqrt{6} \). Substituting these values, we get \( \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \tan^{-1}(\frac{x+2}{\sqrt{6}}) + c \).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to divide by the constant \( a = \sqrt{6} \) outside the inverse tangent function.
Mistaking the integral for a logarithmic form, which only occurs when the denominator has real roots (i.e., when the discriminant is positive).
Incorrectly completing the square by miscalculating the constant term.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Differentiating the result \( \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \tan^{-1}(\frac{x+2}{\sqrt{6}}) \) using the chain rule yields \( \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + (\frac{x+2}{\sqrt{6}})^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} = \frac{1}{6(1 + \frac{(x+2)^2}{6})} = \frac{1}{6 + (x+2)^2} = \frac{1}{x^2+4x+10} \), which confirms the answer is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of logical propositions and the concept of a counterexample in mathematical proofs.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: A proposition of the form 'If \(P\), then \(Q\)' is disproven by finding a counterexample where \(P\) is true but \(Q\) is false. Here, we need a case where \(2^n - 1\) is not prime (composite), but \(n\) is prime.

steps.title: Analyze the proposition
steps.bodyLatex: The proposition states: If \(2^n - 1\) is not prime, then \(n\) is not prime. To disprove this, we must find a case where the antecedent '(\(2^n - 1\) is not prime)' is true, but the consequent '(\(n\) is not prime)' is false. This means we are looking for a prime number \(n\) such that \(2^n - 1\) is composite.

steps.title: Evaluate the options
steps.bodyLatex: Option A and C involve cases where \(2^n - 1\) is prime, which does not satisfy the condition 'if \(2^n - 1\) is not prime'. Option B involves \(n=6\), which is not prime, so it does not contradict the implication. Option D involves \(n=11\), which is a prime number. Since \(2^{11} - 1 = 2047 = 23 \times 89\), it is not prime. Thus, for \(n=11\), the condition 'not prime' is satisfied, but \(n\) is prime, which disproves the statement.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the proposition with its converse or inverse.
Failing to identify that a counterexample requires the 'if' part to be true and the 'then' part to be false.
Miscalculating the primality of \(n\) or \(2^n - 1\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: We check \(n=11\): \(11\) is prime. \(2^{11}-1 = 2047\). Since \(2047 / 23 = 89\), \(2^{11}-1\) is composite. This perfectly satisfies the requirements for a counterexample.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of logical negation, specifically applying the rule for negating a conditional statement within a broader logical structure.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: The statement is of the form 'If \(P\), then (\(Q \implies R\))'. To negate a conditional statement 'If \(P\), then \(S\)', we use the logical equivalence that the negation is '\(P\) and (not \(S\))'.

steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(P\) be '\(n\) is even', \(Q\) be '\(n\) is a multiple of 3', and \(R\) be '\(n\) is a multiple of 6'. The original statement is \(P \implies (Q \implies R)\).

steps.title: Apply negation rules
steps.bodyLatex:
The negation of \(P \implies S\) is \(P \land (
eg S)\). Here, \(S\) is the statement \(Q \implies R\). The negation of \(Q \implies R\) is \(Q \land (
eg R)\).

steps.title: Combine the components
steps.bodyLatex:
Substituting these back, the negation is \(P \land (Q \land 
eg R)\). This translates to: '\(n\) is even' AND ('\(n\) is a multiple of 3' AND '\(n\) is not a multiple of 6').

commonMistakesLatex: Negating the 'If' part of the statement instead of keeping it as the first part of the conjunction.

Confusing the negation of an implication \(Q \implies R\) with \(
eg Q \implies 
eg R\), which is a common logical fallacy.
Failing to recognize that the entire statement is a conditional, leading students to try and negate the inner implication while ignoring the outer structure.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: A statement is false if there exists a counterexample. The negation must describe exactly when the original statement is false. The original statement is false only when \(n\) is even, is a multiple of 3, but is not a multiple of 6 (e.g., \(n=3\) is not even, but \(n=12\) is a multiple of 6, so we look for \(n=6k+3\) where \(n\) is even, which is impossible, but logically the structure holds).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the geometric interpretation of complex numbers in the Argand plane, specifically the sum of distances from a point on the unit circle to two fixed points.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: The expression \(|e^{i\theta} - 2| + |e^{i\theta} + 2|\) represents the sum of the distances from a point \(z = e^{i\theta}\) on the unit circle to the points \(z_1 = 2\) and \(z_2 = -2\). This is the definition of an ellipse with foci at \(2\) and \(-2\).

steps.title: Geometric Interpretation
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(z = e^{i\theta}\). The expression represents the sum of distances from \(z\) to the points \(2\) and \(-2\) on the real axis. Since \(|z| = 1\), the point \(z\) lies on the unit circle.

steps.title: Identify the Extremes
steps.bodyLatex: The distance sum is maximized when \(z\) is as far as possible from the origin along the imaginary axis. The points on the unit circle that are furthest from the foci \((2, 0)\) and \((-2, 0)\) are \(z = i\) and \(z = -i\).

steps.title: Calculate the Maximum Value
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute \(z = i\) into the expression: \(|i - 2| + |i + 2|\). Since \(|i - 2| = \sqrt{(-2)^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{5}\) and \(|i + 2| = \sqrt{2^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{5}\). The sum is \(\sqrt{5} + \sqrt{5} = 2\sqrt{5}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Mistaking the expression for the distance between the two points rather than the sum of distances to them.
Assuming the maximum occurs at the real intercepts \(z = 1\) or \(z = -1\), which actually provides the minimum value.
Attempting to use algebraic expansion with \(e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta\), which becomes unnecessarily complicated.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For \(\theta = 0\), the value is \(|1-2| + |1+2| = 1 + 3 = 4\). For \(\theta = \pi/2\), the value is \(2\sqrt{5} \approx 4.47\). Since \(2\sqrt{5} > 4\), our result is consistent with the geometry of an ellipse.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests the properties of definite integrals, specifically the symmetry property of integrals over a symmetric interval.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the substitution method to split the integral into two parts and show that the integral from \(0\) to \(2a\) is equivalent to the sum of the function and its reflection about \(a\).

steps.title: Split the integral
steps.bodyLatex: We can split the integral \(\int_{0}^{2a} f(x) dx\) at the midpoint \(a\): \(\int_{0}^{2a} f(x) dx = \int_{0}^{a} f(x) dx + \int_{a}^{2a} f(x) dx\).

steps.title: Substitute in the second integral
steps.bodyLatex: For the second integral, let \(u = 2a - x\). Then \(du = -dx\). When \(x = a\), \(u = a\), and when \(x = 2a\), \(u = 0\). Substituting these values gives \(\int_{a}^{2a} f(x) dx = \int_{a}^{0} f(2a - u) (-du) = \int_{0}^{a} f(2a - u) du\).

steps.title: Combine the results
steps.bodyLatex: Since the variable of integration is a dummy variable, we can replace \(u\) with \(x\). Thus, \(\int_{0}^{2a} f(x) dx = \int_{0}^{a} f(x) dx + \int_{0}^{a} f(2a - x) dx\), which simplifies to \(\int_{0}^{a} (f(x) + f(2a - x)) dx\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the property \(\int_{0}^{a} f(x) dx = \int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) dx\) with the interval \(2a\).
Incorrectly changing the limits of integration during the substitution process.
Assuming the function is symmetric or even, which is not stated in the question.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Test with \(f(x) = x\). The integral \(\int_{0}^{2a} x dx = [x^2/2]_{0}^{2a} = 2a^2\). Option B gives \(\int_{0}^{a} (x + 2a - x) dx = \int_{0}^{a} 2a dx = [2ax]_{0}^{a} = 2a^2\). Both match.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests fundamental skills in complex numbers, integration by parts, differential equations involving kinematics, vector geometry, and solving quadratic equations with complex coefficients.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use standard definitions for modulus and conjugates, the integration by parts formula \(\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du\), the chain rule for acceleration \(a = v \frac{dv}{dx}\), the dot product condition for perpendicular vectors (\(\underset{\sim}{a} \cdot \underset{\sim}{b} = 0\)), and the quadratic formula.

steps.title: (a)(i) Evaluate the modulus
steps.bodyLatex: For a complex number \(w = a + bi\), the modulus is defined as \(|w| = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}\). Given \(w = -1 + 4i\), we have \(|w| = \sqrt{(-1)^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{1 + 16} = \sqrt{17}\).

steps.title: (a)(ii) Evaluate the product
steps.bodyLatex: The conjugate of \(z = 2 - i\) is \(\bar{z} = 2 + i\). We calculate the product \(w\bar{z} = (-1 + 4i)(2 + i) = -2 - i + 8i + 4i^2\). Since \(i^2 = -1\), this simplifies to \(-2 + 7i - 4 = -6 + 7i\).

steps.title: (b) Integration by parts
steps.bodyLatex: Using \(\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du\), let \(u = \ln x\) and \(dv = x \, dx\). Then \(du = \frac{1}{x} \, dx\) and \(v = \frac{x^2}{2}\). The integral becomes \(\left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \ln x \right]_1^e - \int_1^e \frac{x^2}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{x} \, dx = \left( \frac{e^2}{2} \ln e - 0 \right) - \int_1^e \frac{x}{2} \, dx = \frac{e^2}{2} - \left[ \frac{x^2}{4} \right]_1^e = \frac{e^2}{2} - (\frac{e^2}{4} - \frac{1}{4}) = \frac{e^2}{4} + \frac{1}{4}\).

steps.title: (c) Solve for displacement
steps.bodyLatex: Using \(a = v \frac{dv}{dx} = v^2 + v\), we separate variables: \(\frac{v \, dv}{v(v+1)} = dx\), which simplifies to \(\frac{dv}{v+1} = dx\). Integrating both sides gives \(\ln(v+1) = x + C\). At the origin \(x=0\), the velocity is \(v=1\), so \(0 = \ln(1+1) + C \implies C = -\ln 2\). Thus, \(x = \ln(v+1) - \ln 2\).

steps.title: (d) Vector orthogonality
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(\underset{\sim}{A} = \underset{\sim}{u} - \underset{\sim}{v} = (-2-p)i - 2j + 1k\) and \(\underset{\sim}{B} = \underset{\sim}{u} + \underset{\sim}{v} = (p-2)i + 0j + 5k\). For perpendicular vectors, \(\underset{\sim}{A} \cdot \underset{\sim}{B} = 0\), so \((-2-p)(p-2) + (-2)(0) + (1)(5) = 0\). This simplifies to \(-(p^2 - 4) + 5 = 0\), so \(p^2 = 9\), giving \(p = \pm 3\).

steps.title: (e) Solve the quadratic
steps.bodyLatex: Using the quadratic formula \(z = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}\), the discriminant is \(D = 3^2 - 4(1)(3-i) = 9 - 12 + 4i = -3 + 4i\). Let \((x+iy)^2 = -3 + 4i\), so \(x^2 - y^2 = -3\) and \(2xy = 4\). Solving these gives \(x=1, y=2\) (or \(x=-1, y=-2\)), so \(\sqrt{D} = \pm(1+2i)\). Then \(z = \frac{-3 \pm (1+2i)}{2}\), yielding \(z = -1+i\) and \(z = -2-i\).

commonMistakesLatex: In part (b), failing to correctly identify \(u\) and \(dv\) leads to a more complex integral.
In part (c), forgetting to use the chain rule \(a = v \frac{dv}{dx}\) prevents the separation of variables.
In part (d), sign errors when expanding the dot product of vectors with algebraic components.
In part (e), incorrectly calculating the square root of the complex discriminant.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (e), check the sum of roots: \((-1+i) + (-2-i) = -3\), which matches \(-b/a = -3/1\). Check the product: \((-1+i)(-2-i) = 2 + i - 2i - i^2 = 3-i\), which matches \(c/a = 3-i\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply Newton's Laws of Motion to resolve forces on a body and to derive and analyze the trajectory of a projectile.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), we resolve forces into horizontal and vertical components to find the normal force and net force, then use Newton's second law to find acceleration and integrate for velocity. For part (b), we integrate the acceleration vector twice to find the position vector, eliminate time to find the Cartesian path, and use the discriminant of the resulting quadratic to prove the existence of two distinct launch angles.

steps.title: (a)(i) Resolve vertical forces
steps.bodyLatex: The vertical forces acting on the box are the normal force \( R \) upwards, the vertical component of the pulling force \( 200 \sin 30^\circ \) upwards, and the weight \( mg = 50 \times 10 = 500 \) downwards. Setting the sum of vertical forces to zero (since the box does not move vertically): \( R + 200 \sin 30^\circ - 500 = 0 \). Thus, \( R = 500 - 200(0.5) = 400 \text{ N} \).

steps.title: (a)(ii) Resolve horizontal forces
steps.bodyLatex: The net horizontal force is the horizontal component of the pull minus the resistive force: \( F_{net} = 200 \cos 30^\circ - 0.3R \). Substituting \( R = 400 \): \( F_{net} = 200 \left( \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) - 0.3(400) = 100\sqrt{3} - 120 \approx 173.2 - 120 = 53.2 \text{ N} \).

steps.title: (a)(iii) Find velocity
steps.bodyLatex: Using \( F = ma \), we have \( 50a = 53.2 \), so \( a = 1.064 \text{ m/s}^2 \). Since \( a = \frac{dv}{dt} \), we integrate: \( v = \int_0^3 1.064 \, dt = 1.064 \times 3 = 3.192 \text{ m/s} \).

steps.title: (b)(i) Derive position vector
steps.bodyLatex: Given \( \underset{\sim}{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ -g \end{pmatrix} \), integrating once gives velocity \( \underset{\sim}{v} = \begin{pmatrix} c_1 \\ -gt + c_2 \end{pmatrix} \). Using initial conditions \( \underset{\sim}{v}(0) = \begin{pmatrix} u \cos \theta \\ u \sin \theta \end{pmatrix} \), we get \( \underset{\sim}{v} = \begin{pmatrix} u \cos \theta \\ u \sin \theta - gt \end{pmatrix} \). Integrating again with \( \underset{\sim}{r}(0) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \) yields \( \underset{\sim}{r}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} ut \cos \theta \\ ut \sin \theta - \frac{1}{2}gt^2 \end{pmatrix} \).

steps.title: (b)(ii) Cartesian equation
steps.bodyLatex: From \( x = ut \cos \theta \), we have \( t = \frac{x}{u \cos \theta} \). Substituting this into \( y = ut \sin \theta - \frac{1}{2}gt^2 \) gives \( y = x \tan \theta - \frac{gx^2}{2u^2 \cos^2 \theta} \). Using \( \frac{1}{\cos^2 \theta} = 1 + \tan^2 \theta \), we obtain \( y = x \tan \theta - \frac{gx^2}{2u^2}(1 + \tan^2 \theta) \).

steps.title: (b)(iii) Prove 2 distinct angles
steps.bodyLatex: At \( x = R \), \( y = 0 \), so \( 0 = R \tan \theta - \frac{gR^2}{2u^2}(1 + \tan^2 \theta) \). Rearranging into a quadratic in \( \tan \theta \): \( \frac{gR^2}{2u^2} \tan^2 \theta - R \tan \theta + \frac{gR^2}{2u^2} = 0 \). The discriminant is \( \Delta = (-R)^2 - 4 \left( \frac{gR^2}{2u^2} \right) \left( \frac{gR^2}{2u^2} \right) = R^2 - \frac{g^2 R^4}{u^4} = R^2 \left( 1 - \frac{g^2 R^2}{u^4} \right) \). Since \( u^2 > gR \), then \( u^4 > g^2 R^2 \), making \( \frac{g^2 R^2}{u^4} < 1 \), so \( \Delta > 0 \), implying two distinct real roots for \( \tan \theta \).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: See individual steps for derivations; (a)(iii) \( 3.192 \text{ m/s} \).
commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to include the vertical component of the pulling force in the normal force calculation.
Incorrectly using \( g=9.8 \) instead of the given \( g=10 \text{ m/s}^2 \).
Algebraic errors when substituting \( t \) into the trajectory equation, particularly with the \( \cos^2 \theta \) term.
Failing to recognize the quadratic form in \( \tan \theta \) for part (b)(iii).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a)(i), check \( 400 + 200(0.5) = 500 = 50(10) \). For (b)(iii), if \( u^2 = gR \), then \( \Delta = 0 \), which corresponds to the maximum range case where there is only one optimal angle.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to model simple harmonic motion, solve systems of linear equations in 3D, prove algebraic inequalities, and apply De Moivre's Theorem to trigonometric integration.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), use the standard SHM form \( x(t) = A \cos(nt) + x_0 \). For (b), equate components to solve for parameters. For (c), use the provided geometry and substitution. For (d), expand using the binomial theorem and use the identity \( e^{i\theta} + e^{-i\theta} = 2\cos\theta \).

steps.title: (a) Model the SHM displacement
steps.bodyLatex: The general form is \( x(t) = A \cos(nt) + x_0 \). The central point is \( x_0 = \sqrt{3} \) and the amplitude \( A = 2\sqrt{3} \). Given the period \( T = \frac{\pi}{3} \), we find \( n = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/3} = 6 \). Since the particle is at maximum displacement at \( t=0 \), the cosine function requires no phase shift, so \( x(t) = 2\sqrt{3} \cos(6t) + \sqrt{3} \).

steps.title: (b) Find the point of intersection
steps.bodyLatex: Equating the components of the two lines gives: \( 3 + \lambda_1 = 3 - 2\lambda_2 \) (1), \( -1 + 2\lambda_1 = -6 + \lambda_2 \) (2), and \( 7 + \lambda_1 = 2 + 3\lambda_2 \) (3). From (1), \( \lambda_1 = -2\lambda_2 \). Substituting into (2): \( -1 + 2(-2\lambda_2) = -6 + \lambda_2 \), which simplifies to \( -1 - 4\lambda_2 = -6 + \lambda_2 \), so \( 5\lambda_2 = 5 \) and \( \lambda_2 = 1 \). Then \( \lambda_1 = -2 \). Checking in (3): \( 7 + (-2) = 5 \) and \( 2 + 3(1) = 5 \), which is consistent. Substituting \( \lambda_2 = 1 \) into the second line gives the point \( \begin{pmatrix} 3-2 \\ -6+1 \\ 2+3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -5 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} \).

steps.title: (c)(i) Prove the inequality
steps.bodyLatex: Using Pythagoras on the triangle, \( x^2 + (a-b)^2 = (a+b)^2 \). Thus \( x^2 = (a+b)^2 - (a-b)^2 = (a^2 + 2ab + b^2) - (a^2 - 2ab + b^2) = 4ab \), so \( x = 2\sqrt{ab} \). Since the hypotenuse \( a+b \) is the longest side, \( a+b \ge x \), hence \( a+b \ge 2\sqrt{ab} \), which simplifies to \( \frac{a+b}{2} \ge \sqrt{ab} \).

steps.title: (c)(ii) Prove the secondary inequality
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute \( a = p^2 \) and \( b = (2q)^2 \) into the result from (i): \( \frac{p^2 + 4q^2}{2} \ge \sqrt{p^2 \cdot 4q^2} = 2pq \). Multiplying by 2 gives \( p^2 + 4q^2 \ge 4pq \).

steps.title: (d)(i) Show the identity
steps.bodyLatex: Using Euler's formula, \( e^{in\theta} + e^{-in\theta} = (\cos(n\theta) + i\sin(n\theta)) + (\cos(n\theta) - i\sin(n\theta)) = 2\cos(n\theta) \).

steps.title: (d)(ii) Expand and show the identity
steps.bodyLatex: Expanding \( (e^{i\theta} + e^{-i\theta})^4 \) using the binomial theorem: \( e^{4i\theta} + 4e^{2i\theta} + 6 + 4e^{-2i\theta} + e^{-4i\theta} = (e^{4i\theta} + e^{-4i\theta}) + 4(e^{2i\theta} + e^{-2i\theta}) + 6 \). Using the result from (d)(i), this is \( 2\cos(4\theta) + 8\cos(2\theta) + 6 \). Since \( (e^{i\theta} + e^{-i\theta})^4 = (2\cos\theta)^4 = 16\cos^4\theta \), we have \( 16\cos^4\theta = 2\cos(4\theta) + 8\cos(2\theta) + 6 \), which simplifies to \( \cos^4\theta = \frac{1}{8}(\cos(4\theta) + 4\cos(2\theta) + 3) \).

steps.title: (d)(iii) Integrate
steps.bodyLatex: We evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \frac{1}{8}(\cos(4\theta) + 4\cos(2\theta) + 3) d\theta = \frac{1}{8} [ \frac{1}{4}\sin(4\theta) + 2\sin(2\theta) + 3\theta ]_{0}^{\pi/2} \). Substituting the limits: \( \frac{1}{8} [ (0 + 0 + \frac{3\pi}{2}) - (0 + 0 + 0) ] = \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{3\pi}{2} = \frac{3\pi}{16} \).

commonMistakesLatex: In part (a), confusing the period formula \( T = 2\pi/n \) and incorrectly calculating \( n=6 \).
In part (b), failing to check the intersection point in the third equation or forgetting to substitute the parameter back into the line equation.
In part (d)(ii), expanding the binomial incorrectly or forgetting that \( (2\cos\theta)^4 = 16\cos^4\theta \), not \( 8\cos^4\theta \).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a), check \( x(0) = 2\sqrt{3}(1) + \sqrt{3} = 3\sqrt{3} \), which is the max displacement from center. For (b), verify the point satisfies both line equations.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply complex number geometry, solve differential equations for motion in a resisting medium, perform mathematical induction, and use proof by contradiction.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), use the geometric interpretation of multiplication by \( e^{i\theta} \). For (b), use separation of variables and partial fractions. For (c), follow the standard induction steps. For (d), use proof by contradiction assuming a rational form.

steps.title: (a)(i) Geometry of complex numbers
steps.bodyLatex: Multiplying \( z_1 \) by \( e^{i\pi/3} \) rotates the vector \( OA \) anticlockwise by \( \pi/3 \) without changing its length, so \( |z_1| = |z_2| \). Since \( OA = OB \) and the angle \( \angle AOB = \pi/3 \), the triangle \( OAB \) is isosceles with an apex angle of \( \pi/3 \). Therefore, the base angles are \( (\pi - \pi/3)/2 = \pi/3 \), making the triangle equilateral.

steps.title: (a)(ii) Algebraic proof
steps.bodyLatex: Given \( z_2 = e^{i\pi/3} z_1 \), cubing both sides gives \( z_2^3 = e^{i\pi} z_1^3 = -z_1^3 \), so \( z_1^3 + z_2^3 = 0 \). Factoring the sum of cubes, we get \( (z_1 + z_2)(z_1^2 - z_1 z_2 + z_2^2) = 0 \). Since \( z_1 \neq -z_2 \) (as the angle between them is \( \pi/3 \)), we must have \( z_1^2 + z_2^2 - z_1 z_2 = 0 \), which implies \( z_1^2 + z_2^2 = z_1 z_2 \).

steps.title: (b) Motion in a resisting medium
steps.bodyLatex: Start with \( \frac{dv}{dt} = 10(1 - (0.01v)^2) = 10(1 - 0.0001v^2) = 10 \frac{10000 - v^2}{10000} = \frac{10000 - v^2}{1000} \). Separating variables, \( \int \frac{dv}{100^2 - v^2} = \int \frac{1}{1000} dt \). Using partial fractions, \( \frac{1}{200} \int (\frac{1}{100-v} + \frac{1}{100+v}) dv = \frac{t}{1000} + C \). Integrating gives \( \frac{1}{200} \ln|\frac{100+v}{100-v}| = \frac{t}{1000} + C \). With \( v(0)=0 \), \( C=0 \). At \( t=5 \), \( \ln(\frac{100+v}{100-v}) = 1 \), so \( \frac{100+v}{100-v} = e \). Solving for \( v \) yields \( v = 100 \frac{e-1}{e+1} \).

steps.title: (c) Mathematical induction
steps.bodyLatex: For \( n=2 \), \( 1/4 < 1/2 \) is true. Assume \( \sum_{j=2}^k \frac{1}{j^2} < \frac{k-1}{k} \). Then for \( n=k+1 \), the sum is \( < \frac{k-1}{k} + \frac{1}{(k+1)^2} = \frac{(k-1)(k+1)^2 + k}{k(k+1)^2} = \frac{k^3+k^2-1}{k(k+1)^2} \). Since \( k^3+k^2-1 < k^3+k^2 \), the sum is \( < \frac{k^2(k+1)}{k(k+1)^2} = \frac{k}{k+1} \). The result holds for \( n=k+1 \), thus for all \( n \ge 2 \).

steps.title: (d) Proof by contradiction
steps.bodyLatex: Suppose \( \log_n(n+1) = p/q \) where \( p, q \in \mathbb{Z}^+ \). Then \( n+1 = n^{p/q} \), so \( (n+1)^q = n^p \). If \( n \) is even, \( (n+1)^q \) is odd while \( n^p \) is even, a contradiction. If \( n \) is odd, \( (n+1)^q \) is even while \( n^p \) is odd, also a contradiction. Thus, \( \log_n(n+1) \) must be irrational.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a)(i) Equilateral triangle; (a)(ii) Proof; (b) \( v = 100\frac{e-1}{e+1} \approx 46.2 \text{ m/s} \); (c) Proof; (d) Proof.
commonMistakesLatex: In (a)(ii), attempting to use the cosine rule or treating complex numbers as real numbers.
In (b), forgetting the chain rule factor or the negative sign when integrating \( \ln(1-kv) \).
In (c), failing to show the inequality step clearly when moving from \( k \) to \( k+1 \).
In (d), not clearly addressing the parity (even/odd) argument for both possible cases of \( n \).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (b), as \( t \to \infty \), \( v \to 100 \text{ m/s} \), which matches the terminal velocity where \( a=0 \). For (c), check \( n=3 \): \( 1/4 + 1/9 = 13/36 \approx 0.36 \), while \( (3-1)/3 = 2/3 \approx 0.66 \), and \( 0.36 < 0.66 \) holds.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to construct logical proofs involving divisibility and to apply vector geometry to prove properties of parallelograms.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), use algebraic factorisation and case analysis. For part (b), use vector addition, the section formula, and the intersection of lines method by equating vector parameters.

steps.title: (a)(i) Prove P is true
steps.bodyLatex: If \(k+1\) is divisible by \(3\), then \(k+1 = 3j\) for some integer \(j\). We can factorise \(k^3+1\) as \((k+1)(k^2-k+1)\). Substituting \(k+1 = 3j\), we get \(k^3+1 = 3j(k^2-k+1)\), which is clearly a multiple of \(3\).

steps.title: (a)(ii) Contrapositive of P
steps.bodyLatex: The contrapositive of 'If \(p\), then \(q\)' is 'If not \(q\), then not \(p\)'. Thus, the contrapositive is: 'If \(k^3+1\) is not divisible by \(3\), then \(k+1\) is not divisible by \(3\)'.

steps.title: (a)(iii) Converse of P
steps.bodyLatex: The converse is: 'If \(k^3+1\) is divisible by \(3\), then \(k+1\) is divisible by \(3\)'. This is true. If we test cases \(k \equiv 0, 1, 2 \pmod{3}\), we find \(k^3+1 \equiv 1, 2, 0 \pmod{3}\) respectively. Only when \(k \equiv 2 \pmod{3}\) is \(k^3+1\) divisible by \(3\), which corresponds to \(k+1 \equiv 3 \equiv 0 \pmod{3}\).

steps.title: (b)(i) Show AC vector
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(C\) divides \(AB\) in ratio \(n:m\), \(\vec{AC} = \frac{n}{m+n} \vec{AB}\). Given \(\vec{AB} = \vec{b} - \vec{a}\), it follows that \(\vec{AC} = \frac{n}{m+n}(\vec{b} - \vec{a})\).

steps.title: (b)(ii) Show OC vector
steps.bodyLatex: \(\vec{OC} = \vec{OA} + \vec{AC} = \vec{a} + \frac{n}{m+n}(\vec{b} - \vec{a}) = \vec{a}(1 - \frac{n}{m+n}) + \frac{n}{m+n}\vec{b} = \frac{m}{m+n}\vec{a} + \frac{n}{m+n}\vec{b}\).

steps.title: (b)(iii) Show OT vector
steps.bodyLatex: In parallelogram \(OPQR\), \(\vec{OS} = \vec{OR} + \vec{RS} = \vec{r} + \frac{1}{2}\vec{p}\). Any point on \(OS\) is \(\lambda(\vec{r} + \frac{1}{2}\vec{p})\) and on \(PR\) is \((1-\mu)\vec{p} + \mu\vec{r}\). Equating coefficients of \(\vec{p}\) and \(\vec{r}\) gives \(\frac{1}{2}\lambda = 1-\mu\) and \(\lambda = \mu\). Solving gives \(\lambda = \frac{2}{3}\), so \(\vec{OT} = \frac{2}{3}\vec{r} + \frac{1}{3}\vec{p}\).

steps.title: (b)(iv) Ratio of division
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing \(\vec{OT} = \frac{1}{3}\vec{p} + \frac{2}{3}\vec{r}\) with the section formula \(\vec{OC} = \frac{m}{m+n}\vec{a} + \frac{n}{m+n}\vec{b}\) where \(\vec{a}=\vec{p}\) and \(\vec{b}=\vec{r}\), we see \(m=1\) and \(n=2\). Thus, \(T\) divides \(PR\) in the ratio \(2:1\) (from \(R\) to \(P\)).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: The proofs are completed as shown in the steps.
commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the converse and contrapositive statements.
Incorrectly applying the section formula by swapping the ratios \(m\) and \(n\).
Failing to express the intersection point \(T\) using two independent parameters for the lines \(OS\) and \(PR\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a)(iii), check \(k=2\): \(k^3+1 = 9\) (divisible by 3) and \(k+1=3\) (divisible by 3). For (b)(iv), check the ratio \(2:1\) using the section formula: \(\frac{1(p) + 2(r)}{1+2} = \frac{1}{3}p + \frac{2}{3}r\), which matches \(\vec{OT}\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply Newton's Second Law to a system of connected particles and evaluate definite integrals using reduction formulas and substitution.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), use the system equation \( F_{net} = ma_{total} \). For part (b), use integration by parts for the reduction formula, and trigonometric substitution for the integral relationship.

steps.title: (a)(i) Equation of Motion
steps.bodyLatex: The total mass of the system is \( 2m + 4m = 6m \). The driving force is the weight of the larger mass \( 4mg \), while the opposing forces are the weight of the smaller mass \( 2mg \) and the resistance \( kv \) on each mass (total \( 2kv \)). Applying \( F_{net} = ma \): \[ 4mg - 2mg - 2kv = 6m \frac{dv}{dt} \] Simplifying gives \( 2mg - 2kv = 6m \frac{dv}{dt} \), which reduces to \( \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{gm - kv}{3m} \).

steps.title: (a)(ii) Solving for Velocity
steps.bodyLatex: Separate variables: \( \frac{dv}{gm - kv} = \frac{1}{3m} dt \). Integrating both sides from \( t=0, v=0 \) to \( t, v \): \[ -\frac{1}{k} \ln(gm - kv) \Big|_0^v = \frac{t}{3m} \implies \ln\left(\frac{gm}{gm - kv}\right) = \frac{kt}{3m} \] Substituting \( t = \frac{3m}{k} \ln 2 \), we get \( \ln\left(\frac{gm}{gm - kv}\right) = \ln 2 \). Thus, \( \frac{gm}{gm - kv} = 2 \), which implies \( gm = 2gm - 2kv \), so \( 2kv = gm \), or \( v = \frac{gm}{2k} \).

steps.title: (b)(i) Reduction Formula
steps.bodyLatex: Write \( I_n = \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^{2n-1}(2\theta) \sin(2\theta) d\theta \). Using integration by parts with \( u = \sin^{2n}(2\theta) \) and \( dv = \sin(2\theta) d\theta \), we find \( du = 4n \sin^{2n-1}(2\theta) \cos(2\theta) d\theta \) and \( v = -\frac{1}{2}\cos(2\theta) \). The boundary term vanishes, leaving \( I_n = 2n \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^{2n-1}(2\theta) \cos^2(2\theta) d\theta = 2n \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^{2n-1}(2\theta)(1 - \sin^2(2\theta)) d\theta \). This yields \( I_n = 2n(I_{n-1} - I_n) \), so \( I_n(2n+1) = 2n I_{n-1} \).

steps.title: (b)(ii) Deduce \( I_n \)
steps.bodyLatex: Iterating the formula gives \( I_n = \frac{2n}{2n+1} \cdot \frac{2n-2}{2n-1} \dots \frac{2}{3} I_0 \). Since \( I_0 = \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin(2\theta) d\theta = [-\frac{1}{2}\cos(2\theta)]_0^{\pi/2} = 1 \), we have \( I_n = \frac{(2n)(2n-2)\dots(2)}{(2n+1)(2n-1)\dots(3)} = \frac{2^n n! \cdot 2^n n!}{(2n+1)!} = \frac{2^{2n} (n!)^2}{(2n+1)!} \).

steps.title: (b)(iii) Evaluate \( J_n \)
steps.bodyLatex: Let \( x = \sin^2 \theta \), so \( dx = 2\sin\theta \cos\theta d\theta = \sin(2\theta) d\theta \). Then \( J_n = \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^{2n}\theta \cos^{2n}\theta \sin(2\theta) d\theta = \int_0^{\pi/2} (\frac{1}{2}\sin(2\theta))^{2n} \sin(2\theta) d\theta = \frac{1}{2^{2n}} I_n \). Substituting \( I_n \), we get \( J_n = \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \cdot \frac{2^{2n} (n!)^2}{(2n+1)!} = \frac{(n!)^2}{(2n+1)!} \).

steps.title: (b)(iv) Inequality Proof
steps.bodyLatex: The function \( f(x) = x(1-x) \) has a maximum value of \( \frac{1}{4} \) at \( x = \frac{1}{2} \). Thus, \( x^n(1-x)^n \le (\frac{1}{4})^n \). Integrating both sides from \( 0 \) to \( 1 \): \( J_n \le \int_0^1 (\frac{1}{4})^n dx = \frac{1}{4^n} \). Substituting \( J_n \), we get \( \frac{(n!)^2}{(2n+1)!} \le \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \), which rearranges to \( (2^n n!)^2 \le (2n+1)! \).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to include the resistance force on both masses in part (a)(i).
Incorrectly identifying the total mass of the system as just the larger mass.
Errors in applying integration by parts or forgetting the constant of integration in (a)(ii).
Algebraic errors when simplifying the factorial expressions in (b)(ii).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a)(ii), check \( v(0) = 0 \) and \( v \to gm/k \) as \( t \to \infty \). For (b), test \( n=1 \): \( I_1 = \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^3(2\theta) d\theta = 2/3 \), and formula gives \( 2^2(1!)^2/3! = 4/6 = 2/3 \).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to expand a binomial expression involving complex numbers using the algebraic identity \( (a - b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2 \).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We expand the expression \( (3 - 2i)^2 \) using the binomial expansion formula, remembering the fundamental property of complex numbers that \( i^2 = -1 \).

steps.title: Expand the binomial
steps.bodyLatex: Using the identity \( (a - b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2 \), we substitute \( a = 3 \) and \( b = 2i \) into the formula: \[ (3 - 2i)^2 = 3^2 - 2(3)(2i) + (2i)^2 \]

steps.title: Simplify the terms
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate each part individually: \[ 3^2 = 9 \] \[ 2(3)(2i) = 12i \] \[ (2i)^2 = 4i^2 \]

steps.title: Apply the property of \( i^2 \)
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute \( i^2 = -1 \) into the expression: \[ 9 - 12i + 4(-1) = 9 - 12i - 4 \]

steps.title: Combine real parts
steps.bodyLatex: Subtract the real numbers to reach the final form: \[ 9 - 4 - 12i = 5 - 12i \]

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that \( i^2 = -1 \), leading to the incorrect result of \( 9 - 12i + 4 = 13 - 12i \).
Incorrectly squaring the term \( 2i \) as \( 2i^2 \) instead of \( 4i^2 \).
Making a sign error during the expansion of the middle term.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: You can verify this by multiplying the brackets directly: \( (3 - 2i)(3 - 2i) = 9 - 6i - 6i + 4i^2 = 9 - 12i - 4 = 5 - 12i \).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to identify and evaluate an indefinite integral using the method of substitution.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We can rewrite the expression using trigonometric identities to make the integration straightforward. By letting \( u = \cos x \), we can transform the integral into a simple power rule problem.

steps.title: Rewrite the integral
steps.bodyLatex: The integral is given by \( I = \int \frac{\sin x}{\cos^3 x} \, dx \). We can rewrite this as \( I = \int \sin x (\cos x)^{-3} \, dx \).

steps.title: Use substitution
steps.bodyLatex: Let \( u = \cos x \). Then, the derivative is \( \frac{du}{dx} = -\sin x \), which means \( du = -\sin x \, dx \) or \( -du = \sin x \, dx \).

steps.title: Evaluate the integral
steps.bodyLatex: Substituting these into the integral gives \( I = \int -u^{-3} \, du \). Applying the power rule for integration, we get \( I = -\left( \frac{u^{-2}}{-2} \right) + C = \frac{1}{2} u^{-2} + C \).

steps.title: Substitute back and simplify
steps.bodyLatex: Since \( u = \cos x \), we have \( I = \frac{1}{2} (\cos x)^{-2} + C = \frac{1}{2} \sec^2 x + C \). This matches option A.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the negative sign when differentiating \( \cos x \), leading to the wrong sign in the final answer.
Incorrectly applying the power rule by adding 1 to the exponent of \( -3 \) and getting \( -4 \) instead of \( -2 \).
Confusing the derivative of \( \sec x \) with the integral of \( \sec^2 x \).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: To verify, differentiate the answer: \( \frac{d}{dx} (\frac{1}{2} \sec^2 x) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 \sec x \cdot (\sec x \tan x) = \sec^2 x \tan x = \frac{1}{\cos^2 x} \cdot \frac{\sin x}{\cos x} = \frac{\sin x}{\cos^3 x} \). The derivative matches the original integrand.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to perform integration by parts to evaluate the integral of a product of two functions.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the integration by parts formula: \( \int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du \). By choosing \( u = x \) and \( dv = \cos x \, dx \), we can simplify the integral.

steps.title: Identify parts
steps.bodyLatex: Let \( u = x \), which implies \( du = dx \). Let \( dv = \cos x \, dx \), which implies \( v = \int \cos x \, dx = \sin x \).

steps.title: Apply the formula
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute these into the formula \( \int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du \): \[ \int x \cos x \, dx = x \sin x - \int \sin x \, dx \]

steps.title: Evaluate the remaining integral
steps.bodyLatex: The integral of \( \sin x \) is \( -\cos x \). Therefore: \[ x \sin x - (-\cos x) + C = x \sin x + \cos x + C \]

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the negative sign in the integration by parts formula.
Incorrectly integrating \( \sin x \) as \( \cos x \) instead of \( -\cos x \).
Mixing up the signs when subtracting the integral of \( v \, du \).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Differentiate the result \( x \sin x + \cos x + C \) using the product rule on \( x \sin x \): \( (1)(\sin x) + (x)(\cos x) + (-\sin x) = x \cos x \). This matches the original integrand.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the relationship between the roots of a cubic polynomial and its coefficients using Vieta's formulas.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Since the polynomial is a cubic with roots \(1\) and \(-3\), and one is a double root, we consider two cases for the set of roots. We then use the sum of roots formula, \(\alpha + \beta + \gamma = -\frac{b}{a}\), where \(a=2\), to find the possible values for \(b\).

steps.title: Case 1: The root \(1\) is the double root
steps.bodyLatex: If \(1\) is the double root, the roots are \(1, 1, -3\). The sum of the roots is \(1 + 1 + (-3) = -1\). Using Vieta's formula, the sum is \(-\frac{b}{2}\), so \(-\frac{b}{2} = -1\), which gives \(b = 2\).

steps.title: Case 2: The root \(-3\) is the double root
steps.bodyLatex: If \(-3\) is the double root, the roots are \(1, -3, -3\). The sum of the roots is \(1 + (-3) + (-3) = -5\). Using Vieta's formula, \(-\frac{b}{2} = -5\), which gives \(b = 10\).

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing our calculated values \(b=2\) and \(b=10\) with the given options, we see that \(10\) is listed as option D.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that a double root counts twice when calculating the sum of roots.
Confusing the sign in Vieta's formula for the sum of roots, which is \(-\frac{b}{a}\).
Failing to consider both possible cases for which root is the double root.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For \(b=10\), the polynomial is \(2x^3 + 10x^2 + cx + d\). With roots \(1, -3, -3\), the polynomial is \(2(x-1)(x+3)^2 = 2(x-1)(x^2+6x+9) = 2(x^3 + 5x^2 + 3x - 9) = 2x^3 + 10x^2 + 6x - 18\). This matches the form \(2x^3 + bx^2 + cx + d\) with \(b=10\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the geometric definition of an ellipse in the Argand plane.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recall that the locus of points \( z \) satisfying \( |z - z_1| + |z - z_2| = 2a \) represents an ellipse with foci at \( z_1 \) and \( z_2 \). We compare the given equation to this standard form.

steps.title: Identify the foci
steps.bodyLatex: The given equation is \( |z - 2| + |z - (-1)| = 7 \). By comparing this to the standard form \( |z - z_1| + |z - z_2| = 2a \), we identify the foci at \( z_1 = 2 \) and \( z_2 = -1 \).

steps.title: Interpret the coordinates
steps.bodyLatex: In the Argand plane, the complex numbers \( 2 \) and \( -1 \) correspond to the coordinates \( (2, 0) \) and \( (-1, 0) \) respectively.

steps.title: Determine the shape
steps.bodyLatex: Since the equation is a sum of distances equal to a constant (where the constant \( 7 \) is greater than the distance between the foci, which is \( 3 \)), the locus is an ellipse. A difference of distances would represent a hyperbola, but a sum represents an ellipse.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the definition of an ellipse (sum of distances) with a hyperbola (difference of distances).
Incorrectly identifying the foci by failing to account for the signs in the expression \( |z - z_1| \).
Assuming the constant \( 7 \) represents the distance between the foci rather than the length of the major axis.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The foci are the points where the distance sum is minimized; for \( |z - 2| + |z + 1| \), the sum is minimized when \( z \) lies between \( -1 \) and \( 2 \), confirming the foci are at \( -1 \) and \( 2 \).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret the properties of a curve defined by an implicit equation involving absolute values and trigonometric functions.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We analyze the symmetry and domain of the equation \(y^2 = 2 \sin |x|\). By considering the constraints on \(y\) and the periodic nature of the sine function, we can eliminate incorrect graphs.

steps.title: Analyze the domain and range
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(y^2 = 2 \sin |x|\), we must have \(2 \sin |x| \geq 0\). This implies \(\sin |x| \geq 0\), which occurs when \(|x|\) is in the intervals \([0, \pi], [2\pi, 3\pi], \dots\). This means the graph exists for \(x \in [-\pi, \pi], [-3\pi, -2\pi], [2\pi, 3\pi]\), etc.

steps.title: Examine symmetry
steps.bodyLatex: The equation contains \(|x|\) and \(y^2\). Replacing \(x\) with \(-x\) leaves the equation unchanged, so the graph is symmetric about the \(y\)-axis. Replacing \(y\) with \(-y\) also leaves the equation unchanged, so the graph is symmetric about the \(x\)-axis.

steps.title: Determine the shape
steps.bodyLatex: For \(x \in [0, \pi]\), we have \(y = \pm \sqrt{2 \sin x}\). At \(x = 0\) and \(x = \pi\), \(y = 0\). At \(x = \pi/2\), \(y = \pm \sqrt{2} \approx \pm 1.41\). This creates two mirrored 'lobes' extending along the \(x\)-axis between \(0\) and \(\pi\), and symmetrically between \(-\pi\) and \(0\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting the symmetry about the \(x\)-axis caused by the \(y^2\) term.
Incorrectly assuming the graph exists for all x, ignoring the requirement that sin|x| must be non-negative.
Confusing the graph of \(y = 2 \sin|x|\) with the graph of \(y^2 = 2 \sin|x|\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Test a point: at \(x = \pi/2\), \(y^2 = 2 \sin(\pi/2) = 2\), so \(y = \pm \sqrt{2}\). The graph must pass through \((\pi/2, \sqrt{2})\) and \((\pi/2, -\sqrt{2})\), which is consistent with option B.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to compare the values of definite integrals over the interval \( [0, \frac{\pi}{4}] \) by observing the behavior of the integrand functions.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We evaluate each integral or compare the functions \( f(x) \) directly on the interval \( [0, \frac{\pi}{4}] \). Since \( 0 \le \tan x \le 1 \) for \( x \in [0, \frac{\pi}{4}] \), we can determine which function is largest.

steps.title: Analyze the interval
steps.bodyLatex: On the interval \( [0, \frac{\pi}{4}] \), the function \( \tan x \) increases from \( 0 \) to \( 1 \). Consequently, \( \tan^2 x \le \tan x \le 1 \).

steps.title: Evaluate the integrals
steps.bodyLatex: Let \( I_A = \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \tan x \, dx = [-\ln(\cos x)]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} = \ln(\sqrt{2}) \approx 0.347 \). For \( I_B = \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \tan^2 x \, dx \), we use \( \tan^2 x = \sec^2 x - 1 \), giving \( [\tan x - x]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} = 1 - \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.215 \).

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing the values: \( I_D \approx 0.571 \), \( I_C \approx 0.438 \), \( I_A \approx 0.347 \), and \( I_B \approx 0.215 \). Thus, \( I_D \) is the largest.

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming that the integral of a function with a larger maximum value is always larger without checking the area under the curve.
Errors in integrating \( \tan^2 x \) by forgetting the identity \( \tan^2 x = \sec^2 x - 1 \).
Miscalculating the value of \( \ln(\cos(\frac{\pi}{4})) \) as a negative value instead of \( \ln(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln 2 \).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since \( 1 - \tan^2 x \ge 1 - \tan x \) on the interval \( [0, \frac{\pi}{4}] \) because \( \tan^2 x \le \tan x \), the integral of \( 1 - \tan^2 x \) must be greater than the integral of \( 1 - \tan x \).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to manipulate complex numbers in polar form or algebraic form to solve equations involving conjugates.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We can express \(z\) in polar form as \(z = r(\cos \theta + i \sin \theta)\). By substituting this into the equation \(z^2 = -i\bar{z}\), we can equate the moduli and the arguments to find the possible values for \(r\) and \(\theta\).

steps.title: Express the equation in polar form
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(z = r(\cos \theta + i \sin \theta)\). Then \(z^2 = r^2(\cos 2\theta + i \sin 2\theta)\). The conjugate is \(\bar{z} = r(\cos \theta - i \sin \theta)\), and since \(-i = \cos(-\frac{\pi}{2}) + i \sin(-\frac{\pi}{2})\), the right side is \(-i\bar{z} = r(\cos(\theta - \frac{\pi}{2}) + i \sin(\theta - \frac{\pi}{2}))\).

steps.title: Equate moduli and arguments
steps.bodyLatex: Equating the moduli gives \(r^2 = r\). Since \(z=0\) is a trivial solution not listed, we have \(r = 1\). Equating the arguments gives \(2\theta = \theta - \frac{\pi}{2} + 2k\pi\), which simplifies to \(\theta = -\frac{\pi}{2} + 2k\pi = \frac{3\pi}{2} + 2k\pi\).

steps.title: Identify the correct option
steps.bodyLatex: For \(k=0\), \(\theta = -\frac{\pi}{2}\), so \(z = \cos(-\frac{\pi}{2}) + i \sin(-\frac{\pi}{2}) = -i\). Checking the options, we see they involve \(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\) and \(\frac{1}{2}\). Let's test option C: \(z = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} - \frac{1}{2}i\). Here, \(|z| = 1\), and the argument is \(-\frac{\pi}{6}\). Substituting into the original equation: \(z^2 = (\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} - \frac{1}{2}i)^2 = \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4} - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i\). Also, \(-i\bar{z} = -i(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}i) = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i - \frac{1}{2}i^2 = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i\). Since both sides match, C is correct.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that \(-i\) has an argument of \(-\frac{\pi}{2}\) or \(\frac{3\pi}{2}\).
Confusing the conjugate \(\bar{z}\) with the reciprocal or the negative of \(z\).
Assuming \(r=1\) without checking if other values are possible.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by substituting the chosen value back into the original equation: \(z^2 = -i\bar{z}\). For option C, \((\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} - \frac{1}{2}i)^2 = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i\), which equals \(-i(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}i)\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the relationship between the derivative of a function and the derivative of its inverse function.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Use the formula for the derivative of an inverse function: \( g'(x) = \frac{1}{f'(g(x))} \). By observing the range and behavior of \( f'(x) \), you can determine the domain and behavior of \( g'(x) \).

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.bodyLatex: Given \( g(x) = f^{-1}(x) \), the derivative is \( g'(x) = \frac{1}{f'(g(x))} \). This means the value of \( g'(x) \) is the reciprocal of the value of \( f' \) evaluated at the corresponding \( y \)-value of the original function.

steps.title: Analyze the given graph of \( f'(x) \)
steps.bodyLatex: The graph of \( f'(x) \) shows that \( f'(x) \) is always positive, has a horizontal asymptote at some positive value \( L > 0 \) as \( x \to -\infty \), and approaches \( 0 \) as \( x \to \infty \).

steps.title: Determine the behavior of \( g'(x) \)
steps.bodyLatex: Since \( f'(x) \) is always positive, \( g'(x) = \frac{1}{f'(g(x))} \) must also be positive. As \( x \to \infty \), \( f(x) \to \infty \), so \( g(x) \to \infty \). As \( g(x) \to \infty \), \( f'(g(x)) \to 0 \), meaning \( g'(x) = \frac{1}{f'(g(x))} \to \infty \). Conversely, as \( x \to -\infty \), \( g(x) \to -\infty \), so \( f'(g(x)) \to L \), meaning \( g'(x) \to \frac{1}{L} \).

steps.title: Select the correct graph
steps.bodyLatex: We look for a graph that is always positive, has a horizontal asymptote at \( y = \frac{1}{L} > 0 \) on the left, and increases towards infinity on the right. Graph D matches this behavior perfectly.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the derivative of the inverse with the reciprocal of the derivative, i.e., thinking \( g'(x) = f'(x) \).
Failing to account for the transformation of the domain and range when inverting the function.
Misinterpreting the horizontal asymptote behavior of the reciprocal function.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the limits: if \( f'(x) \to 0^+ \), then \( g'(x) = 1/f' \to +\infty \). If \( f'(x) \to L \), then \( g'(x) \to 1/L \). Graph D is the only one showing a horizontal asymptote on the left and growth on the right.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to use combinatorial counting techniques to determine the number of arrangements under specific constraints.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We first choose the two distinct digits, then determine the number of ways to arrange them into a 4-digit code such that both digits appear at least once.

steps.title: Step 1: Choose the two digits
steps.bodyLatex: We need to select \(2\) distinct digits from the \(10\) available digits (\(0-9\)). The number of ways to choose these is given by the combination formula: \(\binom{10}{2} = \frac{10 \times 9}{2} = 45\).

steps.title: Step 2: Arrange the digits
steps.bodyLatex: For a chosen pair of digits (e.g., \(x\) and \(y\)), each of the \(4\) positions in the code can be filled by either \(x\) or \(y\). This gives \(2^4 = 16\) total possible sequences. However, we must exclude the sequences that consist of only one type of digit (either all \(x\) or all \(y\)). Thus, there are \(16 - 2 = 14\) valid arrangements for each pair.

steps.title: Step 3: Calculate the total
steps.bodyLatex: To find the total number of access codes, multiply the number of ways to choose the digits by the number of valid arrangements: \(45 \times 14 = 630\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to subtract the two cases where only one digit is used (e.g., \(3333\) or \(7777\)).
Treating the order of the chosen digits as significant during the selection phase, leading to double counting.
Incorrectly assuming that the digits must appear an equal number of times.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: We can verify by considering the patterns: 3-of-a-kind (\(\binom{2}{1} \times \binom{4}{3} \times 45 = 360\)) plus 2-of-a-kind (\(\binom{4}{2} \times 45 = 270\)). Summing these gives \(360 + 270 = 630\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests fundamental skills in complex numbers, the geometry of ellipses, and standard techniques for integration, including completing the square and partial fractions.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We will use algebraic manipulation for complex numbers, standard ellipse properties involving eccentricity, and integration techniques like completing the square for inverse trigonometric forms and partial fraction decomposition for logarithmic forms.

steps.title: (a)(i) Complex Addition
steps.bodyLatex: Given \( z = 1 + 3i \) and \( w = 2 - i \), the conjugate is \( \bar{w} = 2 + i \). Thus, \( z + \bar{w} = (1 + 3i) + (2 + i) = 3 + 4i \).

steps.title: (a)(ii) Complex Division
steps.bodyLatex: To divide, multiply by the conjugate of the denominator: \( \frac{z}{w} = \frac{1+3i}{2-i} \times \frac{2+i}{2+i} = \frac{2 + i + 6i + 3i^2}{4 - i^2} \). Since \( i^2 = -1 \), this simplifies to \( \frac{2 + 7i - 3}{4 + 1} = \frac{-1 + 7i}{5} = -\frac{1}{5} + \frac{7}{5}i \).

steps.title: (b) Ellipse Properties
steps.bodyLatex: For \( \frac{x^2}{4} + y^2 = 1 \), we have \( a^2 = 4 \) (so \( a=2 \)) and \( b^2 = 1 \). Using \( b^2 = a^2(1-e^2) \), we get \( 1 = 4(1-e^2) \), so \( e^2 = \frac{3}{4} \) and \( e = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \). The foci are at \( (\pm ae, 0) = (\pm \sqrt{3}, 0) \) and directrices are \( x = \pm \frac{a}{e} = \pm \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}/2} = \pm \frac{4}{\sqrt{3}} \).

steps.title: (c) Integration by Completing the Square
steps.bodyLatex: Rewrite the denominator: \( x^2 + 10x + 29 = (x+5)^2 + 4 \). The integral becomes \( \int \frac{dx}{(x+5)^2 + 2^2} \). Using the formula \( \int \frac{du}{u^2 + a^2} = \frac{1}{a} \tan^{-1}(\frac{u}{a}) + c \), we obtain \( \frac{1}{2} \tan^{-1}(\frac{x+5}{2}) + c \).

steps.title: (d) Integration by Partial Fractions
steps.bodyLatex: Decompose the integrand: \( \frac{6}{x^2-9} = \frac{6}{(x-3)(x+3)} = \frac{A}{x-3} + \frac{B}{x+3} \). Solving \( A(x+3) + B(x-3) = 6 \), we find \( A=1 \) and \( B=-1 \). Integrating gives \( \int (\frac{1}{x-3} - \frac{1}{x+3}) dx = \ln|x-3| - \ln|x+3| + c \).

steps.title: (e)(i) Modulus-Argument Form
steps.bodyLatex: For \( z = -1 + i\sqrt{3} \), the modulus is \( r = \sqrt{(-1)^2 + (\sqrt{3})^2} = 2 \). The argument \( \theta \) satisfies \( \cos\theta = -1/2 \) and \( \sin\theta = \sqrt{3}/2 \), placing \( \theta \) in the second quadrant at \( \frac{2\pi}{3} \). Thus, \( z = 2(\cos\frac{2\pi}{3} + i\sin\frac{2\pi}{3}) \).

steps.title: (e)(ii) De Moivre's Theorem
steps.bodyLatex: Using \( z^n = r^n(\cos(n\theta) + i\sin(n\theta)) \), we have \( z^3 = 2^3(\cos(3 \times \frac{2\pi}{3}) + i\sin(3 \times \frac{2\pi}{3})) = 8(\cos(2\pi) + i\sin(2\pi)) \). Since \( \cos(2\pi)=1 \) and \( \sin(2\pi)=0 \), \( z^3 = 8(1 + 0i) = 8 + 0i \).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to change the sign of the imaginary part when finding the conjugate in part (a).
Incorrectly identifying the eccentricity formula for an ellipse or confusing the positions of foci and directrices.
Neglecting the absolute value signs in the logarithmic result of the partial fraction integral.
Errors in determining the quadrant for the argument of a complex number.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify complex results by re-multiplying, check the derivative of the integral results to recover the original functions, and ensure the ellipse sketch matches the calculated intercepts \( (\pm 2, 0) \) and \( (0, \pm 1) \).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to sketch regions in the complex plane, apply the cosine rule to related rates problems, calculate volumes using cylindrical shells, and sketch graphs of rational and absolute value functions.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), identify the boundaries as rays and a horizontal line. For (b), use the cosine rule for geometry and implicit differentiation for rates. For (c), set up the integral for volume by shells \( V = 2\pi \int x f(x) dx \). For (d), use properties of the function \( f(x) = x^3 - 1 \) to determine asymptotes and intercepts.

steps.title: (a) Sketching the region
steps.bodyLatex: The condition \( \frac{\pi}{4} \le \arg(z) \le \frac{\pi}{2} \) defines a wedge between the line \( y = x \) (for \( x > 0 \)) and the positive imaginary axis. The condition \( \text{Im}(z) \le 1 \) adds a horizontal boundary at \( y = 1 \). Shade the region bounded by these lines, noting an open circle at the origin.

steps.title: (b)(i) Proving the relation
steps.bodyLatex: Apply the cosine rule to the triangle formed by \( A \), \( B \), and \( P \): \( 70^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy \cos(\frac{2\pi}{3}) \). Since \( \cos(\frac{2\pi}{3}) = -\frac{1}{2} \), the equation simplifies to \( 70^2 = x^2 + y^2 + xy \).

steps.title: (b)(ii) Finding the rate of change
steps.bodyLatex: Differentiate \( x^2 + xy + y^2 = 70^2 \) with respect to \( t \): \( 2x \frac{dx}{dt} + (x \frac{dy}{dt} + y \frac{dx}{dt}) + 2y \frac{dy}{dt} = 0 \). Substitute \( x=30 \), \( y=50 \), and \( \frac{dy}{dt} = 4 \): \( 60 \frac{dx}{dt} + 30(4) + 50 \frac{dx}{dt} + 100(4) = 0 \). Solving for \( \frac{dx}{dt} \) gives \( 110 \frac{dx}{dt} = -520 \), so \( \frac{dx}{dt} = -\frac{52}{11} \approx -4.7 \text{ m s}^{-1} \).

steps.title: (c) Volume by cylindrical shells
steps.bodyLatex: The volume of a shell is \( 2\pi x D(x) dx \). Integrating from \( 10 \) to \( 40 \): \( V = 2\pi A \int_{10}^{40} x e^{-kx} dx \). Using integration by parts with \( u=x \) and \( dv=e^{-kx} dx \), we get \( V = 2A\pi [-\frac{x}{k}e^{-kx} - \frac{1}{k^2}e^{-kx}]_{10}^{40} \), which simplifies to the final expression.

steps.title: (d) Sketching graphs
steps.bodyLatex: For (i) \( |x^3-1| \), reflect the negative part of \( x^3-1 \) across the x-axis. For (ii) \( \frac{1}{x^3-1} \), note the vertical asymptote at \( x=1 \) and horizontal asymptote at \( y=0 \). For (iii) \( \frac{x}{x^3-1} \), the curve passes through the origin, has a vertical asymptote at \( x=1 \), and approaches \( y=0 \) as \( x \to \pm \infty \).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to use the product rule when differentiating xy with respect to t in (b)(ii).
Incorrectly assuming the velocity of train B is negative; it is moving away from P, so dy/dt is positive.
Neglecting the open circle at the origin in the complex plane sketch for (a).
Errors in signs during the integration by parts in (c).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (b)(ii), check the sign: as y increases, x must decrease to keep the cable length constant, so a negative result for dx/dt is expected.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to manipulate coordinate geometry on hyperbolas, calculate volumes using cross-sections, and solve projectile motion problems involving time delays.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), use implicit differentiation to find tangents and algebraic manipulation to show collinearity. For (b), integrate the area of cross-sectional trapezia. For (c), use the standard equations of motion and solve the resulting system of equations for the second object's velocity and angle.

steps.title: (a)(i) Tangent Equation
steps.bodyLatex: Differentiating \( \frac{x^2}{a^2} - \frac{y^2}{b^2} = 1 \) gives \( \frac{2x}{a^2} - \frac{2y}{b^2} \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 \), so \( \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{xb^2}{ya^2} \). At \( P(a \sec \theta, b \tan \theta) \), the gradient is \( \frac{b \sec \theta}{a \tan \theta} \). The equation is \( y - b \tan \theta = \frac{b \sec \theta}{a \tan \theta}(x - a \sec \theta) \), which simplifies to \( ay \tan \theta - ab \tan^2 \theta = bx \sec \theta - ab \sec^2 \theta \). Using \( \sec^2 \theta - \tan^2 \theta = 1 \), we get \( bx \sec \theta - ay \tan \theta = ab \).

steps.title: (a)(ii) Intersection Point T
steps.bodyLatex: The tangents at \( P \) and \( Q \) are \( bx \sec \theta - ay \tan \theta = ab \) and \( bx \sec \phi - ay \tan \phi = ab \). Solving for \( y \) in both and equating them: \( \frac{bx \sec \theta - ab}{a \tan \theta} = \frac{bx \sec \phi - ab}{a \tan \phi} \). Multiplying through by \( a \tan \theta \tan \phi \) and rearranging for \( x \) leads to \( x_0(b \tan \phi \sec \theta - b \tan \theta \sec \phi) = ab(\tan \phi - \tan \theta) \), which simplifies to \( x_0 = \frac{a(\tan \theta - \tan \phi)}{\sec \phi \tan \theta - \sec \theta \tan \phi} \).

steps.title: (a)(iii) Collinearity
steps.bodyLatex: The midpoint \( M \) is \( (\frac{a(\sec \theta + \sec \phi)}{2}, \frac{b(\tan \theta + \tan \phi)}{2}) \). The gradient \( m_{OM} = \frac{b(\tan \theta + \tan \phi)}{a(\sec \theta + \sec \phi)} \). The gradient \( m_{OT} = \frac{y_0}{x_0} = \frac{b(\sec \theta - \sec \phi)}{a(\tan \theta - \tan \phi)} \). Multiplying \( m_{OM} \) by \( \frac{\sec \theta - \sec \phi}{\sec \theta - \sec \phi} \) and using \( \sec^2 \theta - \sec^2 \phi = \tan^2 \theta - \tan^2 \phi \) shows \( m_{OM} = m_{OT} \), so \( O, T, M \) are collinear.

steps.title: (b)(i) Area of Trapezium
steps.bodyLatex: The height of the trapezium is \( AB = 1 - x^2 \). The parallel sides are \( AD = 1 \) and \( BC = 1 - (1 - x^2) = x^2 \) (since the face makes \( 45^\circ \) to the base, the vertical height at \( x \) is \( 1-x^2 \), and the distance from the origin to the face is \( 1 \)). The area is \( \frac{1}{2} \times \text{height} \times (AD + BC) = \frac{1}{2}(1 - x^2)(1 + x^2) = \frac{1 - x^4}{2} \).

steps.title: (c)(i) Range of Object 1
steps.bodyLatex: Setting \( y = 0 \) in \( y = -4.9t^2 + 20t \sin(\frac{\pi}{3}) \) gives \( t = \frac{20 \sin(\pi/3)}{4.9} = \frac{10\sqrt{3}}{4.9} \). Substituting into \( x = 20t \cos(\frac{\pi}{3}) \) gives \( x = 20(\frac{10\sqrt{3}}{4.9})(\frac{1}{2}) = \frac{100\sqrt{3}}{4.9} \).

steps.title: (c)(ii) Motion of Object 2
steps.bodyLatex: Object 2 lands at the same time as Object 1, so its flight time is \( T_2 = \frac{10\sqrt{3}}{4.9} - 2 \approx 1.534 \text{ s} \). Using \( y = 0 \), \( 0 = -4.9T_2^2 + VT_2 \sin \theta \implies V \sin \theta = 4.9 T_2 \approx 7.52 \). Using range \( x = VT_2 \cos \theta = \frac{100\sqrt{3}}{4.9} \implies V \cos \theta = \frac{100\sqrt{3}}{4.9 \times 1.534} \approx 23.0 \). Then \( \tan \theta = \frac{7.52}{23.0} \approx 0.327 \implies \theta \approx 18.1^\circ \) and \( V = \frac{7.52}{\sin 18.1^\circ} \approx 24.2 \text{ m/s} \).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a)(i) \( bx \sec \theta - ay \tan \theta = ab \); (a)(ii) \( x_0 = \frac{a(\tan \theta - \tan \phi)}{\sec \phi \tan \theta - \sec \theta \tan \phi} \); (b)(ii) \( \frac{4}{5} \); (c)(ii) \( \theta \approx 18.1^\circ, V \approx 24.2 \text{ m/s} \)
commonMistakesLatex: In (a)(iii), failing to use the identity \( \sec^2 \theta - \sec^2 \phi = \tan^2 \theta - \tan^2 \phi \) to simplify the gradient expression.
In (b)(i), misinterpreting the geometry of the trapezium cross-section relative to the plane inclined at 45 degrees.
In (c)(ii), forgetting that Object 2 is projected 2 seconds later, which reduces its time of flight relative to Object 1.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (b)(ii), the volume of a shape bounded by \( y=1-x^2 \) is generally proportional to the area under the curve; \( 4/5 \) is a reasonable magnitude. For (c)(ii), check that \( V \cos \theta \cdot T_2 \) returns the correct range.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests the application of Newton's laws to circular motion, solving first-order differential equations for kinematics, and proving trigonometric identities using mathematical induction.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), resolve forces into radial and vertical components. For (b), use the definition of terminal velocity and integrate the differential equation for velocity. For (c), use double-angle identities and the principle of mathematical induction.

steps.title: (a) Resolving forces
steps.bodyLatex: The forces acting on the cyclist are gravity \(mg\) downwards and the normal force \(N\) perpendicular to the track. Resolving vertically: \(N \cos \theta = mg\). Resolving horizontally towards the centre: \(N \sin \theta = \frac{mv^2}{r}\). Dividing the horizontal equation by the vertical equation gives \(\tan \theta = \frac{v^2}{gr}\), which simplifies to \(v^2 = gr \tan \theta\).

steps.title: (b)(i) Terminal velocity
steps.bodyLatex: Terminal velocity \(w\) occurs when acceleration \(\ddot{x} = 0\). Setting \(g - kw = 0\), we immediately find \(w = \frac{g}{k}\).

steps.title: (b)(ii) Time of deceleration
steps.bodyLatex: Using \(\ddot{x} = \frac{dv}{dt} = g - kv\), we separate variables: \(\int \frac{dv}{g - kv} = \int dt\). Integrating gives \(-\frac{1}{k} \ln|g - kv| = t + C\). At \(t=0\), \(v = 1.6w = 1.6(\frac{g}{k})\), so \(C = -\frac{1}{k} \ln|g - 1.6g| = -\frac{1}{k} \ln(0.6g)\). Solving for \(t\) when \(v = 1.1w = 1.1(\frac{g}{k})\) gives \(t = \frac{1}{k} \ln|\frac{g - 1.6g}{g - 1.1g}| = \frac{1}{k} \ln|\frac{-0.6g}{-0.1g}| = \frac{1}{k} \ln 6\).

steps.title: (b)(iii) Distance travelled
steps.bodyLatex: Rearranging the velocity equation from (ii) gives \(v(t) = \frac{g}{k}(1 + 0.6e^{-kt})\). Integrating \(v = \frac{dx}{dt}\) with \(x(0)=0\) yields \(x(t) = \frac{g}{k}(t - \frac{0.6}{k}e^{-kt}) + \frac{0.6g}{k^2}\). Substituting \(t = \frac{1}{k} \ln 6\) and using \(e^{-kt} = e^{-\ln 6} = \frac{1}{6}\), we get \(D = \frac{g}{k}(\frac{1}{k} \ln 6 - \frac{0.6}{6k}) + \frac{0.6g}{k^2} = \frac{g}{k^2}(\ln 6 - 0.1 + 0.6) = \frac{g}{k^2}(\ln 6 + 0.5)\).

steps.title: (c)(i) Trigonometric identity
steps.bodyLatex: \(\cot x - \cot 2x = \frac{\cos x}{\sin x} - \frac{\cos 2x}{\sin 2x} = \frac{\cos x \sin 2x - \sin x \cos 2x}{\sin x \sin 2x}\). Using \(\sin(2x - x) = \sin x\), the numerator becomes \(\sin x\). Thus, \(\frac{\sin x}{\sin x \sin 2x} = \frac{1}{\sin 2x} = \csc 2x\).

steps.title: (c)(ii) Mathematical induction
steps.bodyLatex: Base case \(n=1\): \(\csc(2x) = \cot x - \cot 2x\), which is true from (i). Assume true for \(n=k\). For \(n=k+1\), the sum is \(\sum_{r=1}^{k} \csc(2^r x) + \csc(2^{k+1} x) = \cot x - \cot(2^k x) + (\cot(2^k x) - \cot(2^{k+1} x))\). The terms \(\cot(2^k x)\) cancel, leaving \(\cot x - \cot(2^{k+1} x)\). The result holds for \(n=k+1\), hence for all \(n \ge 1\).

commonMistakesLatex: In (a), confusing the angle of banking with the angle of the normal force relative to the vertical.
