steps.bodyLatex: Calculating the expression, we get: \[ FV = 12\,000(1.01)^{20} \approx 14\,642.280... \] Rounding to the nearest cent, the future value is \( \$14\,642.28 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Dividing the interest rate by \( 4 \) again, even though the question specifies the rate is already per quarter.
Using the number of years (\( 5 \)) as \( n \) instead of the total number of quarters (\( 20 \)).
Using the simple interest formula \( I = Prn \) instead of the compound interest formula.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: A quick sanity check: the interest is roughly \( 1\% \times 20 \) periods, which is \( 20\% \) of \( 12\,000 \) (\( 2\,400 \)). Adding this to the principal gives \( 14\,400 \), which is very close to our calculated value of \( 14\,642.28 \), confirming our result is reasonable.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate total earnings by determining hours worked across different time periods and applying specific penalty rates for weekend work.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Calculate the total hours for each pay category (normal, time-and-a-half, and double time) by finding the duration of each shift. Then, multiply these hours by the corresponding pay rate and sum the totals.

steps.title: Step 1: Calculate hours worked Monday to Friday
steps.bodyLatex: Each shift is from \( 8:30 \text{ am} \) to \( 12:30 \text{ pm} \), which is \( 4 \text{ hours} \). Since she works this shift for \( 5 \text{ days} \), the total hours are \( 4 \times 5 = 20 \text{ hours} \). The earnings are \( 20 \times 24.05 = 481.00 \).

steps.title: Step 2: Calculate hours and earnings for Saturday
steps.bodyLatex: The shift from \( 9:00 \text{ am} \) to \( 11:30 \text{ am} \) is \( 2.5 \text{ hours} \). The Saturday rate is time-and-a-half, so the hourly rate is \( 24.05 \times 1.5 = 36.075 \). Total earnings for Saturday are \( 2.5 \times 36.075 = 90.1875 \), which rounds to \( 90.19 \).

steps.title: Step 3: Calculate hours and earnings for Sunday
steps.bodyLatex: The shift from \( 9:00 \text{ am} \) to \( 12:00 \text{ pm} \) (noon) is \( 3 \text{ hours} \). The Sunday rate is double time, so the hourly rate is \( 24.05 \times 2 = 48.10 \). Total earnings for Sunday are \( 3 \times 48.10 = 144.30 \).

steps.title: Step 4: Calculate total weekly earnings
steps.bodyLatex: Add the earnings from all periods together: \( 481.00 + 90.19 + 144.30 = 715.49 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Incorrectly converting time durations (e.g., treating 30 minutes as 0.3 hours instead of 0.5 hours).
Forgetting to multiply the daily weekday hours by the number of days worked (5 days).
Rounding intermediate values too early, which leads to small errors in the final total.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by checking the total hours: \( 20 + 2.5 + 3 = 25.5 \text{ hours} \). The average hourly rate is higher than \( 24.05 \), so a total of \( 715.49 \) is a reasonable result for \( 25.5 \text{ hours} \) of work.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate and compare annual fuel costs based on consumption rates provided per \(100 \text{ km}\).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To solve this, calculate the total annual fuel consumption for each car by dividing the total distance by \(100\) and multiplying by the consumption rate. Then, multiply these totals by their respective unit costs to find the annual cost for each vehicle, and subtract the electric cost from the petrol cost to find the savings.

steps.title: Step 1: Calculate the annual cost for the petrol car
steps.bodyLatex: First, find the total litres used: \((35\,000 \div 100) \times 8.6 = 3\,010 \text{ L}\). Then, multiply by the cost per litre: \(3\,010 \times \$1.87 = \$5\,628.70\).

steps.title: Step 2: Calculate the annual cost for the electric car
steps.bodyLatex: First, find the total kilowatt-hours used: \((35\,000 \div 100) \times 18 = 6\,300 \text{ kWh}\). Then, multiply by the cost per unit: \(6\,300 \times \$0.25 = \$1\,575.00\).

steps.title: Step 3: Calculate the annual savings
steps.bodyLatex: Subtract the electric car's annual cost from the petrol car's annual cost to find the total savings: \(\$5\,628.70 - \$1\,575.00 = \$4\,053.70\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to divide the total distance by 100, as the consumption rate is given per 100 km.
Multiplying the cost by the distance directly without accounting for the fuel consumption rate.
Confusing the units for petrol (L) and electric (kWh) during the calculation process.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the logic by checking the units: \((\text{km} \div 100) \times (\text{units}/100\text{km}) \times (\$/\text{unit}) = \$\). The magnitude of savings is reasonable given the lower cost per unit of electricity.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate compound interest step-by-step using a table and to apply the simple interest formula to a principal amount.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), observe the pattern: the interest rate is calculated by dividing the interest earned by the principal. For part (b), use the simple interest formula \(I = P \times r \times n\).

steps.title: (a) Calculate A
steps.bodyLatex: First, find the monthly interest rate by looking at the first month: \(30.00 \div 5000.00 = 0.006\) or \(0.6\%\). To find \(A\), multiply the Month 4 principal by this rate: \(A = 5090.54 \times 0.006 = 30.54324\). Rounding to the nearest cent, we get \(A = 30.54\).

steps.title: (a) Calculate B
steps.bodyLatex: The balance at the end of the month is the sum of the principal and the interest earned. Therefore, \(B = 5090.54 + 30.54 = 5121.08\).

steps.title: (b) Calculate Simple Interest
steps.bodyLatex: Use the simple interest formula \(I = P \times r \times n\), where \(P = 5000\), \(r = 0.62\% = 0.0062\), and \(n = 4\). Substituting these values gives \(I = 5000 \times 0.0062 \times 4 = 124\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the simple interest formula with the compound interest formula.
Failing to convert the percentage rate (0.62%) into a decimal (0.0062) before calculating.
Rounding too early in the calculation, which leads to an incorrect final balance.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a), check that \(30.54 / 5090.54 \approx 0.006\). For (b), note that \(5000 \times 0.0062 = 31\) per month, and \(31 \times 4 = 124\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply the compound appreciation formula to calculate the future value of an asset over a set period.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Since the artwork increases in value at a percentage rate each year, we use the compound interest formula: \( A = P(1 + r)^n \). We identify the principal value \( P \), the annual rate \( r \), and the number of years \( n \), then substitute these into the formula.

steps.title: Identify the variables
steps.bodyLatex: From the question, we have the principal value \( P = 15000 \), the annual appreciation rate \( r = 5.3\% = 0.053 \), and the time period \( n = 8 \text{ years} \).

steps.title: Substitute into the formula
steps.bodyLatex: Using the formula \( A = P(1 + r)^n \), substitute the known values: \[ A = 15000(1 + 0.053)^8 \]

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.bodyLatex: Perform the calculation: \[ A = 15000(1.053)^8 \] \[ A \approx 15000 \times 1.511565... \] \[ A \approx 22673.482... \]

steps.title: Round to currency
steps.bodyLatex: Since we are dealing with money, round the answer to two decimal places: \[ A = 22673.48 \]

commonMistakesLatex: Mistaking appreciation for simple interest by using the formula \( A = P(1 + rn) \).
Failing to convert the percentage \( 5.3\% \) to the decimal \( 0.053 \), often using \( 0.53 \) instead.
Rounding the intermediate value of \( (1.053)^8 \) too early, which leads to an inaccurate final answer.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: A quick sanity check: since \( 5\% \) of \( 15000 \) is \( 750 \), over \( 8 \) years simple interest would be roughly \( 6000 \), making the total \( 21000 \). Because of compounding, our result of \( 22673.48 \) is logical and slightly higher.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to model linear relationships, interpret graphical data, and compare costs between two different pricing structures.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Use the formula \( \text{Total Charge} = (\text{Rate} \times \text{Usage}) + \text{Fixed Charge} \) to complete the table. Then, plot the points on the provided grid and identify the intersection point to find where costs are equal.

steps.title: (a) Complete the table
steps.bodyLatex: For Provider A, the cost is \( 0.25 \times \text{kWh} + 40 \). For \( 400 \) kWh, the calculation is \( (0.25 \times 400) + 40 = 100 + 40 = 140 \). The missing value in the table is \( 140 \).

steps.title: (b) Graph the charges
steps.bodyLatex: Plot the points \( (0, 40) \), \( (400, 140) \), and \( (1000, 290) \) on the grid. Use a ruler to draw a straight line passing through these points to represent Provider A's costs.

steps.title: (c) Determine equal charges
steps.bodyLatex: Locate the point where the line for Provider A intersects the existing line for Provider B. This intersection occurs at \( 400 \) kWh, meaning both providers charge the same amount at this usage level.

steps.title: (d) Compare costs at 800 kWh
steps.bodyLatex: For Provider B, the cost at \( 800 \) kWh is \( 0.35 \times 800 = 280 \). For Provider A, the cost is \( (0.25 \times 800) + 40 = 200 + 40 = 240 \). Since \( 240 < 280 \), Provider A is cheaper by \( 280 - 240 = 40 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Failing to use a ruler when drawing the straight-line graph.
Miscalculating the cost for Provider A by forgetting to add the fixed monthly charge of \( 40 \).
Identifying the wrong axis when reading the intersection point.
Forgetting to subtract the two costs to find the difference in part (d).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the costs: Provider A at \( 800 \) kWh is \( 0.25 \times 800 + 40 = 240 \). Provider B at \( 800 \) kWh is \( 0.35 \times 800 = 280 \). The difference is indeed \( 40 \).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate time differences between two locations and adjust for a specific event duration.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, determine the time difference between Town A and Town B. Then, calculate the finish time in Town B and subtract the time difference to find the corresponding time in Town A.

steps.title: Step 1: Calculate the time difference
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(10 \text{ am}\) in Town A is \(2 \text{ pm}\) in Town B, we find the difference by counting the hours: \(10 \text{ am} \to 11 \text{ am} \to 12 \text{ pm} \to 1 \text{ pm} \to 2 \text{ pm}\). This is a difference of \(4 \text{ hours}\), meaning Town B is \(4 \text{ hours}\) ahead of Town A.

steps.title: Step 2: Find the finish time in Town B
steps.bodyLatex: The game starts at \(11:30 \text{ am}\) and lasts for \(2 \text{ hours}\). Adding the duration: \(11:30 \text{ am} + 2 \text{ hours} = 1:30 \text{ pm}\) in Town B.

steps.title: Step 3: Convert the finish time to Town A time
steps.bodyLatex: Since Town A is \(4 \text{ hours}\) behind Town B, we subtract \(4 \text{ hours}\) from \(1:30 \text{ pm}\). Subtracting \(1 \text{ hour}\) gets us to \(12:30 \text{ pm}\), and subtracting the remaining \(3 \text{ hours}\) gets us to \(9:30 \text{ am}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Adding the time difference instead of subtracting it.
Forgetting to switch from pm to am when crossing the 12:00 (noon) mark.
Miscalculating the duration between 10 am and 2 pm as 3 hours instead of 4 hours.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: If Town A is \(9:30 \text{ am}\), adding the \(4 \text{ hour}\) difference gives \(1:30 \text{ pm}\) in Town B, which matches the finish time of the game.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret nutrition labels and perform reverse percentage calculations to find a total value.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Since \(6\%\) of the total daily recommended intake equals \(19.1 \text{ g}\), you can set up an equation where \(0.06 \times \text{Total} = 19.1 \text{ g}\). Solve for the total by dividing the serving amount by the percentage.

steps.title: Identify the relevant information
steps.bodyLatex: From the table, locate the row for 'Carbohydrates'. We see that the quantity per serving is \(19.1 \text{ g}\) and this represents \(6\%\) of the daily recommended intake.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(x\) be the total daily recommended intake. We can write the relationship as: \[ 6\% \times x = 19.1 \text{ g} \]

steps.title: Solve for the total intake
steps.bodyLatex: To find \(x\), divide the serving amount by the percentage (expressed as a decimal): \[ x = 19.1 \div 0.06 \] \[ x = 318.333... \text{ g} \]

steps.title: Round to the nearest gram
steps.bodyLatex: The question asks for the answer to the nearest gram. Rounding \(318.333... \text{ g}\) gives \(318 \text{ g}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Multiplying the serving amount by the percentage (e.g., \(19.1 \times 0.06\)) instead of dividing.
Confusing the energy value (\(420 \text{ kJ}\)) with the carbohydrate mass.
Rounding prematurely before reaching the final step of the calculation.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: To verify, calculate \(6\%\) of \(318 \text{ g}\): \(318 \times 0.06 = 19.08 \text{ g}\), which rounds to the \(19.1 \text{ g}\) given in the table.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply right-angled trigonometry and compass bearings to solve problems involving geometric positions.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We identify right-angled triangles within the diagram to use basic trigonometric ratios (SOH CAH TOA). For part (a), we use the angle between the North line and the bearing to find the adjacent side. For part (b), we use inverse trigonometry to find the angle from the South line and add it to the 180-degree bearing of South.

steps.title: (a) Find the distance from X to P
steps.bodyLatex: Since the bearing of \(Y\) from \(X\) is \(120^\circ\), the angle between the South line (\(XP\)) and the line \(XY\) is \(180^\circ - 120^\circ = 60^\circ\). In the right-angled triangle \(XPY\), we have the hypotenuse \(XY = 15 \text{ km}\) and the angle \(\angle PXY = 60^\circ\). Using the cosine ratio, we find: \[ XP = 15 \times \cos(60^\circ) = 15 \times 0.5 = 7.5 \text{ km} \].

steps.title: (b) Find the bearing of C from X
steps.bodyLatex: Consider the right-angled triangle \(XPC\). We know the hypotenuse \(XC = 40 \text{ km}\) and the adjacent side \(XP = 7.5 \text{ km}\). Let \(\theta = \angle CXP\). Using the cosine ratio: \[ \cos(\theta) = \frac{7.5}{40} \implies \theta = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{7.5}{40}\right) \approx 79.12^\circ \].

steps.title: (b) Final bearing calculation
steps.bodyLatex: The line \(XP\) points due South, which is a bearing of \(180^\circ\). Since \(C\) is to the West of \(P\), we add the angle \(\theta\) to the South bearing: \[ \text{Bearing} = 180^\circ + 79.12^\circ = 259.12^\circ \]. Rounding to the nearest degree, we get \(259^\circ\).

commonMistakesLatex: Using the wrong trigonometric ratio (e.g., using sine instead of cosine) when the adjacent side is known.
Misinterpreting the bearing by adding or subtracting from the wrong reference line (North vs South).
Failing to correctly identify the right-angled triangles within the diagram.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a), \(15 \cos(60^\circ)\) is clearly half of 15, which is 7.5. For (b), since 7.5 is much smaller than 40, the angle \(\theta\) must be close to 90 degrees, making the total bearing slightly less than 270 degrees, which matches 259 degrees.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply two different depreciation models—straight-line and declining-balance—to determine the future value of an asset.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We will calculate the salvage value after \(3\) years for both methods using the standard formulas provided in the HSC Reference Sheet and then compare the results to identify which is lower.

steps.title: Step 1: Calculate salvage value using the Straight-line method
steps.bodyLatex: The formula for straight-line depreciation is \(S = V_0 - (D \times n)\), where \(V_0 = 60000\), \(D = 3500\), and \(n = 3\). Substituting these values gives \(S = 60000 - (3500 \times 3) = 60000 - 10500 = 49500\). The salvage value is \(49500\).

steps.title: Step 2: Calculate salvage value using the Declining-balance method
steps.bodyLatex: The formula for declining-balance depreciation is \(S = V_0(1 - r)^n\), where \(V_0 = 60000\), \(r = 0.12\), and \(n = 3\). Calculating this gives \(S = 60000(1 - 0.12)^3 = 60000(0.88)^3\). Evaluating the power first, \(0.88^3 = 0.681472\), so \(S = 60000 \times 0.681472 = 40888.32\).

steps.title: Step 3: Compare results and conclude
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing the two results, \(40888.32 < 49500\). Therefore, the declining-balance method results in a lower salvage value for the equipment after \(3\) years.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: The declining-balance method provides the lower salvage value of \(40888.32\).
commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the straight-line formula with the declining-balance formula.
Failing to convert the percentage rate (12%) into a decimal (0.12) correctly.
Forgetting to subtract the depreciation amount from the initial value in the straight-line method.
Rounding intermediate steps too early, which can lead to an incorrect final value.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the logic by checking that the declining-balance method compounds the depreciation, which typically results in a faster reduction of value compared to the constant annual deduction of the straight-line method.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate the total area of composite shapes from a scale drawing and determine the volume of a prism based on that area and a given depth.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, convert all grid dimensions to actual metres using the scale \(1 \text{ cm} = 2 \text{ m}\). Then, calculate the area of each distinct shape (triangle, rectangle/L-shape, and semicircle) separately, sum them, and multiply by the depth to find the volume.

steps.title: Step 1: Apply the scale
steps.bodyLatex: The scale is \(1 \text{ cm} = 2 \text{ m}\). Since each grid square represents \(1 \text{ cm} \times 1 \text{ cm}\) on the drawing, each grid square represents \(2 \text{ m} \times 2 \text{ m} = 4 \text{ m}^2\) in reality.

steps.title: Step 2: Calculate the area of the triangle
steps.bodyLatex: The triangle has a base of \(4 \text{ cm}\) (\(8 \text{ m}\)) and a height of \(2 \text{ cm}\) (\(4 \text{ m}\)). The area is \(A = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 4 = 16 \text{ m}^2\).

steps.title: Step 3: Calculate the area of the L-shape
steps.bodyLatex: The L-shape is composed of \(15\) grid squares. Since each square is \(4 \text{ m}^2\), the total area is \(15 \times 4 = 60 \text{ m}^2\).

steps.title: Step 4: Calculate the area of the semicircle
steps.bodyLatex: The semicircle has a diameter of \(4 \text{ cm}\), so the radius is \(2 \text{ cm}\), which equals \(4 \text{ m}\). The area is \(A = \frac{1}{2} \times \pi \times r^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times \pi \times 4^2 = 8\pi \approx 25.13 \text{ m}^2\).

steps.title: Step 5: Calculate total volume
steps.bodyLatex: The total area is \(16 + 60 + 25.13 = 101.13 \text{ m}^2\). Given the depth is \(10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}\), the volume is \(V = \text{Area} \times \text{depth} = 101.13 \times 0.1 = 10.113 \text{ m}^3\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to convert the depth from centimetres to metres (using 10 instead of 0.1).
Incorrectly converting the scale: assuming \(1 \text{ cm}^2 = 2 \text{ m}^2\) instead of \(1 \text{ cm}^2 = 4 \text{ m}^2\).
Miscounting the number of grid squares in the L-shape.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ensure all area components are in \(m^2\) and the final volume is in \(m^3\). The sum of areas \(16 + 60 + 25.13 = 101.13\) seems reasonable for the grid size shown.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to convert a large whole number into scientific notation, also known as standard form.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To write a number in standard form, express it as \(a \times 10^n\), where \(1 \le a < 10\) and \(n\) is an integer. Count how many places the decimal point must move to the left to place it immediately after the first non-zero digit.

steps.title: Identify the decimal placement
steps.bodyLatex: The number is \(650\ 000\ 000\). To satisfy the condition \(1 \le a < 10\), we must place the decimal point between the \(6\) and the \(5\), giving us \(a = 6.5\).

steps.title: Count the decimal shifts
steps.bodyLatex: Starting from the end of the number, move the decimal point to the left until it is between the \(6\) and the \(5\). Counting the jumps: \(6, 5, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0\). This requires moving the decimal point \(8\) places to the left.

steps.title: Form the expression
steps.bodyLatex: Since we moved the decimal \(8\) places, the exponent \(n\) is \(8\). Therefore, the number in standard form is \(6.5 \times 10^8\).

commonMistakesLatex: Miscounting the number of zeros or decimal places.
Selecting \(65 \times 10^7\), which is not in standard form because \(65\) is not between \(1\) and \(10\).
Using the wrong exponent by miscalculating the power of \(10\) required to shift the decimal.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: You can verify the answer by calculating \(6.5 \times 10^8 = 6.5 \times 100\ 000\ 000 = 650\ 000\ 000\), which matches the original value.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret a network diagram by identifying and counting the number of edges.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: An edge is a line segment connecting two vertices (dots). To find the total, carefully count each line segment one by one, marking them off as you go to ensure none are missed or double-counted.

steps.title: Identify the edges
steps.bodyLatex: We count the connections between the vertices: \(AB\), \(BC\), \(AC\), \(BF\), \(BG\), \(FG\), \(FD\), \(GD\), \(BD\), and \(DE\).

steps.title: Sum the edges
steps.bodyLatex: Counting these segments, we have: 1 (\(AB\)), 2 (\(BC\)), 3 (\(AC\)), 4 (\(BF\)), 5 (\(BG\)), 6 (\(FG\)), 7 (\(FD\)), 8 (\(GD\)), 9 (\(BD\)), and 10 (\(DE\)). The total number of edges is \(10\).

commonMistakesLatex: Miscounting the edges by missing the curved lines, such as the edge connecting B and D.
Confusing vertices (the dots) with edges (the lines).
Double-counting an edge by starting from the wrong vertex.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by placing a small tick mark on each line as you count it; if you count exactly 10 ticks, your answer is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of probability without replacement, specifically how picking an item changes the total number of outcomes for the next event.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Calculate the remaining number of red lollies and the remaining total number of lollies after the first red lolly is removed. Then, use the probability formula: \( P(\text{Event}) = \frac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Total number of outcomes}} \).

steps.title: Calculate the initial totals
steps.bodyLatex: Initially, there are \( 12 \) red, \( 10 \) black, and \( 13 \) white lollies. The total number of lollies is \( 12 + 10 + 13 = 35 \).

steps.title: Account for the first lolly being eaten
steps.bodyLatex: After Alex eats one red lolly, the number of red lollies remaining is \( 12 - 1 = 11 \). The total number of lollies remaining in the jar is \( 35 - 1 = 34 \).

steps.title: Calculate the probability for the second lolly
steps.bodyLatex: The probability that the second lolly is red is the number of remaining red lollies divided by the new total number of lollies: \( P(\text{Second is Red}) = \frac{11}{34} \).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to subtract the eaten lolly from the total count (resulting in the incorrect denominator of 35).
Forgetting to subtract the eaten lolly from the red lolly count (resulting in the incorrect numerator of 12).
Confusing this with a 'with replacement' scenario where the probabilities would remain unchanged.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since one red lolly is removed, the probability must be slightly less than the original probability of picking a red lolly (\( 12/35 \approx 0.342 \)). Our result \( 11/34 \approx 0.323 \) is lower, which correctly reflects the reduced number of red lollies available.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to substitute values into a given formula and correctly apply the rules for rounding to significant figures.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We will substitute the radius \( r = 3.55 \) into the area formula \( A = \pi r^2 \) and then round the resulting value to three significant figures.

steps.title: Substitute the value of r
steps.bodyLatex: Using the formula \( A = \pi r^2 \), substitute \( r = 3.55 \) into the equation: \[ A = \pi \times (3.55)^2 \]

steps.title: Calculate the area
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate the square of \( 3.55 \), which is \( 12.6025 \), then multiply by \( \pi \): \[ A = \pi \times 12.6025 \approx 39.5919... \]

steps.title: Round to three significant figures
steps.bodyLatex: To round \( 39.5919... \) to three significant figures, we look at the first three digits: \( 3 \), \( 9 \), and \( 5 \). The fourth digit is \( 9 \), which is \( 5 \) or greater, so we round the third digit up: \[ A \approx 39.6 \]

commonMistakesLatex: Rounding too early in the calculation, which can lead to an inaccurate final result.
Confusing significant figures with decimal places (e.g., rounding to 39.592).
Forgetting to square the radius before multiplying by \( \pi \).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: A quick sanity check: \( 3.5^2 \) is \( 12.25 \), and \( 12.25 \times 3.14 \) is approximately \( 38.5 \). Since \( 3.55 \) is slightly larger than \( 3.5 \), an answer of \( 39.6 \) is reasonable.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of similar figures, specifically the concept that corresponding sides are in the same ratio.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the corresponding sides of the two figures and set up a ratio equation. Since the figures are similar, the ratio of corresponding sides must be equal.

steps.title: Identify corresponding sides
steps.bodyLatex: Looking at the shapes, the side of length \(2 \text{ cm}\) on the left figure corresponds to the side of length \(3 \text{ cm}\) on the right figure. Similarly, the side of length \(12 \text{ cm}\) on the left figure corresponds to the side of length \(x \text{ cm}\) on the right figure.

steps.title: Set up the ratio
steps.bodyLatex: We can set up the ratio of the sides as: \[ \frac{\text{Side on right}}{\text{Side on left}} = \frac{3}{2} \] Now, apply this same ratio to the other pair of corresponding sides: \[ \frac{x}{12} = \frac{3}{2} \]

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.bodyLatex: Multiply both sides by \(12\) to isolate \(x\): \[ x = \frac{3}{2} \times 12 \] \[ x = 3 \times 6 = 18 \]

commonMistakesLatex: Setting up the ratio upside down (e.g., using 2/3 instead of 3/2).
Confusing which sides correspond to each other by not observing the orientation of the right angles.
Attempting to use the side of length 6 or 9, which do not correspond to the sides involving x.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the ratio: \(3 \div 2 = 1.5\) and \(18 \div 12 = 1.5\). Since the ratios are identical, the answer is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to model a linear relationship where a quantity decreases over time at a constant rate.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the initial value (the starting volume) and the rate of change (the amount lost per minute). Since the volume is decreasing, the rate of change will be subtracted from the initial amount.

steps.title: Identify the initial value
steps.bodyLatex: The tank starts full with a volume of \(6000\) litres. This is our starting point when \(t = 0\).

steps.title: Calculate the volume lost
steps.bodyLatex: Water flows out at a rate of \(3\) litres per minute. After \(t\) minutes, the total volume of water lost is \(3 \times t\), or \(3t\).

steps.title: Formulate the expression
steps.bodyLatex: To find the remaining volume \(V\), subtract the amount lost from the initial volume: \(V = 6000 - 3t\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the rate of change with the initial value, leading to expressions like \(3t - 6000\).
Multiplying the initial volume by time instead of the rate, such as \(6000t - 3\).
Adding the rate instead of subtracting it, failing to recognize that the tank is emptying.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Test the formula at \(t = 0\): \(V = 6000 - 3(0) = 6000\), which is correct. Test at \(t = 1\): \(V = 6000 - 3(1) = 5997\), which correctly shows the volume decreasing.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate total earnings by combining hourly wages and a daily allowance over a specific period.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, calculate the total hourly wages for the 5 days. Then, calculate the total meal allowance for the 5 days and add these two amounts together.

steps.title: Calculate total hours worked
steps.bodyLatex: Tian works \(9\) hours per day for \(5\) days. The total hours worked is \(9 \times 5 = 45\) hours.

steps.title: Calculate total hourly wages
steps.bodyLatex: With an hourly rate of \(20.45\), the total wage is \(45 \times 20.45 = 920.25\).

steps.title: Calculate total meal allowance
steps.bodyLatex: Tian receives a meal allowance of \(16.20\) per day for \(5\) days. The total allowance is \(5 \times 16.20 = 81.00\).

steps.title: Calculate total earnings
steps.bodyLatex: Add the total wages and the total allowance: \(920.25 + 81.00 = 1001.25\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to multiply the daily meal allowance by the number of days (5).
Calculating the meal allowance for only one day instead of five.
Miscalculating the total number of hours worked.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: A quick sanity check: \(20 \times 45 = 900\) and \(16 \times 5 = 80\). The total should be slightly more than \(980\), which confirms \(1001.25\) is the correct magnitude.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to convert between compass bearings and true bearings.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: True bearings are measured clockwise from North (\(000^{\circ}\)). The compass bearing \(S48^{\circ}W\) means starting from South (\(180^{\circ}\)) and moving \(48^{\circ}\) towards the West.

steps.title: Identify the starting position
steps.bodyLatex: A compass bearing of \(S\) (South) corresponds to a true bearing of \(180^{\circ}\).

steps.title: Apply the direction of the angle
steps.bodyLatex: The notation \(S48^{\circ}W\) indicates we must move \(48^{\circ}\) clockwise from South towards the West. We add this angle to the true bearing of South: \(180^{\circ} + 48^{\circ} = 228^{\circ}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Subtracting \(48^{\circ}\) from \(180^{\circ}\) instead of adding it.
Confusing the South position (\(180^{\circ}\)) with the West position (\(270^{\circ}\)), leading to incorrect calculations like \(270^{\circ} - 48^{\circ}\).
Misinterpreting the direction of the bearing, which must always be measured clockwise from North.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since \(S\) is \(180^{\circ}\) and \(W\) is \(270^{\circ}\), the bearing must be between \(180^{\circ}\) and \(270^{\circ}\). Only \(222^{\circ}\) and \(228^{\circ}\) fall in this range; \(228^{\circ}\) is exactly \(48^{\circ}\) past \(180^{\circ}\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the present value formula for compound interest, specifically when interest is compounded more frequently than once per year.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the present value formula \( PV = \frac{FV}{(1 + r)^n} \), where \( r \) is the interest rate per compounding period and \( n \) is the total number of compounding periods.

steps.title: Identify the variables
steps.bodyLatex: The future value is \( FV = 150,000 \). The annual interest rate is \( 4\% \), which is \( 0.04 \). Since it is compounded half-yearly, we divide the annual rate by \( 2 \), giving a periodic rate of \( r = \frac{0.04}{2} = 0.02 \).

steps.title: Calculate the total number of periods
steps.bodyLatex: The investment is for \( 10 \) years. Since interest is compounded twice per year, the total number of compounding periods is \( n = 10 \times 2 = 20 \).

steps.title: Formulate the equation
steps.bodyLatex: Substituting these values into the present value formula, we get \( PV = \frac{150,000}{(1 + 0.02)^{20}} \).

commonMistakesLatex: Failing to divide the annual interest rate by the number of compounding periods per year (e.g., using 0.04 instead of 0.02).
Failing to multiply the number of years by the compounding frequency (e.g., using 10 instead of 20).
Confusing the future value formula with the present value formula.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The formula \( PV = FV(1+r)^{-n} \) is equivalent to \( PV = \frac{FV}{(1+r)^n} \). With \( r=0.02 \) and \( n=20 \), option D correctly matches the structure of the compound interest calculation.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret the relationship between the shape of a container and the rate at which the water depth increases over time.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Think about how the cross-sectional area of the container changes as it fills. A constant flow rate means that in narrower sections, the depth increases quickly, while in wider sections, the depth increases more slowly.

steps.title: Analyze the bottom section
steps.bodyLatex: The bottom of the container is a cylinder. Since the cross-sectional area is constant, the depth of the water will increase at a steady, constant rate, resulting in a straight line on the graph.

steps.title: Analyze the top section
steps.bodyLatex: The top of the container is a cone or funnel shape that widens as you move upwards. As the container gets wider, the same volume of water will result in a smaller increase in depth, meaning the rate of change of depth decreases over time.

steps.title: Combine the observations
steps.bodyLatex: The graph must start with a linear (straight) section and transition into a curve that flattens out as the depth increases. Graph A shows this exact behavior, starting with a steady slope and becoming less steep as the container widens.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the volume of water with the depth of water.
Assuming the entire container has a uniform shape, which would lead to a single straight line.
Misinterpreting the slope of the graph as the rate of flow rather than the rate of change of depth.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Imagine filling a narrow straw versus a wide bucket; the straw fills to a certain depth much faster than the bucket. Since our container starts narrow and becomes wide, the depth must rise quickly at first and then slow down, which matches graph A.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret scale drawings, determine a scale ratio, and calculate the area of a composite shape.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), count the door symbols. For (b), compare a measured length on the plan to its given real-world length. For (c), split the floor plan into simple rectangles, convert the plan measurements to actual metres using the scale, and sum the areas.

steps.title: (a) Counting the doors
steps.bodyLatex: Carefully examine the floor plan for the standard door arc symbols. Counting these symbols reveals there are \(5\) doors in total.

steps.title: (b) Verifying the scale
steps.bodyLatex: Measure a known length on the plan, such as the \(55 \text{ mm}\) side, which corresponds to an actual length of \(5500 \text{ mm}\). The scale is the ratio of plan length to actual length: \(55 : 5500 = 1 : 100\).

steps.title: (c) Calculating the total floor area
steps.bodyLatex: Divide the floor plan into two rectangles. Rectangle 1 has plan dimensions of \(5.5 \text{ cm} \times 5 \text{ cm}\), which converts to \(5.5 \text{ m} \times 5 \text{ m} = 27.5 \text{ m}^2\). Rectangle 2 has plan dimensions of \(4 \text{ cm} \times 7 \text{ cm}\), which converts to \(4 \text{ m} \times 7 \text{ m} = 28 \text{ m}^2\). Adding these together gives a total area of \(27.5 \text{ m}^2 + 28 \text{ m}^2 = 55.5 \text{ m}^2\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing perimeter with area by adding the lengths instead of multiplying them.
Forgetting to convert centimetres or millimetres from the plan into metres before calculating the area.
Failing to decompose the composite shape into smaller, manageable rectangles.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ensure the final area is in square metres (\(m^2\)) and that the scale conversion (\(1 \text{ cm} = 1 \text{ m}\)) was applied consistently throughout the calculation.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate a total cost by combining fixed daily charges and variable distance-based charges.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To find the total cost, calculate the fixed hire cost and the variable distance cost separately, then add them together. The formula used is: \( \text{Total Cost} = (\text{Daily Rate} \times \text{Number of Days}) + (\text{Cost per km} \times \text{Total km}) \).

steps.title: Calculate the total hire cost
steps.bodyLatex: Multiply the daily rate of \( \$210 \) by the \( 9 \) days the campervan was hired: \[ 210 \times 9 = 1890 \] The total hire cost is \( \$1890 \).

steps.title: Calculate the total distance cost
steps.bodyLatex: Multiply the cost per kilometre of \( \$0.35 \) by the total distance travelled of \( 2700 \) km: \[ 0.35 \times 2700 = 945 \] The total distance cost is \( \$945 \).

steps.title: Calculate the total cost
steps.bodyLatex: Add the hire cost and the distance cost together to find the final amount paid: \[ 1890 + 945 = 2835 \]

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to include both the daily hire component and the distance component in the final sum.
Errors in decimal multiplication when calculating the distance cost.
Failing to show the working for both parts, which is necessary to gain full marks.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the logic by ensuring the units are consistent: \( (\text{dollars/day} \times \text{days}) + (\text{dollars/km} \times \text{km}) = \text{dollars} + \text{dollars} = \text{dollars} \). The calculation \( 1890 + 945 = 2835 \) is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to evaluate survey design methods and identify common techniques used to create misleading statistical graphs.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), consider the practical benefits of categorical versus numerical data collection. For part (b), examine the graph's axes and visual proportions for inconsistencies.

steps.title: (a) Advantages of survey options
steps.bodyLatex: Option A is a categorical question with a binary response, which makes it very simple and quick to collate data. Option B uses a scale, which allows for a wider range of responses that can provide more detailed information and be easily displayed in a graph.

steps.title: (b) Identifying misleading features
steps.bodyLatex: First, look at the vertical axis. The scale is inconsistent because the gap between \( 0 \) and \( 30 \) is the same as the gap between \( 30 \), \( 40 \), and \( 50 \). Second, observe the column widths; the first column ('Too expensive') is significantly wider than the other two, which visually exaggerates its importance.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) Option A: easy to collate data; Option B: allows for a range of responses. (b) Inconsistent vertical scale and varying column widths.
commonMistakesLatex: Failing to use specific terminology like 'scale' or 'axis' when describing the graph.
Only identifying one misleading feature instead of two.
Confusing the purpose of categorical versus numerical survey questions.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify that your answer for (b) directly references the visual components of the graph: the numbers on the side and the physical size of the bars.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret a travel graph, specifically understanding how distance from a destination changes over time.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To solve this, carefully read the axes: the vertical axis shows distance from town \(N\), and the horizontal axis shows time. Remember that a steep slope indicates a faster speed, while a horizontal line indicates the traveller is stationary.

steps.title: (a) Distance between towns
steps.bodyLatex: At \(8 \text{ am}\), the graph starts at a distance of \(140 \text{ km}\) from town \(N\). Since Jamie began the trip at town \(M\), the distance between town \(M\) and town \(N\) is \(140 \text{ km}\).

steps.title: (b) Time of return to town M
steps.bodyLatex: Jamie is at town \(M\) when the distance from town \(N\) is \(140 \text{ km}\). Looking at the graph, the line returns to the \(140 \text{ km}\) mark at \(10 \text{ am}\).

steps.title: (c) Total distance travelled
steps.bodyLatex: We sum the absolute changes in distance: from \(8 \text{ am}\) to \(9 \text{ am}\) is \(|100 - 140| = 40 \text{ km}\). From \(9:30 \text{ am}\) to \(10 \text{ am}\) is \(|140 - 100| = 40 \text{ km}\). From \(11 \text{ am}\) to \(1 \text{ pm}\) is \(|0 - 140| = 140 \text{ km}\). The total distance is \(40 + 40 + 140 = 220 \text{ km}\).

steps.title: (d) Fastest speed
steps.bodyLatex: The speed is represented by the steepness (gradient) of the line. Comparing the segments, the line segment between \(11 \text{ am}\) and \(11:30 \text{ am}\) is the steepest, meaning Jamie was travelling at the fastest speed during this interval.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) 140 km, (b) 10 am, (c) 220 km, (d) 11–11:30 am because the slope is the steepest.
commonMistakesLatex: Assuming the vertical axis represents distance travelled rather than distance from a specific town.
Forgetting to include all parts of the journey when calculating total distance.
Confusing a steep upward slope with a steep downward slope; both indicate high speed.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the total distance by summing the segments: \(40 \text{ km} + 0 \text{ km} + 40 \text{ km} + 0 \text{ km} + 140 \text{ km} = 220 \text{ km}\). The units are consistent in km and hours.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to substitute values into a linear formula and calculate percentages of a given total.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), subtract Tom's age from the constant provided. For part (b), multiply the maximum heart rate by the decimal equivalents of the percentages, \(0.50\) and \(0.85\).

steps.title: (a) Calculate maximum heart rate
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute Tom's age of \(25\) into the given formula: \[ \text{Maximum heart rate} = 220 - 25 = 195 \]

steps.title: (b) Calculate the lower heart rate limit
steps.bodyLatex: To find the \(50\%\) limit, multiply the maximum heart rate by \(0.50\): \[ 195 \times 0.50 = 97.5 \text{ bpm} \]

steps.title: (b) Calculate the upper heart rate limit
steps.bodyLatex: To find the \(85\%\) limit, multiply the maximum heart rate by \(0.85\): \[ 195 \times 0.85 = 165.75 \text{ bpm} \]

commonMistakesLatex: Performing the subtraction incorrectly in part (a).
Failing to show the working for the percentage calculations, which makes it harder to earn partial marks if a calculation error occurs.
Confusing the percentage values or using incorrect decimal conversions (e.g., using \(0.85\) as \(8.5\%\)).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify that \(97.5\) is exactly half of \(195\), and ensure the range \(97.5\) to \(165.75\) falls within the maximum limit of \(195\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret a scatter plot and identify the nature of a relationship between two variables. It also requires you to read specific values from the graph by interpolating data points.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), observe the shape of the data points; if they do not form a straight line, the relationship is non-linear. For part (b), locate the value on the vertical axis, move horizontally to the trend, and read the corresponding value on the horizontal axis.

steps.title: (a) Determine if the relationship is linear or non-linear
steps.bodyLatex: A linear relationship would mean the points form a straight line. By looking at the graph, we can see the points follow a curve that flattens out as the distance increases. Therefore, the relationship is non-linear.

steps.title: (b) Find the distance for 90 decibels
steps.bodyLatex: Locate the value of \(90 \text{ dB}\) on the vertical axis. Move horizontally across until you reach the data point at this height. Looking down to the horizontal axis, the point aligns between \(5 \text{ m}\) and \(10 \text{ m}\), specifically at approximately \(6 \text{ m}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming the relationship is linear just because it is a trend.
Misreading the scale on the horizontal axis (each small grid square represents \(1 \text{ m}\)).
Failing to draw a line of best fit or curve to help guide the eye when reading values.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the point at \(6 \text{ m}\) on the graph: it sits just above the \(90 \text{ dB}\) line, confirming the estimate is accurate within the accepted range of \(6-7 \text{ m}\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to identify specific outcomes within a sample space and apply the rule of complementary probability.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, identify the winning numbers by listing odd integers between \(20\) and \(30\). Then, calculate the probability of winning and subtract it from \(1\) to find the probability of not winning.

steps.title: (a) List the winning numbers
steps.bodyLatex: The odd numbers between \(20\) and \(30\) are the numbers that are not divisible by \(2\). These are \(21, 23, 25, 27,\) and \(29\).

steps.title: (b) Calculate the probability of winning
steps.bodyLatex: There are \(30\) total cards and \(5\) winning cards. The probability of winning is \(P(\text{win}) = \frac{5}{30}\), which simplifies to \(P(\text{win}) = \frac{1}{6}\).

steps.title: (b) Calculate the probability of not winning
steps.bodyLatex: Using the rule for complementary events, the probability of not winning is \(P(\text{not win}) = 1 - P(\text{win})\). Substituting our value, we get \(1 - \frac{1}{6} = \frac{5}{6}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Including the numbers 20 or 30 in the list of odd numbers.
Forgetting to simplify the fraction \(\frac{5}{30}\) to \(\frac{1}{6}\).
Failing to use the complement rule and instead trying to count all 25 non-winning cards manually.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since there are \(5\) winning cards, there must be \(30 - 5 = 25\) non-winning cards. The probability is \(\frac{25}{30}\), which simplifies to \(\frac{5}{6}\). This matches our result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate time differences between international time zones and convert local times accordingly.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Calculate the total time difference by finding the gap between the two UTC offsets. Since Singapore is ahead of UTC and New York is behind, add this difference to the New York time to find the Singapore time.

steps.title: Calculate the total time difference
steps.bodyLatex: Singapore is \(+8\) and New York is \(-5\). The total time difference is \(8 - (-5) = 13\) hours. This means Singapore is \(13\) hours ahead of New York.

steps.title: Apply the time difference
steps.bodyLatex: Start at \(9\text{ pm}\) on Monday. Adding \(3\) hours brings us to \(12\text{ am}\) (midnight) on Tuesday. Adding the remaining \(10\) hours (\(13 - 3 = 10\)) results in \(10\text{ am}\) on Tuesday.

commonMistakesLatex: Failing to account for the day change when crossing midnight.
Incorrectly calculating the time difference by ignoring the negative sign for the New York offset.
Forgetting to include both the time and the day in the final answer.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: If Singapore is \(10\text{ am}\) Tuesday, subtracting \(13\) hours takes us back \(10\) hours to \(12\text{ am}\) Tuesday, then back \(3\) more hours to \(9\text{ pm}\) Monday, which matches the starting time.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to substitute known values into a given formula and rearrange the equation to solve for an unknown variable.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We start by substituting the given values for the dosage and the adult dosage into Fried's formula. Then, we use algebraic manipulation to isolate the age variable.

steps.title: Step 1: Identify the given values and the formula
steps.bodyLatex: The formula is \[ \text{Dosage} = \frac{\text{age} \times \text{adult dosage}}{150} \]. We are given that the \( \text{Dosage} = 24 \text{ mg} \) and the \( \text{adult dosage} = 200 \text{ mg} \).

steps.title: Step 2: Substitute the values into the formula
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the known values into the equation: \[ 24 = \frac{\text{age} \times 200}{150} \]

steps.title: Step 3: Solve for age
steps.bodyLatex: To isolate the age, first multiply both sides by \( 150 \): \[ 24 \times 150 = \text{age} \times 200 \] \[ 3600 = \text{age} \times 200 \] Now, divide both sides by \( 200 \): \[ \text{age} = \frac{3600}{200} = 18 \]

commonMistakesLatex: Swapping the values for dosage and adult dosage in the formula.
Errors in algebraic rearrangement when trying to isolate the age variable.
Forgetting to include the units (months) in the final answer.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Substitute \( 18 \) back into the formula: \[ \frac{18 \times 200}{150} = \frac{3600}{150} = 24 \text{ mg} \]. The result matches the given dosage.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to represent data from a distance table as a weighted network diagram and identify the minimum spanning tree (MST).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), draw edges between all towns listed in the table and label them with their corresponding distances. For part (b), use Prim's or Kruskal's algorithm to select the shortest edges that connect all vertices without forming a cycle.

steps.title: (a) Draw the weighted network
steps.bodyLatex: Using the table, connect the points with lines and label them with the given distances: \(S-Y=280\), \(S-B=275\), \(Y-C=60\), \(C-B=150\), \(Y-M=530\), and \(B-M=790\).

steps.title: (b) Identify the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)
steps.bodyLatex: To find the MST, we select the smallest edges that connect all 5 towns without creating a loop. We start with the smallest: \(Y-C=60\), then \(C-B=150\), then \(S-B=275\), and finally \(Y-M=530\). Note that we exclude the \(B-M=790\) edge because it would create a cycle or is longer than the path already established via \(Y\).

steps.title: (b) Calculate the total length
steps.bodyLatex: Sum the weights of the edges included in the MST: \(60 + 150 + 275 + 530 = 1015\). The total length is \(1015 \text{ km}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to include all towns in the spanning tree.
Including an edge that creates a cycle, which means it is no longer a tree.
Failing to label the edges with their weights.
Calculating the total length incorrectly by adding an extra edge.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: A spanning tree for \(n=5\) vertices must have exactly \(n-1=4\) edges. Our selection has 4 edges: \(60, 150, 275, 530\), which correctly connects all vertices.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate tiered commission rates by splitting a total amount into different percentage brackets.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To solve this, split the sale price into two parts: the first \(800\,000\) and the remaining amount. Calculate the commission for each part separately using the given percentages and then find the sum.

steps.title: Step 1: Calculate the commission on the first portion
steps.bodyLatex: The first \(800\,000\) is charged at a rate of \(2\%\). We calculate this as: \[ 0.02 \times 800\,000 = 16\,000 \]

steps.title: Step 2: Determine the remaining amount and calculate its commission
Step 3: Calculate the total commission
steps.bodyLatex: Add the two commission amounts together to get the final total: \[ 16\,000 + 10\,500 = 26\,500 \]

commonMistakesLatex: Applying the \(1.5\%\) rate to the entire \(1\,500\,000\) instead of splitting the amount.
Forgetting to subtract the \(800\,000\) from the total before applying the second percentage rate.
Errors in converting percentages to decimals (e.g., writing \(1.5\%\) as \(0.15\) instead of \(0.015\)).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the calculation by checking the parts: \(16\,000 + 10\,500 = 26\,500\). Since the commission is a small fraction of the total sale price, \(26\,500\) is a reasonable value for a \(1.5\%\) to \(2\%\) commission rate.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate the total cost of electricity consumption by converting units and applying the formula for energy usage.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To solve this, first convert the power from watts to kilowatts (kW) and the cost from cents to dollars. Then, calculate the total energy used in kilowatt-hours (kWh) over the seven-day period and multiply by the rate.

steps.title: Convert units to standard form
steps.bodyLatex: First, convert the power from watts to kilowatts: \( 2500 \text{ W} \div 1000 = 2.5 \text{ kW} \). Next, convert the electricity rate from cents to dollars: \( 27 \text{ cents} = \$0.27 \).

steps.title: Calculate total time and energy usage
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate the total hours the air-conditioner is used over seven days: \( 3 \text{ hours/day} \times 7 \text{ days} = 21 \text{ hours} \). The total energy consumed is \( 2.5 \text{ kW} \times 21 \text{ hours} = 52.5 \text{ kWh} \).

steps.title: Calculate the total cost
steps.bodyLatex: Multiply the total energy by the cost per kilowatt-hour: \( 52.5 \text{ kWh} \times \$0.27/\text{kWh} = \$14.175 \). Rounding to the nearest cent, we get \( \$14.18 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to convert watts to kilowatts, leading to an answer 1000 times too large.
Failing to convert cents to dollars, resulting in an answer of 1417.5.
Miscalculating the total time by using only the daily hours instead of the seven-day total.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the units: \( (\text{kW}) \times (\text{hours}) \times (\text{dollars/kWh}) = \text{dollars} \). The calculation \( 2.5 \times 21 \times 0.27 = 14.175 \) confirms the result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to plot data points on a scatterplot and use a line of best fit to make predictions.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), map the table values to the coordinates \((x, y)\) where \(x\) is phone use and \(y\) is sleep. For part (b), use a ruler to draw a straight line that balances the points above and below it, then read the corresponding \(y\)-value for \(x = 2\).

steps.title: (a) Plotting the data
steps.bodyLatex: Identify the coordinates for Alinta as \((4, 8)\) and Birrani as \((0, 10.5)\). Place a clear dot at the intersection of \(4\) on the horizontal axis and \(8\) on the vertical axis, and another at the intersection of \(0\) on the horizontal axis and \(10.5\) on the vertical axis.

steps.title: (b) Drawing the line of best fit
steps.bodyLatex: Using a ruler, draw a single straight line that passes through the general trend of the data. Ensure the line is positioned so that there is a roughly equal number of points above and below the line.

steps.title: (b) Estimating the value
steps.bodyLatex: Locate \(x = 2\) on the horizontal axis. Move vertically up from \(2\) until you reach your line of best fit, then move horizontally to the left to read the corresponding value on the vertical axis, which is approximately \(9\) hours.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: The estimated average sleep for \(2\) hours of phone use is \(9\) hours.
commonMistakesLatex: Swapping the \(x\) and \(y\) coordinates when plotting points.
Drawing a 'dot-to-dot' line instead of a straight line of best fit.
Providing a range of values instead of a single specific estimate from the line.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check that your line of best fit follows the negative correlation shown by the data (as phone use increases, sleep decreases). A value of \(9\) hours is consistent with the trend between the data points at \(1.5\) hours and \(2.5\) hours.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate and compare simple interest and compound interest over a fixed period of time.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We will calculate the interest for Fund A using the simple interest formula \( I = P \times r \times n \) and the interest for Fund B by finding the future value \( A = P(1 + r)^n \) and subtracting the principal.

steps.title: Calculate interest for Fund A
steps.bodyLatex: For simple interest, we use the formula \( I = P \times r \times n \), where \( P = 7000 \), \( r = 0.052 \), and \( n = 3 \). This gives \( I = 7000 \times 0.052 \times 3 = 1092 \). So, the interest earned in Fund A is \( 1092 \).

steps.title: Calculate future value for Fund B
steps.bodyLatex: For compound interest, we first find the total amount using \( A = P(1 + r)^n \), where \( P = 7000 \), \( r = 0.05 \), and \( n = 3 \). Calculating this, we get \( A = 7000(1 + 0.05)^3 = 7000(1.157625) = 8103.375 \).

steps.title: Calculate interest for Fund B
steps.bodyLatex: To find the interest earned in Fund B, we subtract the principal from the future value: \( 8103.375 - 7000 = 1103.375 \). Rounding to the nearest cent, the interest is \( 1103.38 \).

steps.title: Find the difference
steps.bodyLatex: Finally, subtract the interest of Fund A from the interest of Fund B: \( 1103.38 - 1092 = 11.38 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to convert percentages to decimals (e.g., using 5 instead of 0.05).
Calculating only the future value for Fund B instead of the interest earned.
Rounding too early in the calculation, which can lead to an incorrect final answer.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since Fund B compounds, it should yield more interest than Fund A. Our result \( 1103.38 > 1092 \) is consistent with this expectation.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret linear graphs and apply cost, revenue, and profit formulas in a business context.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To find the break-even point, identify where the revenue and cost lines intersect. To calculate profit, use the formula \( \text{Profit} = R - C \) by substituting the given number of items into both equations.

steps.title: (a) Finding the break-even point
steps.bodyLatex: The break-even point occurs where the revenue equals the cost, which is the point where the two lines on the graph cross. Looking at the graph, the intersection occurs at \( x = 24 \) on the horizontal axis.

steps.title: (b) Calculating profit for 60 cupcakes
steps.bodyLatex: First, calculate the revenue for \( x = 60 \) using \( R = 4(60) = 240 \). Next, calculate the cost using \( C = 1.5(60) + 60 = 90 + 60 = 150 \).

steps.title: (b) Final profit calculation
steps.bodyLatex: Now, subtract the total cost from the total revenue to find the profit: \( \text{Profit} = 240 - 150 = 90 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Misreading the scale on the horizontal axis when identifying the intersection point.
Forgetting to include the fixed cost of 60 when calculating the total cost.
Confusing the revenue and cost equations or failing to subtract them correctly to find profit.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a), at \( x = 24 \), \( R = 4 \times 24 = 96 \) and \( C = 1.5 \times 24 + 60 = 36 + 60 = 96 \). Since \( R = C \), the break-even point is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate compound interest on a credit card balance where interest is applied daily over a specific period.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the compound interest formula \( A = P(1 + r)^n \). We must identify the principal amount, the daily interest rate, and the exact number of days the interest accrues before subtracting the payment.

steps.title: Step 1: Identify the variables
steps.bodyLatex: The principal \( P \) is \( 7500 \). The annual interest rate is \( 21\% \), so the daily rate \( r \) is \( \frac{0.21}{365} \). The number of days \( n \) from the 15th to the 30th (inclusive) is \( 30 - 15 + 1 = 16 \) days.

steps.title: Step 2: Calculate the interest-accrued balance
steps.bodyLatex: Using the compound interest formula, the balance before the payment is: \[ A = 7500 \left(1 + \frac{0.21}{365}\right)^{16} \] Calculating this gives: \[ A \approx 7500 \times 1.009245... \approx 7569.34 \]

steps.title: Step 3: Calculate the closing balance
steps.bodyLatex: Finally, subtract the payment made on 30 November from the accrued amount: \[ \text{Closing Balance} = 7569.34 - 2000 = 5569.34 \]

commonMistakesLatex: Miscalculating the number of days by not including the day of purchase (15th).
Subtracting the payment before calculating the interest.
Using the annual interest rate directly instead of dividing by 365 for the daily rate.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: A quick sanity check: the interest for 16 days on 7500 at 21% is roughly \( 7500 \times 0.21 \times \frac{16}{365} \approx 69.04 \). Adding this to 7500 gives 7569.04, which is very close to our precise compound interest result of 7569.34.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate the area of a right-angled triangle and perform unit conversions to determine a total cost. It requires applying Pythagoras' theorem and working with land measurement units.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, use Pythagoras' theorem to find the missing side length \( AC \). Then, calculate the area of the triangle using \( \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} \), convert the result to hectares, and multiply by the cost per hectare.

steps.title: Step 1: Find the length of side AC
steps.bodyLatex: Since the triangle is right-angled at \( C \), we use Pythagoras' theorem: \( AC^2 + BC^2 = AB^2 \). Substituting the known values: \( AC^2 + 3000^2 = 7800^2 \). Solving for \( AC \): \( AC = \sqrt{7800^2 - 3000^2} = \sqrt{60840000 - 9000000} = \sqrt{51840000} = 7200 \text{ m} \).

steps.title: Step 2: Calculate the area in square metres
steps.bodyLatex: The area of a right-angled triangle is given by \( \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} \). Using \( AC = 7200 \text{ m} \) and \( BC = 3000 \text{ m} \), we get: \( \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times 7200 \times 3000 = 10,800,000 \text{ m}^2 \).

steps.title: Step 3: Convert the area to hectares
steps.bodyLatex: Given that \( 1 \text{ hectare} = 10,000 \text{ m}^2 \), divide the total area by \( 10,000 \): \( \frac{10,800,000}{10,000} = 1080 \text{ hectares} \).

steps.title: Step 4: Calculate the total cost
steps.bodyLatex: Finally, multiply the area in hectares by the price per hectare: \( \text{Cost} = 1080 \times 500 = 540,000 \). The total cost is \( \$540,000 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Mistaking the hypotenuse for one of the legs when using the area formula.
Incorrectly converting square metres to hectares (e.g., dividing by 100 instead of 10,000).
Forgetting to calculate the missing side length \( AC \) before finding the area.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the calculation by checking the Pythagoras step: \( 7200^2 + 3000^2 = 51,840,000 + 9,000,000 = 60,840,000 \), and \( \sqrt{60,840,000} = 7800 \), which matches the hypotenuse.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate taxable income by accounting for gross salary, additional income, and deductions, and then applying the correct marginal tax rate from a provided schedule.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, determine the taxable income by adding all income sources and subtracting deductions. Then, locate the correct tax bracket and apply the base tax amount plus the percentage rate for the income exceeding the bracket threshold.

steps.title: Step 1: Calculate the taxable income
steps.bodyLatex: To find the taxable income, add the gross salary and investment income, then subtract the tax deductions: \[ \text{Taxable Income} = 67000 + 780 - 1000 = 66780 \]

steps.title: Step 2: Identify the correct tax bracket
steps.bodyLatex: Since the taxable income of \( 66780 \) falls between \( 45001 \) and \( 120000 \), we use the third tax bracket: \[ \text{Tax} = 5092 + 0.325 \times (\text{Taxable Income} - 45000) \]

steps.title: Step 3: Calculate the tax payable
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the taxable income into the formula: \[ \text{Tax} = 5092 + 0.325 \times (66780 - 45000) \] \[ \text{Tax} = 5092 + 0.325 \times (21780) \] \[ \text{Tax} = 5092 + 7078.50 = 12170.50 \]

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to add the investment income or incorrectly subtracting deductions instead of adding them.
Using the wrong tax bracket or failing to convert the percentage (32.5 cents) into the decimal 0.325.
Calculating the tax on the entire 66780 instead of only the amount over 45000.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the calculation by ensuring the base tax (5092) is added to the product of the marginal rate (0.325) and the excess income (21780), which confirms the result of 12170.50.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to identify statistical outliers in a data set using the interquartile range (IQR) method.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To determine if a value is an outlier, we calculate the interquartile range (IQR) and use the upper threshold formula: \(Upper \text{ } Threshold = Q_3 + 1.5 \times IQR\). If a data point exceeds this value, it is classified as an outlier.

steps.title: Step 1: Find the Quartiles
steps.bodyLatex: The data set is already ordered: \(24, 25, 27, 33, 34, 34, 35, 39, 47, 59\). The median is the average of the 5th and 6th values: \(\frac{34+34}{2} = 34\). The lower quartile \(Q_1\) is the median of the lower half (first 5 values), which is \(27\). The upper quartile \(Q_3\) is the median of the upper half (last 5 values), which is \(39\).

steps.title: Step 2: Calculate the Interquartile Range (IQR)
steps.bodyLatex: The IQR is the difference between the upper and lower quartiles: \(IQR = Q_3 - Q_1 = 39 - 27 = 12\).

steps.title: Step 3: Calculate the Upper Threshold and Conclude
steps.bodyLatex: Using the outlier formula, the upper threshold is \(Q_3 + 1.5 \times IQR = 39 + 1.5 \times 12 = 39 + 18 = 57\). Since the age \(59\) is greater than the threshold of \(57\), it is considered an outlier.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the range (maximum minus minimum) with the interquartile range.
Attempting to identify outliers by visual 'gaps' instead of using the required mathematical formula.
Incorrectly calculating the quartiles for an even-numbered data set.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the calculation: \(1.5 \times 12 = 18\) and \(39 + 18 = 57\). Since \(59 > 57\), the conclusion is mathematically consistent with the formula.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate and graph straight-line depreciation and interpret the intersection point of two different depreciation models.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For straight-line depreciation, subtract the constant depreciation amount of \( 2500 \) each year. Once the table is complete, plot these points on the provided graph and draw a straight line to find where it intersects the existing declining-balance curve.

steps.title: (a) Complete the depreciation table
steps.bodyLatex: Starting with the initial value of \( 15000 \), subtract \( 2500 \) for each subsequent year: Year 1: \( 15000 - 2500 = 12500 \). Year 2: \( 12500 - 2500 = 10000 \). Year 3: \( 10000 - 2500 = 7500 \).

steps.title: (a) Plot the points
steps.bodyLatex: Plot the coordinates \( (1, 12500) \), \( (2, 10000) \), and \( (3, 7500) \) on the graph. Use a ruler to draw a straight line passing through these points and the starting point \( (0, 15000) \).

steps.title: (b) Find the intersection
steps.bodyLatex: Extend your straight line further until it crosses the curved line representing the declining-balance depreciation. Looking at the graph, the lines intersect exactly at the coordinate \( (4, 5000) \).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing straight-line depreciation (a constant dollar amount) with declining-balance depreciation (a percentage of the current value).
Failing to use a ruler to draw the straight line, which leads to inaccurate readings of the intersection point.
Misreading the scale on the vertical axis, which is marked in increments of \( 500 \) per grid square.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: At \( t=4 \), the straight-line value is \( 15000 - (4 \times 2500) = 5000 \). Checking the graph, the curve also passes through \( V=5000 \) at \( t=4 \), confirming the result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to use the compound interest formula to calculate the principal amount when given the future value, interest rate, and time period.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the compound interest formula \(A = P(1 + r)^n\), where \(A\) is the future value, \(P\) is the principal (original value), \(r\) is the annual interest rate as a decimal, and \(n\) is the number of years. Since we need to find the original value \(P\), we rearrange the formula to \(P = \frac{A}{(1 + r)^n}\).

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.bodyLatex: From the question, we have the future value \(A = 6100\), the annual appreciation rate \(r = 5.8\% = 0.058\), and the time period \(n = 10\) years.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the known values into the compound interest formula: \[ 6100 = P(1 + 0.058)^{10} \]

steps.title: Solve for P
steps.bodyLatex: To isolate \(P\), divide both sides by \((1.058)^{10}\): \[ P = \frac{6100}{(1.058)^{10}} \]

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.bodyLatex: Using a calculator, compute the value: \[ P \approx \frac{6100}{1.75735...} \approx 3471.15 \] The value of the watch ten years ago was \(3471.15\).

commonMistakesLatex: Using the simple interest formula \(I = Prn\) instead of the compound interest formula.
Incorrectly converting the percentage \(5.8\%\) to a decimal, such as using \(0.58\) instead of \(0.058\).
Attempting to calculate the value by dividing by \(10\) or subtracting the interest, failing to account for compounding growth.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by calculating the growth: \(3471.15 \times (1.058)^{10} \approx 6100\). Since this returns the original future value, the answer is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to solve problems involving two connected right-angled triangles using trigonometric ratios. You need to find a common side length to bridge the two triangles and then solve for the unknown angle.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, use the known side and angle in triangle \(ABC\) to calculate the length of the common side \(AB\). Then, use that length and the hypotenuse of triangle \(ABD\) to find \(\theta\) using the sine ratio.

steps.title: Step 1: Calculate the length of AB
steps.bodyLatex: In the right-angled triangle \(ABC\), we know the adjacent side \(AC = 35 \text{ cm}\) and the angle \(\angle ACB = 41^\circ\). Using the tangent ratio, \(\tan(\theta) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}\), we have: \[ \tan 41^\circ = \frac{AB}{35} \] Multiplying both sides by \(35\), we find: \[ AB = 35 \times \tan 41^\circ \approx 30.436 \text{ cm} \]

steps.title: Step 2: Set up the ratio for triangle ABD
steps.bodyLatex: Now look at the larger right-angled triangle \(ABD\). We know the opposite side \(AB \approx 30.436 \text{ cm}\) and the hypotenuse \(BD = 93 \text{ cm}\). The sine ratio is \(\sin \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}\), so: \[ \sin \theta = \frac{35 \tan 41^\circ}{93} \]

steps.title: Step 3: Solve for angle theta
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate the value of the fraction: \[ \sin \theta \approx \frac{30.436}{93} \approx 0.32727 \] Now, use the inverse sine function (\(\sin^{-1}\)) on your calculator: \[ \theta = \sin^{-1}(0.32727) \approx 19.0959^\circ \]

steps.title: Step 4: Convert to degrees and minutes
steps.bodyLatex: To convert the decimal part to minutes, multiply by \(60\): \[ 0.0959 \times 60 \approx 5.75' \approx 6' \] Therefore, \(\theta \approx 19^\circ 6'\).

commonMistakesLatex: Using the wrong trigonometric ratio (e.g., using cosine instead of tangent for the first triangle).
Rounding the length of AB too early, which leads to a loss of accuracy in the final answer.
Forgetting to use the inverse sine function (\(\sin^{-1}\)) to find the angle.
Incorrectly converting the decimal degree value into degrees and minutes.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check by calculating \(93 \times \sin(19^\circ 6')\), which gives approximately \(30.436\). This matches our calculated length for \(AB\), confirming the result is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to identify and count the vertices in a network diagram.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: In network theory, vertices (also called nodes) are the points represented by dots in the diagram. Simply count each distinct dot shown in the network.

steps.title: Identify the vertices
steps.bodyLatex: Look closely at the diagram provided. The vertices are the solid black dots where the lines (edges) meet or terminate.

steps.title: Count the vertices
steps.bodyLatex: By counting each dot individually from left to right, we identify \( 6 \) distinct points in the network.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing vertices with edges (the lines connecting the dots).
Miscounting due to overlapping or closely placed dots.
Counting the regions (faces) inside the loops instead of the vertices.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by marking each dot with a small tick as you count them to ensure none are missed or double-counted; there are exactly \( 6 \) dots.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to classify data types, specifically distinguishing between categorical (qualitative) and numerical (quantitative) data, and identifying nominal versus ordinal scales.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Categorical data describes qualities rather than quantities. Nominal data is a type of categorical data where there is no inherent order or ranking between the categories.

steps.title: Define the terms
steps.bodyLatex: Categorical data represents characteristics (e.g., labels or names). Nominal data is categorical data without a natural order, whereas ordinal data has a clear logical order.

steps.title: Evaluate the options
steps.bodyLatex: Option (B) and (C) are numerical (quantitative) because they involve measurements or counts. Option (D) is categorical but ordinal, because 'small', 'medium', and 'large' follow a specific size order.

steps.title: Select the correct category
steps.bodyLatex: Option (A), 'Hair colour', is categorical because it describes a quality. It is nominal because there is no mathematical or logical order (e.g., 'blonde' is not 'greater than' or 'less than' 'brown').

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing ordinal data (like cup sizes) with nominal data.
Mistaking numerical data (like height or count) for categorical data.
Assuming that all non-numerical data must be nominal.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Ask yourself: 'Is there a natural order to these items?' If the answer is 'no', it is nominal. Since you cannot rank hair colours from least to greatest, (A) is the only correct choice.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to identify the degree of each vertex in a network and calculate their total sum.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: The degree of a vertex is the number of edges connected to it. We can find the sum of the degrees by counting the connections at each vertex individually or by using the Handshaking Lemma, which states that the sum of the degrees equals twice the number of edges.

steps.title: Identify the degree of each vertex
steps.bodyLatex: We count the number of edges connected to each vertex: \(A\) has \(4\) edges, \(B\) has \(3\) edges, \(C\) has \(4\) edges, \(D\) has \(2\) edges, and \(E\) has \(3\) edges.

steps.title: Calculate the sum of the degrees
steps.bodyLatex: Summing these values gives: \(4 + 3 + 4 + 2 + 3 = 16\).

commonMistakesLatex: Miscounting the number of edges connected to a vertex, especially when edges are curved.
Forgetting to count all vertices in the network.
Confusing the number of vertices with the sum of the degrees.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Using the Handshaking Lemma: there are \(8\) edges in the diagram. Since the sum of degrees must be \(2 \times \text{edges}\), we have \(2 \times 8 = 16\), which confirms our result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply the declining-balance depreciation formula to calculate the future value of an asset over a specific time period.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the declining-balance depreciation formula: \( S = P(1 - r)^n \), where \( P \) is the initial value, \( r \) is the depreciation rate as a decimal, and \( n \) is the number of years.

steps.title: Identify the variables
steps.bodyLatex: From the question, we have the initial value \( P = 2467 \), the annual depreciation rate \( r = 15\% = 0.15 \), and the time period \( n = 3 \) years.

steps.title: Apply the formula
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute these values into the formula: \( S = 2467(1 - 0.15)^3 \). This simplifies to \( S = 2467(0.85)^3 \).

steps.title: Calculate the result
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate the value: \( S = 2467 \times 0.614125 = 1515.046375 \). Rounding this to the nearest dollar gives \( 1515 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Using the straight-line depreciation formula instead of the declining-balance formula.
Forgetting to convert the percentage \( 15\% \) into the decimal \( 0.15 \).
Rounding the intermediate value \( 0.85^3 \) too early, which can lead to an inaccurate final result.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: A quick sanity check: since the value drops by 15% each year, after 3 years it should be roughly half of the original (a bit more than \( 0.85^3 \approx 0.6 \)). \( 2467 \times 0.6 \approx 1480 \), which confirms that \( 1515 \) is a reasonable answer.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret the features of a linear equation in the form \(y = mx + c\) and identify its corresponding graph.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the slope-intercept form \(y = mx + c\), where \(m\) is the gradient and \(c\) is the \(y\)-intercept. By identifying these two values from the equation, we can eliminate incorrect graphs.

steps.title: Identify the y-intercept
steps.bodyLatex: In the equation \(y = -3x + 4\), the constant term is \(c = 4\). This means the graph must cross the \(y\)-axis at the point \((0, 4)\).

steps.title: Identify the gradient
steps.bodyLatex: The coefficient of \(x\) is \(m = -3\). Since the gradient is negative, the line must slope downwards from left to right.

steps.title: Select the correct graph
steps.bodyLatex: Graph A is the only option that shows a line crossing the \(y\)-axis at \(4\) and sloping downwards, confirming it is the correct representation.
