steps.title: Convert the diagram perimeter to actual distance
steps.bodyLatex: Using the scale \( 1:3000 \), multiply the diagram perimeter by \( 3000 \) to find the actual distance in centimetres: \( 26 \times 3000 = 78,000 \text{ cm} \). Convert this to metres by dividing by \( 100 \): \( \frac{78,000}{100} = 780 \text{ m} \).

steps.title: Calculate speed in metres per minute
steps.bodyLatex: Kerry walked \( 780 \text{ m} \) in \( 12 \text{ minutes} \). The speed in metres per minute is \( \frac{780}{12} = 65 \text{ m/min} \).

steps.title: Convert speed to kilometres per hour
steps.bodyLatex: To convert to km/h, multiply by \( 60 \) to get metres per hour (\( 65 \times 60 = 3900 \text{ m/h} \)) and divide by \( 1000 \) to convert metres to kilometres: \( \frac{3900}{1000} = 3.9 \text{ km/h} \).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to convert centimetres to metres or metres to kilometres.
Incorrectly calculating the perimeter (e.g., only adding two sides instead of all four).
Failing to convert the time units correctly when calculating the final speed.
Misinterpreting the scale ratio.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the logic: \( 3.9 \text{ km/h} \times 1000 = 3900 \text{ m/h} \). Divide by \( 60 \text{ min} \) gives \( 65 \text{ m/min} \). Multiply by \( 12 \text{ min} \) gives \( 780 \text{ m} \), which is \( 78,000 \text{ cm} \). Dividing by \( 3000 \) returns \( 26 \text{ cm} \), confirming the result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate compound interest with varying compounding periods and to equate that result to a simple interest investment.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), use the compound interest formula \(FV = P(1 + r)^n\), adjusting the annual rate and number of periods for monthly compounding. For part (b), determine the interest earned in part (a), then use the simple interest formula \(I = P \times R \times T\) to solve for the unknown rate \(r\).

steps.title: (a) Calculate the future value for Option A
steps.bodyLatex: First, identify the variables: the principal \(P = 35000\), the monthly interest rate \(i = 6\% \div 12 = 0.5\% = 0.005\), and the number of months \(n = 12\). Apply the formula \(FV = P(1 + i)^n\): \[FV = 35000(1 + 0.005)^{12}\]

steps.title: (a) Final calculation
steps.bodyLatex: Calculating the value gives: \[FV = 35000(1.0616778...) = 37158.723...\] Rounding to the nearest cent, the future value is \(37158.72\).

steps.title: (b) Find the simple interest rate r
steps.bodyLatex: The interest earned is the difference between the future value and the principal: \(I = 37158.72 - 35000 = 2158.72\). Using the simple interest formula \(I = P \times \frac{r}{100} \times T\) where \(T = 1\) year: \[2158.72 = 35000 \times \frac{r}{100} \times 1\]

steps.title: (b) Solve for r
steps.bodyLatex: Rearrange the equation to solve for \(r\): \[r = \frac{2158.72 \times 100}{35000} = 6.1677...\] Rounding to two decimal places, we get \(r = 6.17\).

commonMistakesLatex: Using the annual interest rate of 6% directly in the compound interest formula instead of dividing by 12.
Forgetting to subtract the principal when calculating the interest for the simple interest formula.
Confusing the future value with the interest amount when substituting into the simple interest formula.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by calculating simple interest: \(35000 \times 0.0617 \times 1 = 2159.5\). This is very close to the interest from part (a), confirming the result is reasonable given rounding.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate energy consumption costs over time and compare the total cost of ownership between two appliances.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), calculate the annual energy consumption of Television B in kWh and multiply by the electricity rate. For part (b), divide the difference in purchase price by the annual savings calculated in part (a).

steps.title: (a) Calculate annual electricity cost for Television B
steps.bodyLatex: First, calculate the annual energy usage in kWh: \((160 \text{ W} \div 1000) \times 3 \text{ hours/day} \times 365 \text{ days/year} = 175.2 \text{ kWh}\). Then, multiply by the electricity rate: \(175.2 \text{ kWh} \times \$0.25/\text{kWh} = \$43.80\).

steps.title: (a) Calculate the difference in annual cost
steps.bodyLatex: Subtract the annual cost of Television B from the given annual cost of Television A: \(\$48.18 - \$43.80 = \$4.38\).

steps.title: (b) Calculate the price difference
steps.bodyLatex: Find the extra cost of buying Television B compared to Television A: \(\$921.90 - \$900.00 = \$21.90\).

steps.title: (b) Determine the break-even time
steps.bodyLatex: Divide the initial price difference by the annual savings to find the number of years: \(\$21.90 \div \$4.38/\text{year} = 5 \text{ years}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to convert Watts (W) to Kilowatts (kW) by dividing by 1000.
Using the wrong number of days in a year or failing to account for the 3 hours of daily usage.
Confusing the purchase price difference with the annual electricity savings.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check: If Television B saves \$4.38 per year, in 5 years it saves \(5 \times 4.38 = 21.90\). Since Television B costs exactly \$21.90 more to buy, the total costs are equal at 5 years.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to determine the equation of a linear regression line from a graph and understand how adding a new data point influences the slope of that line.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), identify two points on the line to calculate the gradient \(m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}\) and the y-intercept \(c\). For part (b), consider the position of the new point relative to the existing trend to determine its effect on the slope.

steps.title: (a) Identify points on the line
steps.bodyLatex: Looking at the graph, the line passes exactly through the points \((0, 2)\) and \((5, 18)\). These points are easy to read and will help us find the equation.

steps.title: (a) Calculate the gradient
steps.bodyLatex: The gradient \(m\) is calculated using the formula \[ m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{18 - 2}{5 - 0} = \frac{16}{5} = 3.2 \]

steps.title: (a) State the equation
steps.bodyLatex: Since the line crosses the y-axis at \(y = 2\), the y-intercept \(c = 2\). Using the form \(y = mx + c\), the equation is \[ y = 3.2x + 2 \]

steps.title: (b) Analyze the impact of the new point
steps.bodyLatex: The new point is \((10, 45)\). If we check the current line at \(x = 10\), the predicted value is \(y = 3.2(10) + 2 = 34\). Since the actual value \(45\) is significantly higher than the predicted value \(34\), this point pulls the right side of the line upwards, which increases the gradient.

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to include the 'equals' sign in the linear equation.
Confusing the gradient and the y-intercept when writing the final equation.
Misinterpreting how an outlier or a high-value data point affects the slope of a regression line.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a), substitute \(x=5\) into \(y=3.2x+2\) to get \(y=18\), which matches the graph. For (b), visualize the line pivoting upwards at the right end to accommodate the higher value, confirming an increase in steepness.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to solve linear equations involving algebraic fractions.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: The most efficient strategy is to eliminate the fraction by multiplying every term in the equation by the denominator, which is \( 2 \). This simplifies the equation into a standard linear form that is easy to solve.

steps.title: Multiply to remove the fraction
steps.bodyLatex: To clear the fraction, multiply every term on both sides of the equation by \( 2 \): \[ 2(x) + 2\left(\frac{x - 1}{2}\right) = 2(9) \]

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.bodyLatex: After multiplying, the \( 2 \) in the denominator cancels out, leaving: \[ 2x + (x - 1) = 18 \]

steps.title: Combine like terms
steps.bodyLatex: Group the \( x \) terms together: \[ 3x - 1 = 18 \]

steps.title: Isolate the variable
steps.bodyLatex: Add \( 1 \) to both sides, then divide by \( 3 \) to solve for \( x \): \[ 3x = 19 \implies x = \frac{19}{3} \]

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to multiply the \( x \) term or the \( 9 \) by \( 2 \) when clearing the fraction.
Making sign errors when distributing the negative sign in the numerator.
Using guess and check instead of algebraic methods, which can lead to incomplete marks.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Substitute \( x = \frac{19}{3} \) back into the original equation: \( \frac{19}{3} + \frac{\frac{19}{3} - 1}{2} = \frac{19}{3} + \frac{\frac{16}{3}}{2} = \frac{19}{3} + \frac{8}{3} = \frac{27}{3} = 9 \). The answer is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate the dividend yield of an investment, which is a key concept in financial mathematics.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: The dividend yield is the percentage return on an investment, calculated by dividing the annual dividend per share by the market price per share, then multiplying by \( 100 \% \).

steps.title: Calculate the dividend per share
steps.bodyLatex: Since Ariana received a total dividend of \( \$810 \) for \( 1500 \) shares, we find the dividend per share by dividing the total dividend by the number of shares: \[ \text{Dividend per share} = \frac{810}{1500} = 0.54 \]

steps.title: Calculate the dividend yield
steps.bodyLatex: Now, divide the dividend per share by the market price per share (\( \$27 \)) and multiply by \( 100 \% \) to express the yield as a percentage: \[ \text{Dividend yield} = \left( \frac{0.54}{27} \right) \times 100\% \]

steps.title: Final calculation
steps.bodyLatex: Performing the division and multiplication gives: \[ \text{Dividend yield} = 0.02 \times 100\% = 2\% \]

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to convert the final decimal value into a percentage.
Attempting to use the total dividend of \( 810 \) directly against the share price of \( 27 \) without accounting for the number of shares.
Confusing the dividend yield (a percentage) with the total dividend amount (a dollar value).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: You can verify this by calculating \( 2\% \) of the total investment value: \( 0.02 \times (1500 \times 27) = 0.02 \times 40500 = 810 \). This matches the total dividend given in the question.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to use a present value interest factor table to calculate the monthly repayment required to amortise a loan.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, convert the annual interest rate to a monthly rate and determine the total number of periods. Then, identify the correct factor from the table and divide the loan amount by this factor.

steps.title: Calculate the monthly interest rate and total periods
steps.bodyLatex: The annual interest rate is \( 1.5\% = 0.015 \). Since interest is compounded monthly, the monthly rate \( r \) is \( \frac{0.015}{12} = 0.00125 \). The loan term is \( 30 \) years, so the total number of months \( N \) is \( 30 \times 12 = 360 \).

steps.title: Identify the correct interest factor
steps.bodyLatex: Using the provided table, locate the value at the intersection of row \( N = 360 \) and column \( r = 0.00125 \). The present value interest factor is \( 289.75411 \).

steps.title: Calculate the monthly repayment
steps.bodyLatex: The formula for the monthly repayment \( M \) is \( M = \frac{\text{Loan Amount}}{\text{Factor}} \). Substituting the values, we get \( M = \frac{500,000}{289.75411} \approx 1725.6025 \dots \).

steps.title: Final rounding
steps.bodyLatex: Rounding to the nearest cent, the monthly repayment is \( \$1725.60 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Failing to convert the annual interest rate to a monthly rate by dividing by 12.
Using the wrong row or column in the table, such as selecting the 30-year rate instead of the 360-month period.
Attempting to use complex compound interest formulas instead of utilizing the provided table values.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: To verify, multiply the monthly payment by the factor: \( 1725.60 \times 289.75411 \approx 499,999.99 \), which confirms the loan principal of \( 500,000 \) is repaid.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to use trigonometry in a right-angled triangle and calculate the area of composite shapes involving a semicircle.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), use the cosine ratio in the right-angled triangle \(XYZ\). For part (b), subtract the area of the triangle from the area of the semicircle to find the shaded region.

steps.title: (a) Find the length of XY
steps.bodyLatex: In the right-angled triangle \(XYZ\), the hypotenuse is the diameter \(XZ = 16\text{ cm}\). Since we know the angle \(\angle YXZ = 30^\circ\) and we want to find the adjacent side \(XY\), we use the cosine ratio: \[ \cos(30^\circ) = \frac{XY}{16} \]

steps.title: (a) Solve for XY
steps.bodyLatex: Rearranging the equation gives \(XY = 16 \times \cos(30^\circ)\). Calculating this, we get \(XY \approx 13.8564\dots\text{ cm}\). Rounding to two decimal places, we find \(XY = 13.86\text{ cm}\).

steps.title: (b) Find the area of the semicircle
steps.bodyLatex: The radius \(r\) of the semicircle is half the diameter, so \(r = 8\text{ cm}\). The area of the semicircle is \(\frac{1}{2} \pi r^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times \pi \times 8^2 \approx 100.531\text{ cm}^2\).

steps.title: (b) Find the area of the triangle
steps.bodyLatex: First, find the height \(YZ\) using \(\sin(30^\circ) = \frac{YZ}{16}\), so \(YZ = 16 \times \sin(30^\circ) = 8\text{ cm}\). The area of triangle \(XYZ\) is \(\frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \times 13.8564 \times 8 = 55.4256\text{ cm}^2\).

steps.title: (b) Calculate the shaded area
steps.bodyLatex: Subtract the triangle area from the semicircle area: \(\text{Shaded Area} = 100.531 - 55.4256 = 45.1054\dots\text{ cm}^2\). Rounding to one decimal place, we get \(45.1\text{ cm}^2\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to set the calculator to degree mode, which leads to incorrect trigonometric values.
Confusing the sides of the triangle (e.g., using sine instead of cosine to find the adjacent side).
Using the full circle area formula instead of the semicircle formula.
Rounding intermediate values too early, which causes inaccuracies in the final answer.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check: \(13.86^2 + 8^2 = 192.0996 + 64 = 256.0996\), which is approximately \(16^2 = 256\). The area values are positive and physically reasonable for a shape inscribed in a semicircle of radius 8.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to use a linear regression model for prediction, interpret the gradient of a line in context, and evaluate the strength of a relationship using the correlation coefficient.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), substitute the given value into the provided equation. For part (b), compare the absolute values of the correlation coefficients to determine which variable has a stronger relationship with the temperature.

steps.title: (a) (i) Predict the temperature
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the height \(x = 540\) into the regression equation \(y = 29.2 - 0.011x\). This gives \(y = 29.2 - 0.011 \times 540 = 29.2 - 5.94 = 23.26\). Rounding to one decimal place, we get \(23.3^\circ\text{C}\).

steps.title: (a) (ii) Interpret the gradient
steps.bodyLatex: The gradient of \(m = -0.011\) indicates the rate of change. In this context, for every \(1\text{ m}\) increase in height above sea level, the average maximum daily temperature is expected to decrease by \(0.011^\circ\text{C}\).

steps.title: (b) Choose the better predictor
steps.bodyLatex: We compare the strength of the relationships using the correlation coefficients: \(|r_{\text{height}}| = |-0.494| = 0.494\) and \(|r_{\text{latitude}}| = |-0.897| = 0.897\). Since \(0.897 > 0.494\), the relationship with latitude is stronger.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a)(i) \(23.3^\circ\text{C}\); (a)(ii) For a \(1\text{ m}\) increase in height, the temperature decreases by \(0.011^\circ\text{C}\); (b) Latitude, because it has a stronger correlation coefficient.
commonMistakesLatex: Failing to include the units (\(^\circ\text{C}\)) in the final answer for part (a)(i).
Confusing the direction of the change when interpreting the negative gradient.
Assuming the variable with the smaller correlation coefficient value is stronger, forgetting that strength is determined by how close the value is to -1 or 1.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: For (a)(i), verify the calculation: \(29.2 - 5.94 = 23.26\). For (b), confirm that \(| -0.897 | > | -0.494 |\), meaning latitude provides a more reliable prediction.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to model a real-world scenario using simultaneous linear equations and solve them graphically.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We will derive a second equation based on the total number of legs, then plot both linear equations on the provided grid. The solution is found at the point where the two lines intersect.

steps.title: Step 1: Formulate the second equation
steps.bodyLatex: We know goannas have \(4\) legs and emus have \(2\) legs, with a total of \(76\) legs. This gives the equation \(4x + 2y = 76\). To make graphing easier, we can simplify this by dividing by \(2\), resulting in \(2x + y = 38\), or \(y = -2x + 38\).

steps.title: Step 2: Prepare the first equation for graphing
steps.bodyLatex: The first equation is \(x + y = 31\). Rearranging this to make \(y\) the subject gives \(y = -x + 31\).

steps.title: Step 3: Plot the lines
steps.bodyLatex: For \(y = -x + 31\), plot points like \((0, 31)\) and \((31, 0)\). For \(y = -2x + 38\), plot points like \((0, 38)\) and \((19, 0)\). Use a sharp pencil and a ruler to draw these two straight lines accurately across the grid.

steps.title: Step 4: Identify the intersection
steps.bodyLatex: Locate the point where the two lines cross. By reading the coordinates of this intersection point from the grid, we find \(x = 7\) and \(y = 24\).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: Number of goannas = \(7\), Number of emus = \(24\)
commonMistakesLatex: Failing to use a ruler, leading to inaccurate intersection points.
Difficulty rearranging equations into the form \(y = mx + c\) to make plotting easier.
Mixing up which variable represents which animal (x for goannas, y for emus).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the totals: \(7 + 24 = 31\) animals (correct). Check the legs: \(4(7) + 2(24) = 28 + 48 = 76\) legs (correct).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret a quadratic model for revenue and apply it to a real-world pricing scenario. You need to find the vertex of a parabola to maximise a value and identify the vertical intercept.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), use the formula for the axis of symmetry, \(x = -b / (2a)\), to find the value of \(x\) that maximises revenue, then add this to the base price of \(10\). For part (b), the vertical intercept occurs when \(x = 0\), which is simply the constant term in the quadratic equation.

steps.title: (a) Find the x-value for maximum revenue
steps.bodyLatex: The revenue model is \(R = -50x^2 + 4500x + 50000\). This is a downward-opening parabola where \(a = -50\) and \(b = 4500\). The maximum occurs at the axis of symmetry, given by \(x = -b / (2a) = -4500 / (2 \times -50) = -4500 / -100 = 45\).

steps.title: (a) Calculate the optimal price
steps.bodyLatex: The problem states the price is \((10 + x)\). Substituting our value for \(x\), the optimal price is \(10 + 45 = 55\). Therefore, the publisher should charge \(55\) per book.

steps.title: (b) Find the vertical intercept
steps.bodyLatex: The vertical intercept occurs when \(x = 0\). Substituting \(x = 0\) into the equation: \(R = -50(0)^2 + 4500(0) + 50000 = 50000\). The vertical intercept is \(50000\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to add the base price of 10 to the value of x found.
Confusing the x-intercept with the y-intercept.
Incorrectly calculating the axis of symmetry by forgetting the double negative in the formula.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: To check part (a), test a value slightly higher or lower than \(45\), such as \(44\) or \(46\); both yield a lower revenue than when \(x = 45\). For part (b), the intercept represents the revenue when \(x = 0\), which is the initial condition provided in the question (\(5000\) copies at \(10\) each = \(50000\)).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to perform forward and backward scanning on a project network diagram to determine the project duration, critical path, and task float.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To find the minimum completion time, perform a forward pass to find the Earliest Start Time (EST) for each node. The critical path is the sequence of tasks where the total float is zero. The float for a task is calculated using the formula: \( \text{Float} = \text{Latest Start Time (LST)} - \text{Earliest Start Time (EST)} \).

steps.title: (a) Find the minimum completion time
steps.bodyLatex: Perform a forward pass through the network by adding task times along each path. The paths are: \( A+C+D+E+G+J+K = 5+7+2+3+5+8+10 = 40 \) minutes, and \( B+F+H+I+K = 8+6+6+3+10 = 33 \) minutes. The minimum time to complete the project is the longest path, which is \( 40 \) minutes.

steps.title: (b) State the critical path
steps.bodyLatex: The critical path is the path with the longest duration, which determines the project's minimum completion time. Since the path \( A-C-D-E-G-J-K \) totals \( 40 \) minutes, this is the critical path.

steps.title: (c) Add task X and calculate float time
steps.bodyLatex: Task \( X \) has an \( \text{EST} = 17 \) and an \( \text{LST} = 18 \). The float time is calculated as \( \text{LST} - \text{EST} = 18 - 17 = 1 \) minute. To add task \( X \) to the diagram, place it starting at the node reached at \( 17 \) minutes (the node after \( E \)) and ending at the node reached at \( 29 \) minutes (\( 17 + 12 \)).

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) 40 minutes; (b) A - C - D - E - G - J - K; (c) Float time = 1 minute.
commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the minimum completion time of the project with the shortest path through the network.
Miscalculating the float by using the wrong formula or confusing EST and LST values.
Failing to correctly identify the node where the EST is 17 minutes when adding task X.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the critical path by ensuring all tasks on it have zero float (LST = EST). For task X, \( 18 - 17 = 1 \), which confirms the float calculation is consistent with the provided data.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to use the Sine Rule to solve for an unknown angle in a non-right-angled triangle. It specifically requires you to identify and calculate an obtuse angle.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Since we have matching pairs of sides and opposite angles, we use the Sine Rule: \( \frac{\sin A}{a} = \frac{\sin B}{b} \). Because the angle \( \angle ABC \) is obtuse, we must subtract the calculator's acute result from \( 180^\circ \).

steps.title: Set up the Sine Rule
steps.bodyLatex: We identify the known pairs: side \( AC = 25 \text{ cm} \) is opposite \( \angle ABC \), and side \( BC = 16 \text{ cm} \) is opposite \( \angle BAC = 28^\circ \). We set up the equation: \[ \frac{\sin \angle ABC}{25} = \frac{\sin 28^\circ}{16} \]

steps.title: Solve for \( \sin \angle ABC \)
steps.bodyLatex: Multiply both sides by \( 25 \) to isolate \( \sin \angle ABC \): \[ \sin \angle ABC = \frac{25 \times \sin 28^\circ}{16} \approx 0.7334 \]

steps.title: Find the obtuse angle
steps.bodyLatex: The calculator gives the acute angle \( \arcsin(0.7334) \approx 47.18^\circ \). Since the question specifies that \( \angle ABC \) is obtuse, we use the supplementary angle property: \[ \angle ABC = 180^\circ - 47.18^\circ = 132.82^\circ \]

steps.title: Final rounding
steps.bodyLatex: Rounding to the nearest degree, we get: \[ \angle ABC \approx 133^\circ \]

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that the sine rule gives the acute angle and failing to subtract it from 180 degrees.
Attempting to use Pythagoras' theorem, which only applies to right-angled triangles.
Using the Cosine Rule unnecessarily, which is more complex and prone to algebraic errors here.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: You can verify this by checking that the sum of angles in the triangle is 180 degrees. If \( \angle ABC \approx 133^\circ \) and \( \angle BAC = 28^\circ \), then \( \angle ACB \approx 19^\circ \). Using the Sine Rule again, \( \frac{16}{\sin 28^\circ} \approx \frac{25}{\sin 133^\circ} \approx 34.06 \), which confirms the consistency of the values.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret standard normal distribution tables and apply z-score calculations to real-world data.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), use the property that the total area to the right of the mean is \( 0.5 \). For part (b), convert the raw score to a z-score using \( z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} \), then use the probability found in part (a) to calculate the expected frequency.

steps.title: (a) Calculating the tail probability
steps.bodyLatex: The total area under the normal curve to the right of the mean (\( 0 \)) is \( 0.5 \). The table provides the area between \( 0 \) and \( 0.3 \), which is \( 0.1179 \). Therefore, the probability of being greater than \( 0.3 \) is the remainder: \( P(Z > 0.3) = 0.5 - 0.1179 = 0.3821 \).

steps.title: (b) Calculating the z-score
steps.bodyLatex: To find how many babies weigh more than \( 3471 \text{ g} \), we first standardise the value using the formula \( z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} \), where \( \mu = 3300 \) and \( \sigma = 570 \). Substituting these values gives \( z = \frac{3471 - 3300}{570} = \frac{171}{570} = 0.3 \).

steps.title: (b) Finding the expected number
steps.bodyLatex: From part (a), we know that the probability of a z-score being greater than \( 0.3 \) is \( 0.3821 \). To find the expected number of babies out of \( 1000 \), multiply the total population by this probability: \( 1000 \times 0.3821 = 382.1 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting that the area to the right of the mean is 0.5, leading to incorrect subtraction.
Failing to link part (b) to the result in part (a), causing unnecessary recalculation.
Incorrectly applying the z-score formula by swapping the mean and the raw score.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the z-score calculation: \( 3300 + 0.3 \times 570 = 3300 + 171 = 3471 \). The logic holds.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to use non-right-angled trigonometry, specifically the area formula for triangles and the Cosine Rule, within the context of a radial survey.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), use the area formula \( \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}ab \sin C \) to solve for the unknown angle. For part (b), first determine the included angle \( \angle DOC \) using the bearings, then apply the Cosine Rule to find the side length \( CD \), and sum the sides to find the perimeter.

steps.title: (a) Find the size of angle COB
steps.bodyLatex: Using the area formula \( \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times OC \times OB \times \sin(\angle COB) \), substitute the known values: \( 466 = \frac{1}{2} \times 28 \times 35 \times \sin(\angle COB) \). This simplifies to \( 466 = 490 \times \sin(\angle COB) \), so \( \sin(\angle COB) = \frac{466}{490} \approx 0.95102 \). Taking the inverse sine, we get \( \angle COB = \sin^{-1}(0.95102) \approx 72.006^\circ \), which is \( 72^\circ \) to the nearest degree.

steps.title: (b) Find the length of angle DOC
steps.bodyLatex: The bearings of \( D \) and \( C \) are \( 330^\circ \) and \( 150^\circ \) respectively. The angle \( \angle DOC \) is the difference between these bearings: \( 330^\circ - 150^\circ = 180^\circ \). However, looking at the diagram, we need the angle between the lines \( OD \) and \( OC \). Since \( C \) is at \( 150^\circ \) and \( D \) is at \( 330^\circ \), the angle between them is \( 330^\circ - 150^\circ = 180^\circ \), but we must subtract the angle \( \angle COB \) calculated earlier to isolate the specific triangle sector. The angle \( \angle DOC = 330^\circ - 150^\circ - 72^\circ = 108^\circ \).

steps.title: (b) Calculate the length of CD and the perimeter
steps.bodyLatex: Use the Cosine Rule: \( CD^2 = OD^2 + OC^2 - 2(OD)(OC)\cos(\angle DOC) \). Substituting the values: \( CD^2 = 31^2 + 28^2 - 2(31)(28)\cos(108^\circ) \). This gives \( CD^2 \approx 961 + 784 - 1736(-0.3090) \approx 2281.45 \), so \( CD \approx \sqrt{2281.45} \approx 47.76 \text{ m} \). The total fencing is the sum of the three sides: \( 31 + 28 + 47.76 = 106.76 \text{ m} \), which rounds to \( 106.8 \text{ m} \).

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming the triangles are right-angled and using simple SOH CAH TOA.
Incorrectly calculating the angle between bearings by not accounting for the full radial layout.
Rounding intermediate values too early, which leads to an inaccurate final answer.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the area: \( 0.5 \times 28 \times 35 \times \sin(72^\circ) \approx 466.0 \text{ m}^2 \). The perimeter calculation follows directly from the Cosine Rule, confirming the result is consistent with the provided side lengths.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate the future value of an annuity using a provided table and then apply the compound interest formula to that accumulated sum.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: The problem is solved in two distinct stages. First, find the future value of the annuity after 8 years using the table, then treat that total as a lump sum earning compound interest for the final 2 years.

steps.title: Step 1: Calculate the future value after 8 years
steps.bodyLatex: Using the table for \( 8 \) periods at an interest rate of \( 0.75\% \), we find the future value interest factor is \( 8.2132 \). Since Simone deposits \( 1000 \) each year, the total amount after 8 years is: \[ 1000 \times 8.2132 = 8213.20 \]

steps.title: Step 2: Calculate the final amount after 2 more years
steps.bodyLatex: The amount \( 8213.20 \) now earns compound interest at \( 1.25\% \) per annum for \( 2 \) years. We use the compound interest formula \( A = P(1+r)^n \), where \( P = 8213.20 \), \( r = 0.0125 \), and \( n = 2 \): \[ A = 8213.20 \times (1 + 0.0125)^2 \]

steps.title: Step 3: Final calculation
steps.bodyLatex: Evaluating the expression: \[ A = 8213.20 \times (1.0125)^2 = 8213.20 \times 1.02515625 = 8419.811... \] Rounding to the nearest cent, we get \( 8419.81 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Using the wrong interest rate or period from the table, such as selecting the 1.25% column for the first 8 years.
Forgetting to convert the percentage 1.25% to the decimal 0.0125 when using the compound interest formula.
Treating the final two years as another annuity instead of a lump sum compound interest calculation.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: The result is slightly higher than the initial 8213.20, which is expected as the money continues to grow with interest over the final two years.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to use the properties of the normal distribution, specifically the empirical rule, to determine population parameters and apply them to a related group.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the empirical rule to identify z-scores for the given percentages. By setting up equations based on the mean \( \mu \) and standard deviation \( \sigma \), we solve for these values and then apply the provided multipliers to find the male height.

steps.title: Step 1: Identify z-scores for females
steps.bodyLatex: Using the empirical rule, \( 97.5\% \) corresponds to \( 2 \) standard deviations above the mean (\( z = 2 \)), and \( 16\% \) corresponds to \( 1 \) standard deviation below the mean (\( z = -1 \)).

steps.title: Step 2: Calculate \( \mu \) and \( \sigma \) for females
steps.bodyLatex: We have two equations: \( \mu + 2\sigma = 175 \) and \( \mu - 1\sigma = 160.6 \). Subtracting the second from the first gives \( 3\sigma = 14.4 \), so \( \sigma = 4.8 \). Substituting back, \( \mu - 4.8 = 160.6 \), so \( \mu = 165.4 \).

steps.title: Step 3: Determine male parameters
steps.bodyLatex: The mean height for males is \( 1.05\mu = 1.05 \times 165.4 = 173.67 \). The standard deviation for males is \( 1.1\sigma = 1.1 \times 4.8 = 5.28 \).

steps.title: Step 4: Calculate the specific male height
steps.bodyLatex: A male taller than \( 84\% \) of the population is \( 1 \) standard deviation above the mean (since \( 50\% + 34\% = 84\% \)). Thus, the height is \( 173.67 + 5.28 = 178.95 \text{ cm} \).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the percentages with z-scores or failing to use the empirical rule correctly.
Misinterpreting algebraic expressions like \(1.05\mu\) as a constant rather than a multiplier.
Forgetting to apply the multipliers (1.05 and 1.1) to both the mean and standard deviation respectively.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the logic: \( 175 - 160.6 = 14.4 \) which represents \( 3 \) standard deviations. \( 14.4 / 3 = 4.8 \). The calculation \( 173.67 + 5.28 \) is straightforward addition, confirming the result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to identify the graph of a quadratic function by analysing its key features.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We identify the features of the function \(y = -x^2 + 1\). The negative coefficient of \(x^2\) indicates a downward-opening parabola, and the constant term \(+1\) indicates the \(y\)-intercept.

steps.title: Identify the shape
steps.bodyLatex: The equation is in the form \(y = ax^2 + c\). Since the coefficient of \(x^2\) is \(-1\) (which is negative), the graph must be a parabola that opens downwards.

steps.title: Find the y-intercept
steps.bodyLatex: To find the \(y\)-intercept, we set \(x = 0\). Substituting this into the equation gives \(y = -(0)^2 + 1 = 1\). Therefore, the graph must cross the \(y\)-axis at \(y = 1\).

steps.title: Find the x-intercepts
steps.bodyLatex: To find the \(x\)-intercepts, we set \(y = 0\). Solving \(0 = -x^2 + 1\) leads to \(x^2 = 1\), which gives \(x = 1\) and \(x = -1\). The graph must cross the \(x\)-axis at these two points.

steps.title: Match with the options
steps.bodyLatex: Graph C is the only one that is a downward-opening parabola with a \(y\)-intercept at \(1\) and \(x\)-intercepts at \(-1\) and \(1\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing a positive \(x^2\) term (upward parabola) with a negative \(x^2\) term (downward parabola).
Misinterpreting the constant term as the \(x\)-intercept instead of the \(y\)-intercept.
Selecting a linear graph (A) or an exponential graph (B) by failing to recognise the squared term indicates a quadratic relationship.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Test a point: if \(x = 0\), \(y = 1\). If \(x = 1\), \(y = -(1)^2 + 1 = 0\). These points match the features shown in graph C.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to convert a decimal number into standard form (scientific notation) while correctly applying rounding rules for significant figures.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To convert to standard form, move the decimal point until there is one non-zero digit to the left of the decimal. Then, count the number of places moved to determine the exponent of \(10\), and round the coefficient to the required number of significant figures.

steps.title: Identify the non-zero digits
steps.bodyLatex: The number is \(0.002073\). The first non-zero digit is \(2\). We need to move the decimal point to the right of this \(2\), which gives us \(2.073\).

steps.title: Determine the power of 10
steps.bodyLatex: To move the decimal from its original position to after the \(2\), we shift it \(3\) places to the right. Since we are moving to the right for a number less than \(1\), the exponent is negative: \(10^{-3}\).

steps.title: Round to two significant figures
steps.bodyLatex: We have \(2.073 \times 10^{-3}\). To keep two significant figures, we look at the first two digits, \(2.0\). Because the next digit is \(7\) (which is \(5\) or greater), we round the \(0\) up to \(1\), resulting in \(2.1 \times 10^{-3}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the number of decimal places with the number of significant figures.
Incorrectly rounding the second digit when the third digit is 5 or greater.
Using a positive exponent instead of a negative exponent for a number less than 1.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check by expanding the answer: \(2.1 \times 10^{-3} = 2.1 \div 1000 = 0.0021\), which is the correct rounded value of \(0.002073\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate time using the relationship between distance, speed, and time.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Use the formula \( \text{Time} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Speed}} \) to find the duration in hours, then convert the decimal portion into minutes by multiplying by \( 60 \).

steps.title: Calculate time in hours
steps.bodyLatex: Given the distance \( d = 75 \text{ km} \) and the speed \( v = 50 \text{ km/h} \), we calculate the time \( t \) as: \[ t = \frac{75}{50} = 1.5 \text{ hours} \]

steps.title: Convert to hours and minutes
steps.bodyLatex: The \( 1.5 \text{ hours} \) consists of \( 1 \text{ hour} \) and \( 0.5 \text{ hours} \). Since \( 1 \text{ hour} = 60 \text{ minutes} \), we calculate \( 0.5 \times 60 = 30 \text{ minutes} \). Thus, the total time is \( 1 \text{ hour } 30 \text{ minutes} \).

commonMistakesLatex: Mistaking the decimal \( 0.5 \) hours for \( 50 \) minutes instead of \( 30 \) minutes.
Using the incorrect formula \( \text{Time} = \text{Distance} \times \text{Speed} \) resulting in \( 3750 \text{ hours} \).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by checking the speed: \( \frac{75 \text{ km}}{1.5 \text{ hours}} = 50 \text{ km/h} \), which matches the given information.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate the future value of an investment using the compound interest formula with non-annual compounding periods.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the compound interest formula \( A = P(1 + r)^n \), where \( P \) is the principal, \( r \) is the interest rate per compounding period, and \( n \) is the total number of periods.

steps.title: Identify the variables
steps.bodyLatex: The principal \( P = 200 \). The annual interest rate is \( 3\% \), so the monthly rate is \( r = \frac{0.03}{12} = 0.0025 \). The time is \( 1.5 \) years, so the number of months is \( n = 1.5 \times 12 = 18 \).

steps.title: Apply the formula
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the values into the formula: \( A = 200(1 + 0.0025)^{18} \).

steps.title: Calculate the result
steps.bodyLatex: Calculating this gives \( A = 200(1.0025)^{18} \approx 200 \times 1.045956 \approx 209.1912 \). Rounding to the nearest cent, we get \( 209.19 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Using the annual interest rate \( 0.03 \) instead of dividing it by \( 12 \) for monthly compounding.
Using the time in years \( 1.5 \) instead of converting it to the number of months \( 18 \).
Rounding the interest rate decimal too early, which leads to a slight inaccuracy in the final result.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: A simple sanity check is to calculate simple interest: \( 200 \times 0.03 \times 1.5 = 9 \). Since compound interest should be slightly higher than simple interest, \( 209.19 \) is a logical result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of absolute error and percentage error in the context of measurement precision.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To find the percentage error, we use the formula: \( \text{Percentage Error} = \frac{\text{Absolute Error}}{\text{Measured Value}} \times 100\%. \) The absolute error is half of the smallest unit of measurement.

steps.title: Determine the absolute error
steps.bodyLatex: The measurement is given as \( 16.0 \text{ cm} \), which is correct to one decimal place (the nearest \( 0.1 \text{ cm} \)). The absolute error is half of this precision: \( \text{Absolute Error} = \frac{0.1 \text{ cm}}{2} = 0.05 \text{ cm} \).

steps.title: Calculate the percentage error
steps.bodyLatex: Using the measured value of \( 16.0 \text{ cm} \), we calculate the percentage error as follows: \( \text{Percentage Error} = \left( \frac{0.05}{16.0} \right) \times 100\% \).

steps.title: Simplify the calculation
steps.bodyLatex: Performing the division: \( \frac{0.05}{16.0} = 0.003125 \). Multiplying by \( 100 \) gives \( 0.3125\% \).

commonMistakesLatex: Using the full unit of precision (0.1) instead of half the unit (0.05) for the absolute error.
Forgetting to multiply the final decimal by 100 to convert it into a percentage.
Confusing the absolute error with the measurement itself.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: A quick sanity check: \( 0.3125\% \) of \( 16 \) is \( 0.003125 \times 16 = 0.05 \), which matches our absolute error calculation.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of the slope (gradient) of a linear equation and how changes in the independent variable \(x\) affect the dependent variable \(y\).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We identify the gradient \(m\) from the linear equation in the form \(y = mx + c\). The gradient represents the rate of change, so we multiply the change in \(x\) by the gradient to find the total change in \(y\).

steps.title: Identify the gradient
steps.bodyLatex: The given equation is \(y = -1 - 2x\), which can be rewritten as \(y = -2x - 1\). Comparing this to the standard form \(y = mx + c\), we see that the gradient \(m = -2\).

steps.title: Calculate the change in y
steps.bodyLatex: The gradient \(m\) is defined as \(\frac{\text{change in } y}{\text{change in } x}\). Given that \(x\) increases by \(5\), we have \(\text{change in } x = 5\). Therefore, \(\text{change in } y = m \times (\text{change in } x) = -2 \times 5 = -10\).

steps.title: Interpret the result
steps.bodyLatex: The result of \(-10\) indicates that \(y\) decreases by \(10\). This matches option D.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the gradient with the y-intercept and incorrectly using \(-1\) in the calculation.
Ignoring the negative sign in the gradient, leading to the incorrect conclusion that \(y\) increases.
Attempting to substitute specific values for \(x\) and making arithmetic errors.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Pick a value for \(x\), such as \(x = 0\). Then \(y = -1\). If \(x\) increases by \(5\), the new \(x = 5\). The new \(y = -1 - 2(5) = -1 - 10 = -11\). The difference is \(-11 - (-1) = -10\), confirming \(y\) decreases by \(10\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to identify the shape of a data distribution from a histogram, specifically focusing on positive skewness.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recall that skewness refers to the direction of the 'tail' of the distribution. A positively skewed distribution has a long tail extending towards the higher values on the right side of the horizontal axis.

steps.title: Understand Positive Skew
steps.bodyLatex: A distribution is positively skewed (or right-skewed) when the majority of the data is clustered on the left, and the tail of the distribution stretches out towards the right, where the higher values are located.

steps.title: Analyze the Options
steps.bodyLatex: Looking at the histograms: Option A shows a peak on the left with a tail tapering off to the right. Option B shows a peak on the right with a tail on the left (negatively skewed). Option C is symmetrical, and Option D is uniform.

steps.title: Select the Correct Histogram
steps.bodyLatex: Since Option A is the only histogram where the tail extends towards the positive direction (the right), it represents a positively skewed dataset.

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing positive skew with negative skew by looking at where the peak is rather than where the tail is.
Assuming 'positive' means the peak is on the right side of the graph.
Mixing up skewness with symmetry or uniform distributions.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the tail: In A, the tail points to the right (positive direction). In B, the tail points to the left (negative direction). This confirms A is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to compare performance across different datasets using z-scores, which measure how many standard deviations a score is from the mean.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To determine the relative strength of each subject, calculate the z-score for each test using the formula \( z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} \), where \( x \) is John's score, \( \mu \) is the mean, and \( \sigma \) is the standard deviation. A higher z-score indicates a stronger relative performance.

steps.title: Calculate the z-score for French
steps.bodyLatex: For French, \( x = 82 \), \( \mu = 70 \), and \( \sigma = 8 \). The z-score is \( z = \frac{82 - 70}{8} = \frac{12}{8} = 1.5 \).

steps.title: Calculate the z-score for Commerce
steps.bodyLatex: For Commerce, \( x = 80 \), \( \mu = 65 \), and \( \sigma = 5 \). The z-score is \( z = \frac{80 - 65}{5} = \frac{15}{5} = 3.0 \).

steps.title: Compare the results
steps.bodyLatex: Comparing the z-scores: Commerce (\( 3.0 \)) is the highest, making it the strongest subject. French (\( 1.5 \)) is the lowest, making it the weakest subject.

commonMistakesLatex: Mistaking the highest raw score (82 in French) for the strongest performance without considering the mean and standard deviation.
Incorrectly calculating the z-score by swapping the mean and the score in the numerator.
Confusing the standard deviation with the variance or misinterpreting what a higher z-score represents.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by checking the gaps: Commerce is \( 3 \) standard deviations above the mean, Music is \( 2 \) above, and French is only \( 1.5 \) above. The order \( 3.0 > 2.0 > 1.5 \) confirms A is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to identify a bipartite graph, which represents connections between two distinct groups where no connections exist within the same group.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Since each member of Team \(A\) plays each member of Team \(B\), we are looking for a complete bipartite graph with \(3\) vertices in each set. This means there should be no edges connecting vertices within the same team, and each vertex must have a degree of \(3\).

steps.title: Analyze the problem structure
steps.bodyLatex: We have two teams of \(3\) members each, making a total of \(6\) vertices. Because players only play against members of the opposing team, there should be no lines connecting members of the same team.

steps.title: Calculate the required number of games
steps.bodyLatex: Each of the \(3\) members in Team \(A\) plays \(3\) members in Team \(B\). Therefore, the total number of games is \(3 \times 3 = 9\). This means the correct network diagram must have exactly \(9\) edges.

steps.title: Evaluate the options
steps.bodyLatex: Diagram A has \(6\) edges, C has \(12\) edges, and D has \(15\) edges. Diagram B is the only one with exactly \(9\) edges, where each of the \(6\) vertices is connected to the \(3\) vertices of the opposite team.

commonMistakesLatex: Assuming all players play everyone else (a complete graph), which would lead to choosing D.
Miscounting the number of edges required for the matches.
Failing to recognize that a bipartite graph cannot have edges between members of the same team.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: A bipartite graph with \(m=3\) and \(n=3\) vertices must have \(m \times n = 9\) edges. Counting the edges in diagram B confirms there are \(9\) connections, which matches our calculation.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to translate a real-world word problem into a linear algebraic equation, specifically focusing on unit conversion.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Identify the fixed cost and the variable cost. Since the variable cost is given per minute but the time is given in hours, you must convert the hourly rate into a per-minute rate or convert the time into minutes.

steps.title: Identify the components
steps.bodyLatex: The total cost \(C\) consists of a fixed call-out fee of \(90\) and a variable charge. The variable charge is \(2\) per minute.

steps.title: Convert units
steps.bodyLatex: Since there are \(60\) minutes in \(1\) hour, working for \(t\) hours is equivalent to working for \(60t\) minutes.

steps.title: Formulate the equation
steps.bodyLatex: The variable charge is \(2\) multiplied by the number of minutes, which is \(2 \times 60t = 120t\). Adding the fixed call-out fee of \(90\), we get the total cost equation: \(C = 90 + 120t\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to convert hours to minutes, leading to the incorrect choice B.
Confusing the fixed fee with the hourly rate.
Misinterpreting the variable rate as applying to the hour rather than the minute.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: If the plumber works for \(1\) hour (\(t=1\)), the cost should be \(90 + (60 \times 2) = 210\). Substituting \(t=1\) into option D gives \(90 + 120(1) = 210\), which confirms the equation is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply the declining-balance depreciation formula over multiple compounding periods.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the declining-balance formula \(S = V(1 - r)^n\), where \(V\) is the initial value, \(r\) is the depreciation rate per period, and \(n\) is the total number of periods. Since the rate is per half-year, we must calculate the total number of half-year periods in \(5\) years.

steps.title: Identify the variables
steps.bodyLatex: The initial value is \(V = 10000\). The rate of depreciation is \(r = 8\% = 0.08\). Because there are \(2\) half-years in each year, for \(5\) years, the number of periods is \(n = 5 \times 2 = 10\).

steps.title: Apply the formula
steps.bodyLatex: Substitute the values into the formula: \(S = 10000(1 - 0.08)^{10}\). This simplifies to \(S = 10000(0.92)^{10}\).

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.bodyLatex: Using a calculator, we find \(S = 10000 \times 0.4343884... = 4343.884...\). Rounding to the nearest cent, we get \(S = 4343.88\).

commonMistakesLatex: Using \(n = 5\) instead of \(n = 10\), failing to account for the half-yearly compounding.
Using the straight-line depreciation formula instead of the declining-balance formula.
Incorrectly converting the percentage \(8\%\) to a decimal, such as using \(0.8\) instead of \(0.08\).
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since \(0.92^{10}\) is slightly less than \(0.5\) (because \(0.92^9 \approx 0.47\)), the result should be slightly less than half of \(10000\), which aligns with \(4343.88\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your understanding of Pearson's correlation coefficient, denoted by \( r \), and how it relates to the visual appearance of a scatter plot.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Recall that \( r = -1 \) indicates a perfect negative linear relationship. This means all data points must lie exactly on a straight line that slopes downwards from left to right.

steps.title: Understanding the value of \( r \)
steps.bodyLatex: The correlation coefficient \( r \) ranges from \( -1 \) to \( 1 \). A value of \( r = -1 \) signifies a perfect negative linear correlation, meaning as \( x \) increases, \( y \) decreases at a constant rate.

steps.title: Evaluating the options
steps.bodyLatex: Graph A shows a positive trend (\( r > 0 \)). Graph B shows points that do not form a single straight line. Graph C shows a positive trend (\( r > 0 \)). Graph D shows all points lying perfectly on a straight line sloping downwards.

steps.title: Selecting the correct graph
steps.bodyLatex: Since Graph D is the only one showing a perfect linear relationship with a negative gradient, it must represent \( r = -1 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing a positive correlation (\( r = 1 \)) with a negative correlation (\( r = -1 \)).
Mistaking a non-linear relationship for a perfect linear one.
Assuming that any downward-sloping graph represents \( r = -1 \), even if the points are not perfectly collinear.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by checking if all points in Graph D can be connected by a single straight line with a negative slope; since they can, the correlation is indeed \( -1 \).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply a linear rate of change formula to determine the time required for a specific reduction in blood alcohol content (BAC).
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We calculate the reduction in BAC required, then use the given rate of \(0.015\) BAC units per hour to find the time elapsed. Finally, we add this duration to the starting time of \(10:30 \text{ pm}\).

steps.title: Calculate the required drop in BAC
steps.bodyLatex: Jake starts at a BAC of \(0.08375\) and wants to reach a BAC of \(0.05\). The total drop required is \(0.08375 - 0.05 = 0.03375\).

steps.title: Calculate the time taken
steps.bodyLatex: Using the formula \[ \text{Time} = \frac{\text{BAC drop}}{0.015} \] we substitute the drop: \[ \text{Time} = \frac{0.03375}{0.015} = 2.25 \text{ hours} \]

steps.title: Convert the time to hours and minutes
steps.bodyLatex: Since \(0.25\) of an hour is \(0.25 \times 60 = 15 \text{ minutes}\), the total time elapsed is \(2 \text{ hours and } 15 \text{ minutes}\).

steps.title: Determine the final time
steps.bodyLatex: Adding \(2 \text{ hours and } 15 \text{ minutes}\) to \(10:30 \text{ pm}\): \(10:30 \text{ pm} + 2 \text{ hours} = 12:30 \text{ am}\). Adding the remaining \(15 \text{ minutes}\) gives \(12:45 \text{ am}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Using the full BAC value (\(0.08375\)) in the formula instead of the required drop (\(0.03375\)).
Misinterpreting \(2.25 \text{ hours}\) as \(2 \text{ hours and } 25 \text{ minutes}\) instead of \(2 \text{ hours and } 15 \text{ minutes}\).
Failing to correctly transition from PM to AM when crossing midnight.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check: \(12:45 \text{ am}\) is \(2.25 \text{ hours}\) after \(10:30 \text{ pm}\). Multiplying \(2.25 \text{ hours} \times 0.015 \text{ BAC/hour} = 0.03375 \text{ BAC drop}\). Subtracting this from \(0.08375\) gives \(0.05\), which confirms the result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your conceptual understanding of the time value of money, specifically the relationship between the total nominal value of payments and their present and future values.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Think about the impact of compound interest. The present value discounts future payments back to today, while the future value accumulates interest on past payments.

steps.title: Analyze the total nominal value
steps.bodyLatex: The annuity consists of \(10\) payments of \(1000\). The total nominal value without considering interest is \(10 \times 1000 = 10000\).

steps.title: Evaluate the Present Value (PV)
steps.bodyLatex: The present value is calculated by discounting each payment back to \(30\) June \(2020\). Since each payment is moved back in time, the present value must be less than the total nominal sum of \(10000\).

steps.title: Evaluate the Future Value (FV)
steps.bodyLatex: The future value is calculated by accumulating interest on each payment up to \(30\) June \(2030\). Since interest is added to the payments over time, the future value must be greater than the total nominal sum of \(10000\).

steps.title: Compare the values
steps.bodyLatex: Combining these observations, we have \(PV < 10000 < FV\).

commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the definitions of present value and future value.
Assuming the total value of an annuity is always equal to the sum of the payments regardless of interest.
Misinterpreting the time period, leading to an incorrect direction of the inequality.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Since money earns interest over time, a dollar in the future is worth more than a dollar today, confirming that the future value must be the largest and the present value the smallest.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate the probability of independent events by considering all possible outcomes that satisfy a specific condition.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We identify all pairs of (die value, table section) that multiply to give a score of \(6\). Since the die has \(6\) faces and the table has \(8\) sections, there are \(6 \times 8 = 48\) total possible outcomes.

steps.title: Determine the total number of outcomes
steps.bodyLatex: The die has \(6\) possible outcomes and the table has \(8\) sections. Since these are independent, the total number of possible outcomes is \(6 \times 8 = 48\).

steps.title: Identify successful outcomes
steps.bodyLatex:
We need the product of the die face (\(D\)) and the table section (\(S\)) to equal \(6\). We list the pairs \((D, S)\) where \(D \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}\) and \(S \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}\): 
1. If \(D=1\), then \(S=6\). 
2. If \(D=2\), then \(S=3\). 
3. If \(D=3\), then \(S=2\). 
4. If \(D=6\), then \(S=1\). 
Note that \(D=4\) and \(D=5\) have no corresponding integer \(S\) in the range \(1-8\) that results in \(6\).

steps.title: Calculate the probability
steps.bodyLatex: There are \(4\) successful outcomes out of a total of \(48\). The probability is \(\frac{4}{48}\), which simplifies to \(\frac{1}{12}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to include all possible factors of 6 (e.g., missing the pair (6,1)).
Incorrectly calculating the total sample space as 8 or 6 instead of 48.
Confusing the number of sections on the table with the number of faces on the die.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: We found 4 successful outcomes out of 48. Since \(4 \times 12 = 48\), the fraction \(\frac{4}{48}\) correctly simplifies to \(\frac{1}{12}\), confirming option B.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply right-angled trigonometry ratios and Pythagoras' Theorem to solve for unknown angles and side lengths in a triangle.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), use the tangent ratio since you are given the opposite and adjacent sides. For part (b), use Pythagoras' Theorem to find the hypotenuse.

steps.title: (a) Find the value of \(\theta\)
steps.bodyLatex: Identify the sides relative to \(\theta\): the opposite side is \(8\) and the adjacent side is \(10\). Using the tangent ratio, we have \(\tan \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{8}{10}\).

steps.title: (a) Calculate \(\theta\)
steps.bodyLatex: To find \(\theta\), use the inverse tangent function: \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.8)\). Calculating this gives \(\theta \approx 38.6598^\circ\), which rounds to \(39^\circ\) to the nearest degree.

steps.title: (b) Find the value of \(x\)
steps.bodyLatex: Since this is a right-angled triangle, we can use Pythagoras' Theorem: \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\). Substituting our values, we get \(x^2 = 8^2 + 10^2\).

steps.title: (b) Calculate \(x\)
steps.bodyLatex: Simplify the equation: \(x^2 = 64 + 100 = 164\). Therefore, \(x = \sqrt{164}\), which is approximately \(12.806\). Rounding to one decimal place, we get \(x = 12.8\).

commonMistakesLatex: Using the wrong trigonometric ratio (e.g., using sine or cosine instead of tangent).
Forgetting to use the inverse function (\(\tan^{-1}\)) when solving for the angle.
Rounding the answer too early in the calculation, which can lead to accuracy errors.
Incorrectly identifying which side is the hypotenuse.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the result using the sine ratio: \(\sin(38.66^\circ) = \frac{8}{12.806} \approx 0.624\), which matches \(\frac{8}{12.806} \approx 0.624\). The values are consistent.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to use ratios and proportions to estimate a population based on a sample area.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To solve this, calculate the area of both the sample section and the total block of land. Then, set up a proportion to find the total number of trees by assuming the density of trees remains constant across the entire area.

steps.title: Calculate the area of the sample section
steps.bodyLatex: The sample section is a square measuring \(5 \text{ m}\) by \(5 \text{ m}\). The area is calculated as: \[ \text{Area}_{\text{sample}} = 5 \text{ m} \times 5 \text{ m} = 25 \text{ m}^2 \]

steps.title: Calculate the total area of the block
steps.bodyLatex: The entire block is a square measuring \(1000 \text{ m}\) by \(1000 \text{ m}\). The total area is: \[ \text{Area}_{\text{total}} = 1000 \text{ m} \times 1000 \text{ m} = 1\,000\,000 \text{ m}^2 \]

steps.title: Set up the proportion and solve for the total number of trees
steps.bodyLatex: Let \(x\) be the total number of trees. Using the ratio of trees to area: \[ \frac{8}{25} = \frac{x}{1\,000\,000} \] Multiplying both sides by \(1\,000\,000\), we get: \[ x = \frac{8 \times 1\,000\,000}{25} = \frac{8\,000\,000}{25} = 320\,000 \]

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to calculate the area of the squares and instead trying to use the side lengths directly in the ratio.
Errors in place value when multiplying \(1000 \times 1000\).
Failing to show the step-by-step working, which is required to earn full marks.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by checking the density: \(8 \div 25 = 0.32 \text{ trees/m}^2\). Multiplying this density by the total area gives \(0.32 \times 1\,000\,000 = 320\,000\), which confirms the result.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to identify a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) in a weighted network using Kruskal's or Prim's algorithm and to update the MST when a new vertex is added.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To find the MST, use Kruskal's algorithm: list all edges by weight and add them one by one, ensuring no cycles are created until all vertices are connected. For the revised network, simply connect the new vertex to the existing tree using the edge with the smallest weight.

steps.title: (a) Find the Minimum Spanning Tree
steps.bodyLatex: List the edges in increasing order of weight: \(GH=1\), \(AB=2\), \(DE=2\), \(CF=2\), \(FG=2\), \(FH=2\), \(BC=3\), \(BE=3\), \(CD=3\), \(DH=4\), \(CG=5\). Starting with the smallest, add edges that do not form a cycle: add \(GH(1)\), \(AB(2)\), \(DE(2)\), \(CF(2)\), \(FG(2)\), \(FH(2)\), \(BC(3)\). We now have \(8\) vertices connected with \(7\) edges, which forms a spanning tree.

steps.title: (a) Calculate the Minimum Length
steps.bodyLatex: Sum the weights of the edges included in the tree: \(1 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 3 = 14\). Thus, the minimum length of the spanning tree is \(14\).

steps.title: (b) Revised Network with Vertex K
steps.bodyLatex: To connect the new vertex \(K\) to the existing MST, we choose the edge with the smallest weight connecting \(K\) to the existing tree. Comparing \(AK=12\) and \(CK=10\), we choose the edge \(CK\) with weight \(10\). The new total length is the previous MST length plus the new edge: \(14 + 10 = 24\).

commonMistakesLatex: Creating a cycle by including too many edges, which means it is no longer a tree.
Forgetting to include all vertices in the spanning tree, resulting in a disconnected graph.
Miscalculating the sum of the weights of the chosen edges.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: A spanning tree for \(n\) vertices must have \(n-1\) edges. In part (a), there are \(8\) vertices and we used \(7\) edges. In part (b), there are \(9\) vertices and we used \(8\) edges, which is correct.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to interpret a quadratic model for area and use algebraic substitution to solve for dimensions.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), use the provided graph to identify values of \(x\) where \(A = 30,000\). For (b) and (c), find the vertex of the parabola to maximize area and substitute back into the linear fencing constraint.

steps.title: (a) Find the largest x for A = 30,000
steps.bodyLatex: Locate \(30,000\) on the vertical axis (Area) and move horizontally to intersect the curve. You will find two points of intersection at \(x = 100\) and \(x = 200\). Since the question asks for the largest value, the answer is \(x = 200\text{ m}\).

steps.title: (b) Maximize the area
steps.bodyLatex: The maximum area occurs at the vertex of the parabola. Looking at the graph, the peak is exactly halfway between \(x = 0\) and \(x = 300\), which is \(x = 150\text{ m}\). To find \(y\), substitute \(x = 150\) into the fencing equation: \(3(150) + 2y = 900\), which simplifies to \(450 + 2y = 900\), so \(2y = 450\), giving \(y = 225\text{ m}\).

steps.title: (c) Calculate the largest area
steps.bodyLatex: Using the dimensions found in part (b), calculate the area: \(A = x \times y = 150 \times 225 = 33,750\text{ m}^2\).

commonMistakesLatex: Choosing the smaller x-value (100) instead of the larger one (200) in part (a).
Confusing the x-axis variable with the y-dimension of the paddock.
Failing to use the linear constraint equation to solve for the second variable.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the fencing constraint: \(3(150) + 2(225) = 450 + 450 = 900\text{ m}\). This matches the total fencing provided.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate income tax using a progressive tax bracket table and determine a tax refund based on PAYG instalments.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, identify the correct tax bracket for Wally's income. Then, calculate the total tax payable using the table's formula and subtract the total PAYG tax paid throughout the year to find the refund.

steps.title: Step 1: Identify the correct tax bracket
steps.bodyLatex: Wally's taxable income is \( 122,680 \). Looking at the table, this falls into the \( 90,001 - 180,000 \) bracket. The tax for this bracket is \( 20,797 \) plus \( 0.37 \) for each \( 1 \) over \( 90,000 \).

steps.title: Step 2: Calculate the total tax payable
steps.bodyLatex: Calculate the amount over the threshold: \( 122,680 - 90,000 = 32,680 \). Now, apply the tax rate: \( 20,797 + 0.37 \times 32,680 = 20,797 + 12,091.60 = 32,888.60 \). The total tax payable is \( 32,888.60 \).

steps.title: Step 3: Calculate total PAYG tax paid
steps.bodyLatex: Wally paid \( 3,000 \) per month for \( 12 \) months. The total PAYG tax paid is \( 3,000 \times 12 = 36,000 \).

steps.title: Step 4: Calculate the tax refund
steps.bodyLatex: The refund is the difference between the tax paid and the tax owed: \( 36,000 - 32,888.60 = 3,111.40 \).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to multiply the monthly PAYG payment by 12 to get the annual total.
Incorrectly applying the tax rate to the entire income instead of just the amount over the threshold.
Subtracting the tax payable from the income instead of the PAYG total.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by adding the tax payable and the refund: \( 32,888.60 + 3,111.40 = 36,000 \), which matches the total PAYG paid.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to reverse a percentage increase caused by inflation to find the original price.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Since the price increased by \(2\%\), the current price represents \(102\%\) of the original price. We use the formula \(PV = \frac{FV}{1 + r}\), where \(FV\) is the future value and \(r\) is the inflation rate expressed as a decimal.

steps.title: Identify the variables
steps.bodyLatex: We know the future value \(FV = \$122\) and the inflation rate \(r = 2\% = 0.02\). We need to find the present value \(PV\), which is the cost in January 2019.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.bodyLatex: The relationship between the original price and the new price is given by the equation: \[PV \times (1 + 0.02) = 122\]

steps.title: Solve for the original price
steps.bodyLatex: To isolate \(PV\), divide both sides by \(1.02\): \[PV = \frac{122}{1.02}\] Calculating this gives \(PV = 119.6078...\)

steps.title: Round to the nearest cent
steps.bodyLatex: Since we are dealing with currency, we round the result to two decimal places: \[PV \approx \$119.61\]

commonMistakesLatex: Calculating \(2\%\) of \$122 and subtracting it (i.e., \(122 - 2.44 = 119.56\)), which is incorrect because the inflation applies to the original price, not the new price.
Multiplying by \(0.98\) instead of dividing by \(1.02\).
Failing to recognise that the \$122 represents \(102\%\) of the original value.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: To verify, multiply the original price by the inflation factor: \(119.61 \times 1.02 = 122.0022\), which rounds back to the original \$122.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply the compound interest formula to a credit card balance where interest is calculated daily and a payment is made mid-period.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: We use the compound interest formula \(A = P(1 + r)^n\) to calculate the balance after 15 days of interest, then subtract the payment made by Nisa.

steps.title: Identify the variables
steps.bodyLatex: The principal \(P = 500\), the annual interest rate is \(17\%\) (or \(0.17\)), the number of days \(n = 15\), and the number of days in a year is \(365\). The daily interest rate is therefore \(\frac{0.17}{365}\).

steps.title: Calculate the balance before payment
steps.bodyLatex: We calculate the amount owing after 15 days using the formula \(A = 500 \times (1 + \frac{0.17}{365})^{15}\). Calculating this gives \(A \approx 503.50\).

steps.title: Subtract the payment
steps.bodyLatex: Finally, we subtract the \(250\) payment from the accrued balance: \(503.50 - 250 = 253.50\). The amount owing immediately after the payment is \(253.50\).

commonMistakesLatex: Using 360 or 364 days instead of 365 when calculating the daily interest rate.
Forgetting to compound the interest daily and instead using simple interest.
Subtracting the payment before calculating the interest for the 15-day period.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: A quick sanity check: \(500 \times 0.17 \div 365 \times 15\) is approximately \(3.49\) in interest. Adding this to the original \(500\) gives \(503.49\), and subtracting \(250\) yields \(253.49\), which confirms our result of \(253.50\).

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to work with ratios and apply them to real-world volume and capacity problems. It requires converting between cubic centimetres and litres, and using the unitary method to distribute parts of a whole.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For part (a), use the unitary method to find the value of one 'part' of the ratio. For part (b), calculate the total volume in cubic centimetres, convert to litres, and then multiply the total capacity by the fraction representing mango juice.

steps.title: (a) Calculate orange juice required
steps.bodyLatex: The ratio of pineapple to mango to orange is \(15:9:4\). Since \(15\) parts of pineapple equal \(3\) litres, one part is \(3 \div 15 = 0.2\) litres. As orange juice represents \(4\) parts, we calculate \(4 \times 0.2 = 0.8\) litres.

steps.title: (b) Calculate container capacity
steps.bodyLatex: The volume of the rectangular prism is \(V = \text{length} \times \text{width} \times \text{height} = 40 \text{ cm} \times 20 \text{ cm} \times 35 \text{ cm} = 28,000 \text{ cm}^3\). Since \(1,000 \text{ cm}^3 = 1 \text{ litre}\), the capacity is \(28,000 \div 1,000 = 28 \text{ litres}\).

steps.title: (b) Calculate mango juice volume
steps.bodyLatex: The total number of parts in the ratio is \(15 + 9 + 4 = 28\). Mango juice accounts for \(9\) out of these \(28\) parts. Therefore, the amount of mango juice is \(\frac{9}{28} \times 28 \text{ litres} = 9 \text{ litres}\).

commonMistakesLatex: Forgetting to convert cubic centimetres to litres (1000 cm³ = 1 L).
Incorrectly adding the ratio parts or using the wrong part for the calculation.
Failing to use the unitary method effectively, leading to errors in scaling the ratio.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Check the ratios: Pineapple = \(15 \times (28/28) = 15\) L, Mango = \(9 \times (28/28) = 9\) L, Orange = \(4 \times (28/28) = 4\) L. Total = \(15+9+4 = 28\) L, which matches the container capacity.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to model linear relationships with graphs and solve simultaneous equations to find specific time values.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: For (a), plot the linear function for Tank B starting at its delayed start time. For (b) and (c), identify the intersection point or solve the equations algebraically to find the required time values.

steps.title: (a) Graphing Tank B
steps.bodyLatex: Tank B starts at \( t = 15 \) with \( V = 0 \). Since it fills at \( 30 \text{ L/min} \), the equation is \( V = 30(t - 15) \). Plot the point \( (15, 0) \) and another point, such as \( (30, 450) \), then draw a straight line through them.

steps.title: (b) Finding when volumes are equal
steps.bodyLatex: The volumes are equal when the lines for Tank A (\( V = 1000 - 20t \)) and Tank B (\( V = 30t - 450 \)) intersect. Setting them equal: \( 1000 - 20t = 30t - 450 \). Solving for \( t \): \( 1450 = 50t \), which gives \( t = 29 \text{ minutes} \).

steps.title: (c) Finding when total volume is 1000 litres
steps.bodyLatex: We need the sum of the volumes to be \( 1000 \). At \( t = 45 \), Tank A is \( 1000 - 20(45) = 100 \text{ L} \) and Tank B is \( 30(45 - 15) = 900 \text{ L} \). Since \( 100 + 900 = 1000 \), the total volume is \( 1000 \text{ L} \) at \( t = 45 \text{ minutes} \).

commonMistakesLatex: Starting the graph for Tank B at the origin \( (0,0) \) instead of the correct start time \( (15,0) \).
Forgetting to account for the time delay in the equation for Tank B.
Misinterpreting the total volume question in (c) as an intersection point question.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify (b): At \( t = 29 \), Tank A is \( 1000 - 20(29) = 420 \text{ L} \) and Tank B is \( 30(29 - 15) = 30(14) = 420 \text{ L} \). The volumes match.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to calculate the total surface area of a composite 3D solid by identifying and summing the areas of its exposed outer faces.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: To find the total surface area, identify all external faces: the base, four sides of the cube, two triangular ends of the prism, and two rectangular slanted roof panels. Remember to exclude the internal face where the prism meets the cube.

steps.title: 1. Find the height of the triangular prism
steps.bodyLatex: The total height of the solid is \(11 \text{ cm}\) and the cube height is \(8 \text{ cm}\). The height of the triangular prism is the difference: \(h_{\text{triangle}} = 11 \text{ cm} - 8 \text{ cm} = 3 \text{ cm}\).

steps.title: 2. Calculate the area of the cube faces
steps.bodyLatex: The cube has a base and four side faces exposed. Since the top face is covered by the prism, we count \(5\) square faces of size \(8 \text{ cm} \times 8 \text{ cm}\). Total area = \(5 \times (8 \times 8) = 5 \times 64 = 320 \text{ cm}^2\).

steps.title: 3. Calculate the area of the triangular prism faces
steps.bodyLatex: There are two triangular ends with base \(8 \text{ cm}\) and height \(3 \text{ cm}\). Area = \(2 \times (\frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 3) = 24 \text{ cm}^2\). There are also two rectangular roof panels with dimensions \(5 \text{ cm} \times 8 \text{ cm}\). Area = \(2 \times (5 \times 8) = 80 \text{ cm}^2\).

steps.title: 4. Calculate the total surface area
steps.bodyLatex: Sum the areas of all exposed faces: \(320 \text{ cm}^2 + 24 \text{ cm}^2 + 80 \text{ cm}^2 = 424 \text{ cm}^2\).

commonMistakesLatex: Including the top face of the cube or the base of the prism in the calculation, even though they are hidden inside the solid.
Confusing the slant height of the prism (\(5 \text{ cm}\)) with the vertical height of the triangle.
Calculating the volume of the solid instead of the surface area.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify by ensuring all 9 external faces are accounted for: 4 cube sides + 1 cube bottom + 2 triangular ends + 2 rectangular roof slopes = 9 faces total.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to perform forward and backward scanning on a project network diagram to determine the minimum completion time, identify the critical path, and calculate float time.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: Use forward scanning to find the earliest start time (EST) for each node and backward scanning to find the latest finish time (LFT). The minimum completion time is the EST at the final node, the critical path consists of activities with zero float, and float is calculated as \( \text{Float} = \text{Latest Start} - \text{Earliest Start} \).

steps.title: (a) Minimum completion time
steps.bodyLatex: Perform a forward scan to find the longest path from Start to Finish. The path \( C \to D \to E \to F \to H \to I \) gives \( 8 + 12 + 6 + 10 + 8 + 2 = 46 \) minutes. Since this is the longest path, the minimum time to complete the meal is \( 46 \) minutes.

steps.title: (b) Identify the critical path
steps.bodyLatex: The critical path is the sequence of activities where there is no slack (float). By checking all paths, we confirm that the path \( C - D - E - F - H - I \) results in the total duration of \( 46 \) minutes, matching the minimum time.

steps.title: (c) Calculate float for A and G
steps.bodyLatex: For activity \( A \), the \( \text{EST} = 0 \). The latest start time is \( 12 \), so \( \text{Float} = 12 - 0 = 12 \) minutes. For activity \( G \), the \( \text{EST} = 20 \) (from \( C+D+E \)) and the latest start is \( 35 \), giving \( \text{Float} = 35 - 20 = 15 \) minutes.

workedSolutions.finalAnswerLatex: (a) 46 minutes; (b) C - D - E - F - H - I; (c) A: EST 0, Float 12; G: EST 20, Float 15
commonMistakesLatex: Confusing the longest path with the shortest path when calculating the minimum completion time.
Forgetting that float is calculated using the latest start time minus the earliest start time, not just subtracting activity duration.
Errors in forward or backward scanning due to not labelling the nodes clearly on the diagram.
workedSolutions.checkLatex: Verify the critical path by ensuring the sum of activity times equals the total duration (46) and that all other paths are shorter than or equal to this value.

workedSolutions.summaryLatex: This question tests your ability to apply the Trapezoidal rule to estimate the area of an irregular shape using a scale diagram. It also requires you to interpret the geometric relationship between the rule's approximation and the actual area.
workedSolutions.approachLatex: First, convert the diagram measurements into real-world units using the scale of \(1 \text{ cm} = 5 \text{ m}\). Then, divide the shape into two sections and apply the Trapezoidal rule formula: \(A \approx \frac{h}{2}(d_1 + d_2)\) for each section.

steps.title: (a) Convert measurements and set up the rule
steps.bodyLatex: The scale is \(1 \text{ cm} = 5 \text{ m}\). Looking at the grid, each grid square represents \(1 \text{ cm}\), so each square side is \(5 \text{ m}\). The total width of the first section is \(4 \text{ cm} = 20 \text{ m}\), and the second is \(4 \text{ cm} = 20 \text{ m}\). The heights at the boundaries are \(5 \text{ cm} (25 \text{ m})\), \(4 \text{ cm} (20 \text{ m})\), and \(4 \text{ cm} (20 \text{ m})\).

steps.title: (a) Calculate the area
steps.bodyLatex: Applying the Trapezoidal rule for two sections: \[ Area \approx \frac{20}{2}(25 + 20) + \frac{20}{2}(20 + 20) \] \[ Area \approx 10(45) + 10(40) = 450 + 400 = 850 \text{ m}^2 \]

steps.title: (b) Compare with actual area
